```mermaid
    style nodeO4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeO5 fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Space Complexity. Differentiate between Space Complexity and Auxiliary Space.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Space Complexity** is the total amount of memory an algorithm requires to run, expressed as a function $S(n)$ of the input size $n$. It includes both the memory for storing the input and the extra (auxiliary) memory for computations.

**Differentiation (2 marks):**
- **Space Complexity** = Input Space + Auxiliary Space (Total memory footprint)
- **Auxiliary Space** = Extra temporary space used by the algorithm *excluding* the space taken by inputs

*Example:* In merge sort, total space is $O(n)$ for the input plus $O(n)$ for the auxiliary array, but auxiliary space is reported as $O(n)$ alone.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meant by the "fixed part" and "variable part" of space complexity? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fixed Part:** Memory that does not depend on the instance characteristics of the problem. *Example:* Space for storing compiled code, simple variables like loop counters, constants.
- **Variable Part:** Memory that depends on the input size $n$. *Example:* Space for a dynamically allocated array of size $n$, or the recursion call stack for a recursive function.

Mathematically: $S(n) = c + S_p(n)$, where $c$ is fixed and $S_p(n)$ is variable.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** For the following algorithms, determine the **space complexity** in Big-O notation. Justify your answer with a clear memory accounting table. **(7 marks)**

**Algorithm 1:**
```c
int findMax(int arr[], int n) {
    int max = arr[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
        if (arr[i] > max) max = arr[i];
    }
    return max;
}
```

**Algorithm 2:**
```c
int recursiveMax(int arr[], int n) {
    if (n == 1) return arr[0];
    return (arr[n-1] > recursiveMax(arr, n-1)) ? arr[n-1] : recursiveMax(arr, n-1);
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm 1 — Memory Accounting Table:**

| Variable | Type | Size | Fixed/Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| `arr[]` | Input array | $n$ | Variable |
| `n` | int | 1 word | Fixed |
| `max` | int | 1 word | Fixed |
| `i` | int | 1 word | Fixed |
| Return value | int | 1 word | Fixed |

$$
S(n) = n + 4 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S(n) = O(n) \text{ (total)}, \quad S_{\text{aux}}(n) = O(1)
$$

**Algorithm 2 — Memory Accounting Table:**

| Component | Size | Fixed/Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Input `arr[]` | $n$ | Variable |
| Stack frames (depth $=n$) | $n$ | Variable (auxiliary) |
| Local `n` per frame | 1 per frame | — |

$$
S_{\text{aux}}(n) = n \cdot k \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; S_{\text{aux}}(n) = O(n)
$$

$$
S_{\text{total}}(n) = n + n \cdot k = O(n)
$$

**Conclusion:** Both have total space $O(n)$, but their **auxiliary** space differs — $O(1)$ iterative vs. $O(n)$ recursive.

---

**(b)** Explain the **time-space trade-off** with two real-world examples. Why is iterative binary search preferred in operating-system kernels? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A *time-space trade-off* occurs when an algorithm can be made faster by using more memory, or made to use less memory by running slower.

**Example 1 — Hash Table (3 marks):**
- A hash table uses $O(n)$ extra space to achieve $O(1)$ average-case lookup.
- Without it (linear search in an array), lookup is $O(n)$ time but $O(1)$ extra space.
- This is a classic **space-for-time** trade-off.

**Example 2 — Lookup Tables / DP Tables (2 marks):**
- Computing Fibonacci recursively: $O(2^n)$ time, $O(n)$ stack space.
- Computing Fibonacci with DP: $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ auxiliary space (or $O(n)$ table for memoization).
- This is a **time-for-space** trade-off (DP table).

**Why iterative binary search in OS kernels (2 marks):**
- OS kernels (Linux, Windows NT) run with severely limited memory and must avoid stack overflows.
- Recursive binary search uses $O(\log n)$ auxiliary stack space, while iterative uses $O(1)$.
- Iterative is also faster in practice (no function call overhead).
- The kernel's memory allocator itself uses iterative binary search in its free-list management.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** For the following recursive function, derive the **auxiliary space complexity** step by step. **(7 marks)**

```c
int mystery(int n) {
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    int x = mystery(n - 1);   // Call 1
    int y = mystery(n - 1);   // Call 2
    return x + y + n;
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the recursion tree:**
This is a binary recursion. For input $n$, the function calls itself twice with $(n-1)$, each of which calls twice with $(n-2)$, and so on, until $n=1$.

**Step 2 — Count the maximum recursion depth:**
Although the tree has $2^n$ nodes, the *depth* of the call stack at any one moment is $n$ (each recursive call waits for two sub-calls to return before it can return).

**Step 3 — Memory per active stack frame:**
Each frame stores: return address, parameter `n`, local variables `x`, `y`. This is a constant $k$ bytes.

**Step 4 — Auxiliary space equation:**

$$
S_{\text{aux}}(n) = k \cdot n
$$

**Step 5 — Asymptotic order:**

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{aux}}(n) = \Theta(n) \text{ or } O(n)}
$$

> Note: Even though the *time* complexity is $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + O(1) = O(2^n)$, the *space* complexity is only $O(n)$ because the call stack unwinds before new branches grow too deep.

---

**(b)** Compare the space complexity of **Merge Sort** and **Quick Sort**. Show that Merge Sort is **not** in-place, while Quick Sort (in the average case) is. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Merge Sort Space Analysis (3 marks):**
- The merge step requires an auxiliary array of size $n$ to hold the merged result before copying back.
- This auxiliary array is allocated once at the top-level merge and reused.
- Recursion depth is $\log_2 n$, but the *auxiliary array* dominates.
$$
S_{\text{aux}}(n) = O(n) \quad \text{(NOT in-place)}
$$

**Quick Sort Space Analysis (4 marks):**
- The partition step is in-place (uses $O(1)$ extra).
- However, the *recursion* uses call stack space.
- In the **average case** (balanced partitions), recursion depth is $O(\log n)$.
- In the **worst case** (already sorted array with bad pivot), recursion depth is $O(n)$.

$$
S_{\text{aux}}(n) = 
\begin{cases}
O(\log n) & \text{average case (in-place)} \\
O(n) & \text{worst case (degenerate)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Conclusion:** Quick Sort is in-place *in the average case* but not in the worst case. Merge Sort is *never* in-place because of the auxiliary merge array.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing time and space complexity:** Students often write $O(2^n)$ for the *space* of the binary-recursive function in Question B(a). The space is $O(n)$ — only the time is exponential.
> 2. **Counting input as auxiliary:** When asked for "auxiliary space", do **not** include the input array. KTU deducts up to 2 marks for this.
> 3. **Ignoring call-stack in recursive functions:** Many students write $O(1)$ for recursive algorithms. Always account for the recursion depth multiplied by the frame size.
> 4. **Mixing up "in-place" and "$O(1)$ space":** In-place means $O(1)$ *auxiliary* space excluding input. Be precise with terminology.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Space Complexity** $S(n) = c + S_p(n)$ → fixed part + variable part.
- ✅ **Auxiliary Space** excludes input; **Total Space** includes input.
- ✅ Always account for the **recursion call stack** in recursive algorithms — frame size × depth.
- ✅ Iterative algorithms generally use **$O(1)$ auxiliary space**; recursive ones use **$O(\text{depth})$**.
- ✅ Common orders (cheat): $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n)$.
- ✅ **Time-space trade-off:** faster algorithms often use more memory (hashing, DP tables).
- ✅ In-place algorithms use $O(1)$ auxiliary space (Bubble, Selection, Insertion, Quick Sort avg).
- ✅ Merge Sort is **NOT** in-place (uses $O(n)$ auxiliary array for merging).
- ✅ Naive recursive Fibonacci: $O(2^n)$ time, $O(n)$ space. DP Fibonacci: $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space.
- ✅ When asked for "auxiliary space", explicitly write: *"Excluding the input array of size $n$."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Complexity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time Complexity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Data Structures and Algorithms*, **Time Complexity** is formally defined as a **mathematical function $T(n)$** that quantifies the **number of primitive computational operations** an algorithm performs as a function of the **input size $n$**. It belongs to the domain of **computational complexity theory** and forms the bedrock of **asymptotic analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** *Time complexity of an algorithm is the amount of time required by the algorithm to complete its execution, expressed as a function of the length of the input, and analyzed using asymptotic notations to capture the growth rate for large inputs.*

A **primitive operation** is any low-level, constant-time instruction such as:
- Arithmetic operations ($+,-,\times,\div$)
- Logical comparisons ($<, >, ==, \neq$)
- Variable assignments
- Array indexing (read/write)
- Function return statements

The three pillars of time complexity analysis are:
1. **Best Case** — minimum operations (denoted by $\Omega$)
2. **Average Case** — expected operations over all inputs (denoted by $\Theta$, when deterministic)
3. **Worst Case** — maximum operations (denoted by $O$)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

**Analogy: The "Kerala-State Bus vs. Metro Rail" Analogy**

Imagine you need to travel across Kochi city to visit **$n$** different locations:

- **Walking Algorithm (Linear $O(n)$):** You visit each location one by one on foot. As $n$ doubles, your travel time doubles.
- **Smart Walking (Logarithmic $O(\log n)$):** You skip every second location and ask a local. Each step reduces your search space by half — like a "divide and check" approach.
- **Bicycle (Constant $O(1)$):** You have a magical teleporting bicycle. No matter how many locations, you reach instantly.
- **Old Auto-Rickshaw (Quadratic $O(n^2)$):** For every location, you circle the whole city once. The work explodes.
- **Chartered Flight (Factorial $O(n!)$):** For every location, you must visit *all possible permutations* of the remaining locations — completely infeasible for large $n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Time complexity is **not** the *actual execution time* in seconds. It is the **mathematical growth behavior** of operations as input size $n \to \infty$. Two algorithms with $O(n^2)$ complexity may take different real times, but both will degrade *quadratically* for large inputs.

## 1.3 Why Time Complexity Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

| Engineering Concern | Role of Time Complexity |
|---|---|
| Algorithm Selection | Choose optimal DSA for the problem scale |
| Scalability Prediction | Forecast performance for big data |
| Resource Planning | Estimate compute infrastructure needs |
| Interview/Placement | Core topic in TCS, Infosys, Amazon hiring |
| Software Engineering | Enables Big-O analysis in code reviews |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following **bold** constants are universally referenced in KTU 2024 scheme examinations:

- **Time Unit $T(n)$:** Count of primitive operations, dimensionless.
- **Input Size $n$:** The length of the input (number of array elements, characters in a string, nodes in a graph, etc.).
- **Asymptotic Constant $c$:** A positive real multiplier that absorbs hardware differences.
- **Threshold Size $n_0$:** Beyond which the asymptotic bound is guaranteed to hold.
- **Base of Logarithm:** Unless stated, $\log$ denotes $\log_2$ in DSA (base-2 logarithm).
- **Worst-Case Bound $O$:** Most commonly tested in KTU ESE questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Asymptotic Notations: Formal Theory

Asymptotic notations are mathematical tools used to describe the **limiting behavior** of $T(n)$ as $n \to \infty$. They are classified into **five standard notations** in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

### 2.1.1 Big-O Notation $O$ — Upper Bound (Worst Case)

$$T(n) = O(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ T(n) \leq c \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

**Operational Logic:**
- Find a function $f(n)$ that **grows at least as fast** as $T(n)$.
- Pick constants $c$ and $n_0$ that "cover" $T(n)$ from above.
- If the statement holds, $T(n)$ is "Big-O of $f(n)$".

**Why:** Provides a **guarantee** that the algorithm will *never* take more than $c \cdot f(n)$ time for sufficiently large $n$.

### 2.1.2 Big-Omega Notation $\Omega$ — Lower Bound (Best Case)

$$T(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ T(n) \geq c \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

**Operational Logic:**
- Find a function $f(n)$ that **grows at most as fast** as $T(n)$.
- $T(n)$ will *always* require at least $c \cdot f(n)$ operations.

### 2.1.3 Big-Theta Notation $\Theta$ — Tight Bound (Average Case)

$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c_1, c_2 > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ c_1 \cdot f(n) \leq T(n) \leq c_2 \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

**Operational Logic:**
- Both $O$ and $\Omega$ must hold *simultaneously* with the same $f(n)$.
- Implies $T(n)$ grows at **exactly the same rate** as $f(n)$.

### 2.1.4 Little-o Notation $o$ — Strict Upper Bound

$$T(n) = o(f(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} = 0$$

**Operational Logic:** $T(n)$ grows **strictly slower** than $f(n)$. The ratio vanishes.

### 2.1.5 Little-omega Notation $\omega$ — Strict Lower Bound

$$T(n) = \omega(f(n)) \iff \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{T(n)}{f(n)} = \infty$$

**Operational Logic:** $T(n)$ grows **strictly faster** than $f(n)$.

---

## 2.2 Hierarchy of Complexity Classes (Sorted by Growth)

From **fastest (best)** to **slowest (worst)**:

$$O(1) < O(\log n) < O(\sqrt{n}) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!) < O(n^n)$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it covers 80% of KTU Part-A & Part-B questions on Time Complexity.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Simple sequential statements | $O(1)$ | Variable assignments, basic I/O |
| Single loop of $n$ iterations | $O(n)$ | Linear scan of array |
| Nested loop (2 levels) | $O(n^2)$ | Pairwise comparisons (Bubble Sort) |
| Loop with variable multiplied/divided by constant | $O(\log n)$ | Binary search loop |
| Loop with $i = i/2$ running $\log n$ times | $O(\log n)$ | Bit manipulation |
| Two consecutive loops of size $n$ | $O(n) + O(n) = O(n)$ | Dominant term rule |
| If-else (no recursion) | $O(\max(T_{\text{if}}, T_{\text{else}}))$ | Conditional branching |
| Recursive call halving input | $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(\log n)$ | Binary search |
| Recursive call halving + linear combine | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) \Rightarrow O(n \log n)$ | Merge sort |
| Two recursive calls + constant work | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(2^n)$ | Tower of Hanoi |
| Sum of first $n$ integers | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$ | Nested loop analysis |
| Sum of geometric series | $\sum_{i=0}^{k} 2^i = 2^{k+1} - 1$ | Tree algorithm analysis |
| Logarithm identity | $\log_a b = \dfrac{\log_c b}{\log_c a}$ | Change of base |
| L'Hopital's rule for limits | $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{\log n}{n} = 0$ | Verifying $o$ relations |
| Common product identity | $n \cdot \log n = O(n^{1+\epsilon}),\ \epsilon > 0$ | Polynomial vs quasi-poly |
| Master Theorem (preview for Mod 2) | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ | Divide & conquer recurrences |

---

## 2.4 Rules for Computing Time Complexity (Algorithm Analysis)

When KTU board questions ask *"Find the time complexity of the following algorithm"*, apply these rules **in order**:

1. **Identify Basic Operations:** Find the *innermost* statement (usually the comparison or assignment inside the deepest loop).
2. **Determine Frequency:** Express the number of times that operation executes as a function of $n$.
3. **Sum Across Statements:** For each block, sum the frequencies.
4. **For Nested Loops:** Multiply the iteration counts.
5. **For Consecutive Statements:** Add their complexities.
6. **Drop Constants and Lower-Order Terms:** $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \to O(n^2)$.
7. **For Recursion:** Express as a recurrence relation and solve via *iteration*, *recursion tree*, or *Master Theorem* (Module 2).

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Time Complexity Used For |
|---|---|
| **Database Indexing (B-Trees)** | $O(\log n)$ lookups vs $O(n)$ linear scans |
| **Network Routing (Dijkstra's)** | $O((V+E)\log V)$ shortest path |
| **Machine Learning (k-NN)** | $O(nd)$ brute force vs $O(\log n)$ with KD-trees |
| **Cryptography (RSA)** | $O(\log^3 n)$ modular exponentiation |
| **Compiler Design** | $O(n)$ lexical analysis |
| **Operating Systems** | $O(\log n)$ priority queues, $O(1)$ hashing |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Step Mandate:** Every algebraic step is explicitly written. No shortcuts, no "and so on" placeholders.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sum of First $n$ Natural Numbers (Nested Loop Foundation)

This derivation is the **single most tested identity** in KTU time-complexity questions, used in bubble sort, selection sort, and insertion sort analyses.

We want to find a closed-form expression for:

$$S_n = 1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + (n-1) + n$$

### Step 1: Write the sum in reverse order

$$S_n = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + \dots + 2 + 1$$

### Step 2: Add the two forms column-wise

$$\begin{aligned}
2 \cdot S_n &= (1 + n) + (2 + (n-1)) + (3 + (n-2)) + \dots + (n + 1) \\
&= \underbrace{(n+1) + (n+1) + (n+1) + \dots + (n+1)}_{n \ \text{times}} \\
&= n \cdot (n+1)
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic:** Each column pair sums to $(n+1)$ regardless of position. There are exactly $n$ such pairs.

### Step 3: Solve for $S_n$

$$S_n = \frac{n \cdot (n+1)}{2}$$

### Step 4: Apply asymptotic simplification

$$S_n = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} = \frac{1}{2} n^2 + \frac{1}{2} n$$

Dropping the constant $\frac{1}{2}$ and lower-order term $\frac{1}{2} n$:

$$S_n = \Theta(n^2)$$

**KTU Implication:** A double loop where the inner loop runs $i$ times (for $i = 1$ to $n$) takes $O(n^2)$ time. Examples: **Bubble Sort, Selection Sort, Insertion Sort**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solving $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ via the **Iteration Method** (Binary Search)

This is the recurrence governing **binary search** — the most frequently tested recursive algorithm in KTU ESE.

### Recurrence Statement

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

### Step 1: Apply the recurrence once

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

### Step 2: Substitute $T(n/2)$ using the recurrence

$$T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) = T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1$$

Therefore:

$$T(n) = \left[T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1\right] + 1 = T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2$$

### Step 3: Substitute $T(n/4)$ using the recurrence

$$T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) = T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 1$$

Therefore:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3$$

### Step 4: Generalize to the $k$-th substitution

After $k$ substitutions, the pattern is:

$$T(n) = T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k$$

> **Logic:** Each substitution reduces the problem size by a factor of $2$ and adds $1$ to the constant cost. After $k$ steps, the constant is $k$.

### Step 5: Apply the base case condition

The base case $T(1) = 1$ is reached when:

$$\frac{n}{2^k} = 1 \implies n = 2^k \implies k = \log_2 n$$

### Step 6: Substitute $k = \log_2 n$ into the general form

$$T(n) = T(1) + \log_2 n = 1 + \log_2 n$$

### Step 7: Asymptotic conclusion

$$T(n) = 1 + \log_2 n = O(\log n)$$

**KTU Implication:** Binary search is **logarithmic** — searching 1 billion elements takes only ~30 comparisons.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ via the **Iteration Method** (Merge Sort)

This is the canonical recurrence for **merge sort**, a high-weight KTU question.

### Recurrence Statement

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

### Step 1: Apply the recurrence once

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$

### Step 2: Expand $T(n/2)$ in both recursive calls

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n$$

Distribute the factor of $2$:

$$T(n) = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2 \cdot \frac{n}{2} + n = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n + n = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2n$$

> **Logic:** The cost at level $1$ is $n$. After expansion, the leaf cost becomes $4T(n/4)$ and the combine cost is $2n$.

### Step 3: Expand $T(n/4)$ further

$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2n$$

$$T(n) = 8T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 4 \cdot \frac{n}{4} + 2n = 8T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + n + 2n = 8T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3n$$

### Step 4: Identify the pattern after $k$ levels

After $k$ expansions:

$$T(n) = 2^k \cdot T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot n$$

> **Logic:** The number of subproblems doubles each level (factor $2^k$). The combine cost at each level is $n$, and there are $k$ levels.

### Step 5: Apply the base case condition

The recursion stops when:

$$\frac{n}{2^k} = 1 \implies k = \log_2 n$$

### Step 6: Substitute $k = \log_2 n$

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \cdot \log_2 n$$

Using the identity $2^{\log_2 n} = n$:

$$T(n) = n \cdot 1 + n \cdot \log_2 n = n + n \log_2 n$$

### Step 7: Asymptotic conclusion

$$T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = O(n \log n)$$

**KTU Implication:** Merge sort runs in $O(n \log n)$ — strictly faster than $O(n^2)$ quadratic sorts for large $n$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Solving $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ (Sum of Series)

This recurrence models algorithms like **insertion sort** (best/average case) and linear-time prefix computations.

### Recurrence Statement

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + n, \quad T(0) = 0$$

### Step 1: Apply the recurrence once

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + n$$

### Step 2: Expand $T(n-1)$

$$T(n-1) = T(n-2) + (n-1)$$

Therefore:

$$T(n) = T(n-2) + (n-1) + n$$

### Step 3: Expand again to see the pattern

$$T(n) = T(n-3) + (n-2) + (n-1) + n$$

### Step 4: Generalize after $k$ expansions

$$T(n) = T(n-k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (n-i)$$

### Step 5: Apply the base case $T(0) = 0$ when $k = n$

$$T(n) = T(0) + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (n-i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} j = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

### Step 6: Asymptotic conclusion

$$T(n) = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Time Complexity by Algorithm Type

| Algorithm Type | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Binary Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | $O(1)$ iterative / $O(\log n)$ recursive |
| Bubble Sort | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insertion Sort | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Selection Sort | $\Omega(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Merge Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Quick Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Heap Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Tower of Hanoi | — | $\Theta(2^n)$ | $O(2^n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Fibonacci (naive) | — | — | $O(2^n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Fibonacci (DP) | — | — | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams use **Mermaid** syntax. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain uppercase text to prevent rendering errors.

## 4.1 Flowchart: The Time Complexity Analysis Procedure

This diagram shows the **exact procedural flow** that KTU examiners expect when solving "find the time complexity" questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START READ ALGORITHM]:::startNode --> B[IDENTIFY INPUT SIZE N]
    B --> C[PINPOINT BASIC PRIMITIVE OPERATIONS]
    C --> D{ALGORITHM TYPE}
    D -->|ITERATIVE| E[COUNT LOOP ITERATIONS]
    D -->|RECURSIVE| F[FORM RECURRENCE T N]
    D -->|HYBRID| G[DECOMPOSE INTO BOTH]
    E --> H[FORM SUMMATION EXPRESSION]
    F --> I[APPLY ITERATION TREE OR MASTER THEOREM]
    G --> H
    G --> I
    H --> J[SIMPLIFY USING STANDARD IDENTITIES]
    I --> J
    J --> K[DROP CONSTANTS AND LOWER ORDER TERMS]
    K --> L[FINAL ASYMPTOTIC CLASS O BIG O OMEGA OR THETA]
    L --> M[END]:::endNode

    classDef startNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Begin at the top. Determine whether the algorithm is iterative or recursive. For iterative, count loop iterations and form a sum. For recursive, form a recurrence. Finally, simplify to a Big-O class.

---

## 4.2 Hierarchy of Complexity Classes (Ordered by Growth Rate)

This diagram shows the **dominance relationship** between complexity classes — every arrow means "is asymptotically smaller than".

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[O 1 CONSTANT] --> B[O LOG N LOGARITHMIC]
    B --> C[O SQRT N SQUARE ROOT]
    C --> D[O N LINEAR]
    D --> E[O N LOG N LINEARITHMIC]
    E --> F[O N SQUARED QUADRATIC]
    F --> G[O N CUBIC CUBIC]
    G --> H[O 2 POWER N EXPONENTIAL]
    H --> I[O N FACTORIAL FACTORIAL]
```

**Interpretation:** Any algorithm that runs in $O(n)$ time will eventually be faster than any $O(n^2)$ algorithm, no matter the constant factors. This is the **central theorem** of asymptotic analysis.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Asymptotic Notation Family

```mermaid
graph TD
    FAM[ASYMPTOTIC NOTATIONS]:::family --> BIG[UPPER LOWER TIGHT BOUNDS]:::branch
    FAM --> LITTLES[STRICT BOUNDS]:::branch

    BIG --> BIGO[BIG O UPPER BOUND WORST CASE]:::leaf
    BIG --> BIGOM[BIG OMEGA LOWER BOUND BEST CASE]:::leaf
    BIG --> BIGTH[BIG THETA TIGHT BOUND AVERAGE CASE]:::leaf

    LITTLES --> LITTLESM[STRICT UPPER BOUND]:::leaf
    LITTLES --> LITTLEOM[STRICT LOWER BOUND]:::leaf
    LITTLESM --> LITTLESML[LITTLE O T N OVER F N TENDS TO ZERO]:::leaf
    LITTLEOM --> LITTLEOML[LITTLE OMEGA T N OVER F N TENDS TO INFINITY]:::leaf

    classDef family fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    classDef branch fill:#FFA500,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** The asymptotic notation family is divided into **non-strict bounds** ($O, \Omega, \Theta$) used for **standard analysis**, and **strict bounds** ($o, \omega$) used for **fine-grained comparisons**.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: The Big-O Bound Construction

This diagram illustrates the **geometric meaning** of the definition $T(n) = O(f(n))$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUNCTION T N ALGORITHM] -->|UPPER BOUNDED BY| B[C TIMES F N]
    A -->|LOWER BOUNDED BY| D[C1 TIMES F N]
    B --> E[THEREFORE T N IS THETA F N]:::conclusion
    D --> E

    classDef conclusion fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:** $T(n)$ is sandwiched between $c_1 \cdot f(n)$ (below) and $c_2 \cdot f(n)$ (above) for all $n \geq n_0$. This sandwiching is the **geometric essence** of Big-Theta notation.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns with the standard **80-mark paper** structure (Part A: 3 marks × 10 questions; Part B: 14 marks × 6 questions, choice permitted).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define time complexity of an algorithm. List the three asymptotic notations used to express it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Time complexity of an algorithm is a **function $T(n)$** that represents the **number of primitive operations** performed by the algorithm as a function of the input size $n$. It quantifies how the runtime grows with increasing input size, independent of machine hardware or programming language.

The three asymptotic notations used are:

1. **Big-O $O$** — Upper bound (worst-case guarantee)
2. **Big-Omega $\Omega$** — Lower bound (best-case guarantee)
3. **Big-Theta $\Theta$** — Tight bound (exact growth rate)

**[Marks: 1 + 2 = 3]**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the best, average, and worst case time complexity with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider **linear search** in an array of size $n$ searching for a key $K$:

- **Best Case $\Omega(1)$:** $K$ is found at the **first position**. Only one comparison is needed. The algorithm takes the *minimum* possible time.
- **Average Case $\Theta(n)$:** $K$ is equally likely to be at any of the $n$ positions. Expected number of comparisons = $\frac{1+2+3+\dots+n}{n} = \frac{n+1}{2} = \Theta(n)$.
- **Worst Case $O(n)$:** $K$ is at the **last position** or is **not present** at all. The algorithm must scan all $n$ elements, performing $n$ comparisons.

**Key Insight:** Best case is rarely useful in practice; **worst case is the standard for KTU board analysis**.

**[Marks: 1 + 2 = 3]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Explain the Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta asymptotic notations in detail. Provide the mathematical definition and a graphical representation for each.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Big-O Notation $O$ — Upper Bound (Worst Case)**

$$T(n) = O(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ T(n) \leq c \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

The function $T(n)$ grows **no faster** than $f(n)$ up to a constant factor. Graphically, $c \cdot f(n)$ lies *above* $T(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**2. Big-Omega Notation $\Omega$ — Lower Bound (Best Case)**

$$T(n) = \Omega(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ T(n) \geq c \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

The function $T(n)$ grows **at least as fast** as $f(n)$. Graphically, $c \cdot f(n)$ lies *below* $T(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**3. Big-Theta Notation $\Theta$ — Tight Bound (Average / Exact)**

$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n)) \iff \exists \ c_1, c_2 > 0,\ n_0 > 0 \ \text{such that} \ c_1 \cdot f(n) \leq T(n) \leq c_2 \cdot f(n) \ \forall\ n \geq n_0$$

$T(n)$ grows at **exactly the same rate** as $f(n)$, sandwiched between $c_1 \cdot f(n)$ and $c_2 \cdot f(n)$. This implies $T(n) = O(f(n))$ AND $T(n) = \Omega(f(n))$.

**Graphical Representation (Combined):**

```
T(n)
  |                          c2·f(n)        <-- UPPER BOUND
  |                       ___________
  |                     /             \
  |   c1·f(n)         /  T(n)          \    <-- T(n) lies between
  |  _________      /                   \
  | /         \   /                      \
  |/            \_/                        \
  |__________________________________________ n
  0       n0
        ^ start of asymptotic region
```

**Example:** If $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100$, then $T(n) = O(n^2)$, $T(n) = \Omega(n^2)$, and $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$.

**[Marks: 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7]**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration (substitution) method. State the time complexity class.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Write the recurrence.

$$T(n) = 2T\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$

**Step 2:** First expansion. Substitute $T(n/2)$ in both recursive calls.

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2 \cdot \frac{n}{2} + n = 4T\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2n$$

**Step 3:** Second expansion. Substitute $T(n/4)$.

$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2n = 8T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 4 \cdot \frac{n}{4} + 2n = 8T\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3n$$

**Step 4:** Generalize after $k$ iterations.

$$T(n) = 2^k T\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot n$$

> **Logic:** The number of subproblems is $2^k$ (each level doubles). The combine cost per level is $n$, accumulated over $k$ levels.

**Step 5:** Apply base case. Recursion terminates when $n/2^k = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 6:** Substitute $k = \log_2 n$ and use $2^{\log_2 n} = n$.

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \log_2 n = n \cdot 1 + n \log_2 n = n + n \log_2 n$$

**Step 7:** Asymptotic conclusion.

$$T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = O(n \log n)$$

**Algorithm Application:** This is the recurrence for **Merge Sort**, which guarantees $O(n \log n)$ time in all three cases.

**[Marks: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7]**

---

### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Differentiate between worst-case, best-case, and average-case time complexity. Determine the complexity of linear search and binary search in all three cases.**

**Model Answer:**

**Case Definitions:**

- **Worst Case:** Maximum number of operations over all inputs of size $n$. Represents the **upper limit** of runtime.
- **Best Case:** Minimum number of operations over all inputs of size $n$. Represents the **most favorable** runtime.
- **Average Case:** Expected number of operations assuming a probability distribution over inputs. Represents the **typical** runtime.

**Linear Search Analysis** (Search for $K$ in array of size $n$):

| Case | Condition | Comparisons | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best | $K$ at index $0$ | $1$ | $\Omega(1)$ |
| Average | $K$ at random index | $\frac{n+1}{2}$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Worst | $K$ at last position or absent | $n$ | $O(n)$ |

**Average case derivation for linear search:**

$$T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \frac{n+1}{2} = \Theta(n)$$

**Binary Search Analysis** (Requires sorted array of size $n$):

| Case | Condition | Comparisons | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best | $K$ is the middle element | $1$ | $\Omega(1)$ |
| Average | Random $K$ | $\log_2 n - 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| Worst | $K$ at leaf of decision tree | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ | $O(\log n)$ |

**Recurrence for binary search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$, solving to $O(\log n)$.

**Comparison Insight:** Binary search is asymptotically faster, but **only works on sorted data**. Sorting first takes $O(n \log n)$, so the total cost is $O(n \log n + \log n) = O(n \log n)$.

**[Marks: 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 2 = 7]**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Analyze the time complexity of the following code segments. Show step-by-step working.**

**Model Answer:**

**Code Segment 1 — Simple Loop:**

```c
int sum = 0;                         // 1 operation, O(1)
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {       // loop runs n times
    sum = sum + i;                   // 1 operation per iteration
}
```

**Analysis:**
- Initialization: $1$ operation
- Loop body: $1$ operation per iteration $\times n$ iterations = $n$ operations
- Total: $T(n) = 1 + n + 1 = n + 2$

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n)}$$

**Code Segment 2 — Nested Loop:**

```c
int count = 0;                       // O(1)
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {       // outer: n iterations
    for (int j = 1; j <= n; j++) {   // inner: n iterations per outer
        count++;                     // O(1) per inner iteration
    }
}
```

**Analysis:**
- Outer loop runs $n$ times.
- For each outer iteration, inner loop runs $n$ times.
- Total inner iterations: $n \times n = n^2$.

$$T(n) = 1 + n^2 + 1 = n^2 + 2 = O(n^2)$$

**Code Segment 3 — Logarithmic Loop:**

```c
int i = 1;
while (i <= n) {
    printf("KTU");
    i = i * 2;                       // i doubles each iteration
}
```

**Analysis:**
- Iteration values of $i$: $1, 2, 4, 8, \dots, 2^k$
- Loop terminates when $2^k > n$, i.e., $k = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$
- Number of iterations: $\log_2 n + 1$

$$T(n) = \log_2 n + 1 = O(\log n)$$

**Code Segment 4 — Triangle Pattern (Mixed Complexity):**

```c
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {       // outer: n
    for (int j = 1; j <= i; j++) {   // inner: i (depends on i)
        printf("*");
    }
}
```

**Analysis:**
- Total inner iterations: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$

$$T(n) = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} = O(n^2)$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Code Segment | Time Complexity |
|---|---|
| Simple loop | $O(n)$ |
| Nested loop (square) | $O(n^2)$ |
| Logarithmic loop | $O(\log n)$ |
| Triangle loop | $O(n^2)$ |

**[Marks: 1.75 × 4 = 7]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing $O$ with $\Theta$:** Writing $T(n) = O(n^2)$ when the bound is *tight* and $\Theta$ should be used. ($O$ is upper bound; $\Theta$ is tight bound.)
> 2. **Forgetting the base case in recurrence:** Always state $T(1) = $ some constant explicitly. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for omitting.
> 3. **Wrong iteration count in logarithmic loops:** Count carefully — loop `i = i*2` runs $\log_2 n + 1$ times, **not** $n/2$ times.
> 4. **Not simplifying after derivation:** Always drop constants and lower-order terms. $5n^2 + 3n + 100 = O(n^2)$, not $O(5n^2 + 3n)$.
> 5. **Mixing up best/worst case for linear vs binary search:** Linear search is $O(n)$ worst case; binary search is $O(\log n)$ worst case.
> 6. **Skipping algebraic steps in derivation:** Examiners award **partial marks per step**. A "final answer only" loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and read 5 minutes before your KTU exam!**

### ✅ Core Concepts

- **Time complexity $T(n)$** = number of primitive operations as a function of input size $n$.
- **Asymptotic analysis** studies behavior as $n \to \infty$.
- **Big-O $O$** = upper bound (worst case, most commonly tested).
- **Big-Omega $\Omega$** = lower bound (best case).
- **Big-Theta $\Theta$** = tight bound (average case, implies both $O$ and $\Omega$).
- **Little-o $o$** = strict upper bound; $\lim_{n\to\infty} T(n)/f(n) = 0$.
- **Little-omega $\omega$** = strict lower bound.

### ✅ Complexity Hierarchy (Must Memorize)

$$O(1) < O(\log n) < O(\sqrt{n}) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(n^3) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$$

### ✅ Must-Know Identities

- $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$
- $\sum_{i=0}^{k} 2^i = 2^{k+1} - 1$
- $2^{\log_2 n} = n$
- $\log_a b = \dfrac{\log_c b}{\log_c a}$

### ✅ Must-Know Recurrences

| Recurrence | Algorithm | Solution |
|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | Binary Search | $O(\log n)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | Merge Sort | $O(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | Linear Recursion | $O(n)$ |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | Insertion Sort | $O(n^2)$ |
| $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ | Tower of Hanoi | $O(2^n)$ |

### ✅ Rules of Thumb

- **Simple loop over $n$:** $O(n)$
- **Nested loop over $n \times n$:** $O(n^2)$
- **Loop with $i = i/2$ or $i = i*2$:** $O(\log n)$
- **Recursion halving the input + constant work:** $O(\log n)$
- **Recursion halving + linear combine:** $O(n \log n)$
- **Drop constants and lower-order terms** in final answer.

### ✅ Search & Sort Quick Reference

| Algorithm | Best | Average | Worst |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Binary Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Bubble Sort | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Merge Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| Quick Sort | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n^2)$ |

### ✅ Last-Minute Exam Tips

1. Always **state the base case** when solving recurrences.
2. Always **drop constants** in the final Big-O answer.
3. **Worst case is the default** unless explicitly asked for best or average.
4. For KTU 14-mark questions, **show every algebraic step** — partial marks are awarded.
5. Use **Mermaid / diagrams** in your answer sheet where possible to earn visualization marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Asymptotic Notations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Asymptotic Notations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Asymptotic Notations** are the mathematical tools used in the analysis of algorithms to describe the *limiting behavior* of a function as its argument (typically the input size $n$) tends towards a particular value, most commonly infinity. These notations provide a standardized, machine-independent framework for classifying algorithms based on their **Time Complexity** and **Space Complexity** as the input size grows arbitrarily large.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, asymptotic notations form the foundational vocabulary of algorithm analysis. They allow a computer scientist to make precise statements about an algorithm's **efficiency** without being tied to the specifics of any particular hardware, compiler, or programming language.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Asymptotic notations are used to express the *upper bound*, *lower bound*, and *tight bound* of an algorithm's running time. The five standard notations are: **Big-O ($O$)**, **Big-Omega ($\Omega$)**, **Big-Theta ($\Theta$)**, **Little-o ($o$)**, and **Little-omega ($\omega$)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Speed Limit on a Highway"

Imagine you are driving a car and want to describe the *maximum* possible speed of your vehicle over a long journey.

- The **Big-O ($O$)** notation is like saying: *"My car's speed will **never exceed** 120 km/hr no matter what."* — This is an **upper bound** (worst-case ceiling).
- The **Big-Omega ($\Omega$)** notation is like saying: *"My car will **always travel at least** 60 km/hr."* — This is a **lower bound** (best-case floor).
- The **Big-Theta ($\Theta$)** notation is like saying: *"My car consistently travels **at** around 90 km/hr — neither much faster nor much slower."* — This is a **tight bound** (both upper and lower).
- The **Little-o ($o$)** notation is a **strict** upper bound — like saying: *"My car is **strictly slower** than 120 km/hr, not equal to it."*
- The **Little-omega ($\omega$)** notation is a **strict** lower bound — like saying: *"My car is **strictly faster** than 60 km/hr, not equal to it."*

In algorithm analysis, the "speed" of the algorithm is replaced by the **number of operations** (or **memory units**), and the "long journey" is replaced by **input size $n \to \infty$**.

## 1.3 Why Asymptotic Notations Matter

- **Hardware Independence**: The analysis remains valid across all machines.
- **Scalability Prediction**: They predict how the algorithm will behave for large inputs ($n = 10^6, 10^9$).
- **Algorithm Comparison**: Two algorithms can be rigorously compared without writing code.

> [!NOTE]
> **The constant $c$ and threshold $n_0$:**
> In asymptotic analysis, we look for the existence of *positive constants* $c$ and $n_0$ such that the inequality holds for **all $n \geq n_0$**. We do not care about small input sizes — only the *asymptotic* (long-term) behavior.

## 1.4 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

As per the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcomes-Based Education framework, this topic maps to:

> **CO1**: *Analyze the asymptotic performance of algorithms using standard mathematical notations and compare the efficiency of algorithmic strategies.*

This topic directly trains the cognitive skill of **Analysis (Level 4 — Revised Bloom's Taxonomy)** and **Evaluation (Level 5)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Standard Asymptotic Notations — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Big-O Notation — $O(g(n))$: The Upper Bound

**Definition**: A function $f(n)$ is said to be in $O(g(n))$ if there exist **positive constants** $c$ and $n_0$ such that:

$$0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

- **Meaning**: $f(n)$ grows **no faster** than $g(n)$.
- **Use Case**: Describing the **worst-case** running time of an algorithm.
- **Example**: If $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100$, then $f(n) = O(n^2)$ because $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq 4n^2$ for all $n \geq 100$.

### 2.1.2 Big-Omega Notation — $\Omega(g(n))$: The Lower Bound

**Definition**: A function $f(n)$ is said to be in $\Omega(g(n))$ if there exist **positive constants** $c$ and $n_0$ such that:

$$0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

- **Meaning**: $f(n)$ grows **at least as fast** as $g(n)$.
- **Use Case**: Describing the **best-case** running time of an algorithm.
- **Example**: $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Omega(n^2)$ because $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \geq 3n^2$ for all $n \geq 1$.

### 2.1.3 Big-Theta Notation — $\Theta(g(n))$: The Tight Bound

**Definition**: A function $f(n)$ is said to be in $\Theta(g(n))$ if and only if there exist **positive constants** $c_1$, $c_2$, and $n_0$ such that:

$$0 \leq c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

- **Meaning**: $f(n)$ grows **at the same rate** as $g(n)$ (sandwiched between two scaled copies).
- **Use Case**: The most accurate classification — used when best, worst, and average cases share the **same growth rate**.
- **Example**: $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Theta(n^2)$ because it is both $O(n^2)$ and $\Omega(n^2)$.

### 2.1.4 Little-o Notation — $o(g(n))$: The Strict Upper Bound

**Definition**: A function $f(n)$ is said to be in $o(g(n))$ if for **every** positive constant $c > 0$, there exists a constant $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$0 \leq f(n) < c \cdot g(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

- **Meaning**: $f(n)$ grows **strictly slower** than $g(n)$.
- **Mathematical Form**: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$
- **Example**: $5n^2 = o(n^3)$ but $5n^2 \neq o(n^2)$ (because the limit is $5$, not $0$).

### 2.1.5 Little-omega Notation — $\omega(g(n))$: The Strict Lower Bound

**Definition**: A function $f(n)$ is said to be in $\omega(g(n))$ if for **every** positive constant $c > 0$, there exists a constant $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$0 \leq c \cdot g(n) < f(n) \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

- **Meaning**: $f(n)$ grows **strictly faster** than $g(n)$.
- **Mathematical Form**: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$
- **Example**: $5n^3 = \omega(n^2)$ but $5n^2 \neq \omega(n^2)$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Notation | Mathematical Form | Limit Form (if it exists) | Order Relation | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $O(g(n))$ | $f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ | $\limsup \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} < \infty$ | $f \leq g$ (asymptotically) | Worst-case upper bound |
| $\Omega(g(n))$ | $c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n)$ | $\liminf \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} > 0$ | $f \geq g$ (asymptotically) | Best-case lower bound |
| $\Theta(g(n))$ | $c_1 g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 g(n)$ | $0 < \lim \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} < \infty$ | $f = g$ (asymptotically) | Tight bound |
| $o(g(n))$ | $f(n) < c \cdot g(n)$ for all $c$ | $\lim \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$ | $f < g$ (strictly) | Strictly smaller growth |
| $\omega(g(n))$ | $c \cdot g(n) < f(n)$ for all $c$ | $\lim \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$ | $f > g$ (strictly) | Strictly larger growth |

## 2.3 Common Complexity Classes (Ordered from Best to Worst)

$$\mathcal{O}(1) < \mathcal{O}(\log \log n) < \mathcal{O}(\log n) < \mathcal{O}(\sqrt{n}) < \mathcal{O}(n) < \mathcal{O}(n \log n) < \mathcal{O}(n^2) < \mathcal{O}(n^3) < \mathcal{O}(2^n) < \mathcal{O}(n!) < \mathcal{O}(n^n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> In production systems, even a small reduction in asymptotic complexity (e.g., from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log n)$) can mean the difference between a system that completes in **seconds** versus one that takes **days** when $n = 10^9$.

## 2.4 The Relationship Between the Five Notations

The following logical implications always hold for any two functions $f$ and $g$:

$$f(n) = \Theta(g(n)) \iff f(n) = O(g(n)) \text{ AND } f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

$$f(n) = o(g(n)) \implies f(n) = O(g(n))$$

$$f(n) = \omega(g(n)) \implies f(n) = \Omega(g(n))$$

This means $\Theta$ is the **intersection** of $O$ and $\Omega$, and the "little" notations are **strict subsets** of the "big" notations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Proving $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2)$

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1**: State what we need to prove. We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq c \cdot n^2 \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

**Step 2**: Bound the lower-order terms using $n$. For $n \geq 1$, we have $5n \leq 5n^2$ and $100 \leq 100n^2$. Substituting:

$$3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq 3n^2 + 5n^2 + 100n^2 = (3 + 5 + 100)n^2 = 108n^2$$

**Step 3**: Choose the constants. We can take $c = 108$ and $n_0 = 1$.

**Step 4**: Verify the inequality for $n \geq 1$:

$$3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq 108n^2$$

This is clearly true because $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq 3n^2 + 5n^2 + 100n^2 = 108n^2$.

**Conclusion**: Therefore, $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Proving $f(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Omega(n^2)$

**Step 1**: We need constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that:

$$c \cdot n^2 \leq 3n^2 + 5n + 100 \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

**Step 2**: For $n \geq 1$, observe that $3n^2 + 5n + 100 \geq 3n^2$ trivially (since $5n \geq 0$ and $100 \geq 0$).

**Step 3**: Choose $c = 3$ and $n_0 = 1$. Then:

$$3n^2 \leq 3n^2 + 5n + 100 \quad \text{is true for all } n \geq 1$$

**Conclusion**: Therefore, $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Omega(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Combining Both to Get $\Theta(n^2)$

Since we have shown:
- $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2)$ with $c_2 = 108$, $n_0 = 1$
- $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Omega(n^2)$ with $c_1 = 3$, $n_0 = 1$

By the definition of $\Theta$, with $c_1 = 3$, $c_2 = 108$, and $n_0 = 1$:

$$3n^2 \leq 3n^2 + 5n + 100 \leq 108n^2 \quad \text{for all } n \geq 1$$

Therefore, $3n^2 + 5n + 100 = \Theta(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Using the Limit Method

To prove $5n^2 = o(n^3)$, we use the limit definition:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5n^2}{n^3} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{5}{n} = 0$$

Since the limit is exactly $0$, by the limit-form definition of little-o notation, $5n^2 = o(n^3)$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Function Classification

The following table shows the proper classification of common polynomial functions:

| Function $f(n)$ | Big-O Class | Big-Omega Class | Big-Theta Class | Reasoning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $5$ | $O(1)$ | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | Constant function |
| $3 \log n$ | $O(\log n)$ | $\Omega(\log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Logarithmic growth |
| $2n + 7$ | $O(n)$ | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Linear growth |
| $4n \log n + 2n$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Quasi-linear |
| $n^2 + 3n$ | $O(n^2)$ | $\Omega(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Quadratic |
| $5n^2$ vs $n^3$ | $O(n^3)$ | $\Omega(n^2)$ | NOT $\Theta$ | Different growth rates |
| $2^n + n^5$ | $O(2^n)$ | $\Omega(2^n)$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ | Exponential dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **General Rule for Polynomials:** For any polynomial $f(n) = a_k n^k + a_{k-1} n^{k-1} + \ldots + a_0$ with $a_k > 0$, we always have $f(n) = \Theta(n^k)$. The **highest-degree term dominates** the asymptotic behavior, and all lower-order terms become negligible.

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Limit Calculation Examples

**Example A**: Show $n^2 = o(n^3)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2}{n^3} &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \\
&= 0
\end{aligned}$$

Since the limit is $0$, $n^2 = o(n^3)$. The exponent $2 < 3$ confirms the strict inequality.

**Example B**: Show $n^3 \neq o(n^2)$ (it is actually $\omega(n^2)$).

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^3}{n^2} &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n \\
&= \infty
\end{aligned}$$

Since the limit is $\infty$, $n^3 = \omega(n^2)$, meaning $n^3$ grows strictly faster than $n^2$.

**Example C**: Compare $n \log n$ with $n^2$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2} &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log n}{n} \\
&= 0 \quad \text{(by L'Hopital's rule, since } \log n \text{ grows slower than } n\text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

So $n \log n = o(n^2)$, confirming that $n \log n$ grows strictly slower than $n^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: The Relationship Between the Five Notations

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **set-theoretic hierarchy** among the five asymptotic notations when applied to two functions $f(n)$ and $g(n)$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Theta Class TIGHT BOUND f = Theta g"] --> nodeB["Intersection of O and Omega"]
    nodeC["Big-O Class O UPPER BOUND f is at most c times g"] --> nodeB
    nodeD["Big-Omega Class Omega LOWER BOUND f is at least c times g"] --> nodeB
    nodeE["Little-o Class o STRICT UPPER BOUND f grows slower than g"] --> nodeC
    nodeF["Little-omega Class omega STRICT LOWER BOUND f grows faster than g"] --> nodeD
    nodeG["Proper Subsets Strictly smaller class"] --> nodeE
    nodeG --> nodeF
    nodeH["All Polynomial Functions of degree k"] --> nodeI["Theta n power k"]
    nodeJ["All Constant Functions"] --> nodeK["Theta 1"]
    nodeL["All Logarithmic Functions"] --> nodeM["Theta log n"]
    nodeN["All Linear Functions"] --> nodeO["Theta n"]
    nodeP["All Exponential Functions"] --> nodeQ["Theta 2 power n"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow Diagram: Steps for Asymptotic Analysis

This diagram maps out the **methodology** a KTU student should follow when classifying any function using asymptotic notation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["STEP 1: Identify the Function f of n"] --> stepB["STEP 2: Determine the Input Size n is Tending to Infinity"]
    stepB --> stepC["STEP 3: Choose the Comparison Function g of n"]
    stepC --> stepD{"STEP 4: Compute the Limit of f n over g n"}
    stepD -->|Limit equals 0| stepE["Result: f n equals little o of g n and also Big O of g n"]
    stepD -->|Limit is a finite positive constant| stepF["Result: f n equals Theta of g n Tight Bound"]
    stepD -->|Limit equals Infinity| stepG["Result: f n equals little omega of g n and also Big Omega of g n"]
    stepE --> stepH["STEP 5: Write Final Conclusion with Constants c and n0"]
    stepF --> stepH
    stepG --> stepH
```

## 4.3 Complexity Growth Comparison Chart (Conceptual Block Diagram)

This diagram provides a **block-level comparison** of how the major complexity classes grow relative to each other as $n$ increases.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blk1["Constant O 1 FLAT LINE"] --> blk2["Logarithmic O log n VERY SLOW RISE"]
    blk2 --> blk3["Linear O n DIAGONAL LINE"]
    blk3 --> blk4["Log Linear O n log n SLIGHTLY STEEPER"]
    blk4 --> blk5["Quadratic O n squared CURVE GOING UP"]
    blk5 --> blk6["Cubic O n cubed STEEPER CURVE"]
    blk6 --> blk7["Exponential O 2 power n NEAR VERTICAL EXPLOSION"]
    blk7 --> blk8["Factorial O n factorial ESSENTIALLY INFINITE FOR LARGE n"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:**
> Each block in the above diagram represents an order-of-magnitude jump in resource consumption. Notice how the gap between consecutive classes widens dramatically as we move from left to right — this visually demonstrates why **algorithm choice matters far more than hardware speed** for large inputs.

## 4.4 Best, Average, Worst Case Analysis Flow

The following diagram illustrates the three standard cases used in KTU algorithm analysis.

```mermaid
graph TD
    caseA["Algorithm Analysis Three Cases"] --> caseB["BEST CASE Omega notation Lower bound"]
    caseA --> caseC["AVERAGE CASE Expected running time"]
    caseA --> caseD["WORST CASE O notation Upper bound Most important for KTU"]
    caseB --> ex1["Example: Linear Search finds target at index 0 in one comparison"]
    caseC --> ex2["Example: Linear Search expected position is at index n over 2"]
    caseD --> ex3["Example: Linear Search target is at last index requires n comparisons"]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`

**Define Big-O notation. Prove that the function $f(n) = 5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 10$ is $O(n^3)$.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** $f(n) = O(g(n))$ if there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that $0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Proof (2 Marks):** We need to find $c$ and $n_0$ such that $5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 10 \leq c \cdot n^3$.

For $n \geq 1$: $2n^2 \leq 2n^3$, $7n \leq 7n^3$, and $10 \leq 10n^3$.

Therefore: $5n^3 + 2n^2 + 7n + 10 \leq 5n^3 + 2n^3 + 7n^3 + 10n^3 = 24n^3$.

Choose $c = 24$ and $n_0 = 1$. Hence $f(n) = O(n^3)$. $\blacksquare$

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`

**Distinguish between Big-O, Big-Omega, and Big-Theta notations with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Notation | Meaning | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Big-O ($O$)** | Asymptotic **upper bound** — function grows no faster | $3n^2 + 5n = O(n^2)$ |
| **Big-Omega ($\Omega$)** | Asymptotic **lower bound** — function grows no slower | $3n^2 + 5n = \Omega(n^2)$ |
| **Big-Theta ($\Theta$)** | Asymptotic **tight bound** — function grows at the same rate | $3n^2 + 5n = \Theta(n^2)$ |

Since the given function is **both** $O(n^2)$ and $\Omega(n^2)$, it is also $\Theta(n^2)$. **(1 Mark for distinction, 1 Mark for definitions, 1 Mark for the example with justification)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1]`

#### (a) Define the three asymptotic notations: $O$, $\Omega$, and $\Theta$. For each, give the formal mathematical definition and explain its significance in algorithm analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Big-O Notation (Upper Bound) (2 Marks):**
A function $f(n)$ is $O(g(n))$ if there exist constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \leq f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Significance**: Provides a guarantee that the running time of an algorithm will **never exceed** a certain rate of growth, regardless of input. Used predominantly for **worst-case analysis** in KTU examinations.

**Big-Omega Notation (Lower Bound) (2 Marks):**
A function $f(n)$ is $\Omega(g(n))$ if there exist constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \leq c \cdot g(n) \leq f(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Significance**: Provides a guarantee of **minimum resource consumption** — the algorithm cannot run faster than this bound asymptotically. Useful for proving **lower-bound impossibility results**.

**Big-Theta Notation (Tight Bound) (2 Marks):**
A function $f(n)$ is $\Theta(g(n))$ if there exist constants $c_1, c_2 > 0$ and $n_0 > 0$ such that $c_1 \cdot g(n) \leq f(n) \leq c_2 \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

**Significance**: Represents the **exact** asymptotic growth rate. If $f(n) = \Theta(g(n))$, the algorithm's performance scales **proportionally** to $g(n)$. This is the most informative and rigorous classification.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** The three notations together form the **complete vocabulary** of algorithmic efficiency. A KTU student must master all three to analyze algorithms accurately.

#### (b) Prove that $f(n) = 2n^2 + 3n + 5$ is $\Theta(n^2)$. Clearly state the values of $c_1$, $c_2$, and $n_0$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Prove $f(n) = O(n^2)$ (3 Marks):**

We need $2n^2 + 3n + 5 \leq c_2 \cdot n^2$.

For $n \geq 1$: $3n \leq 3n^2$ and $5 \leq 5n^2$.

So: $2n^2 + 3n + 5 \leq 2n^2 + 3n^2 + 5n^2 = 10n^2$.

Choose $c_2 = 10$ and $n_0 = 1$. This establishes the upper bound. ✓

**Step 2 — Prove $f(n) = \Omega(n^2)$ (3 Marks):**

We need $c_1 \cdot n^2 \leq 2n^2 + 3n + 5$.

For $n \geq 1$: $2n^2 + 3n + 5 \geq 2n^2$ (since $3n \geq 0$ and $5 \geq 0$).

Choose $c_1 = 2$ and $n_0 = 1$. This establishes the lower bound. ✓

**Step 3 — Combine for $\Theta$ (1 Mark):**

With $c_1 = 2$, $c_2 = 10$, and $n_0 = 1$:

$$2n^2 \leq 2n^2 + 3n + 5 \leq 10n^2 \quad \text{for all } n \geq 1$$

Therefore, by definition, $f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q1]`

#### (a) Define little-o and little-omega notations. Explain how they differ from Big-O and Big-Omega. Use mathematical examples to illustrate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Little-o Definition (2 Marks):**
$f(n) = o(g(n))$ if for **every** constant $c > 0$, there exists $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \leq f(n) < c \cdot g(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

Equivalently: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = 0$

**Little-omega Definition (2 Marks):**
$f(n) = \omega(g(n))$ if for **every** constant $c > 0$, there exists $n_0 > 0$ such that $0 \leq c \cdot g(n) < f(n)$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

Equivalently: $\lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \infty$

**Key Differences (2 Marks):**
- Big-O allows the inequality $f(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$ for **some** $c$ — Little-o requires the strict inequality for **all** $c$.
- This means $f(n) = O(g(n))$ allows $f(n)$ to be **equal to** $c \cdot g(n)$, but $f(n) = o(g(n))$ forbids equality in the limit.
- Formally: $o(g(n)) \subset O(g(n))$ and $\omega(g(n)) \subset \Omega(g(n))$ — the "little" notations are **strict proper subsets** of the "big" notations.

**Examples (1 Mark):**
- $5n^2 = o(n^3)$ is true, but $5n^2 = O(n^3)$ is also true.
- $5n^2 = o(n^2)$ is **false** (since the limit is $5 \neq 0$), but $5n^2 = O(n^2)$ is true.
- $5n^3 = \omega(n^2)$ is true, but $5n^3 = \Omega(n^2)$ is also true.

#### (b) Arrange the following functions in asymptotically increasing order: $n^2$, $2^n$, $n \log n$, $n^3$, $\log n$, $n$, $n!$. Justify your answer using the limit method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Establish the standard complexity ordering (3 Marks):**

Using the growth rate hierarchy:

$$\log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!$$

**Step 2 — Verify using limit comparisons (3 Marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\log n}{n} &= 0 \quad &\text{(log grows slower than linear)} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n}{n \log n} &= 0 \quad &\text{(linear is slower than n log n)} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \log n}{n^2} &= 0 \quad &\text{(n log n is slower than quadratic)} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^2}{n^3} &= 0 \quad &\text{(quadratic is slower than cubic)} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^3}{2^n} &= 0 \quad &\text{(polynomial is slower than exponential)} \\
\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^n}{n!} &= 0 \quad &\text{(exponential is slower than factorial)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Final Answer (1 Mark):**

The asymptotically **increasing order** is:

$$\log n \to n \to n \log n \to n^2 \to n^3 \to 2^n \to n!$$

This means as $n$ grows large, $\log n$ requires the least resources, and $n!$ requires the most. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $O$ and $\Theta$:** Students often write $f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ when they only proved $f(n) = O(n^2)$. Remember, $\Theta$ requires **both** upper and lower bounds. **(−2 marks typical penalty)**
> 2. **Forgetting the constant $c$ and $n_0$:** The KTU board requires you to **explicitly state** the values of $c$ (and $n_0$) in any proof. A proof without these is considered incomplete. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Writing "=" instead of "$\in$":** Strictly, $f(n)$ is a *member* of the set $O(g(n))$. Some strict examiners deduct a mark for writing $f(n) = O(g(n))$ instead of $f(n) \in O(g(n))$. Best practice: write "$f(n)$ is $O(g(n))$".
> 4. **Dropping the lower-order terms too early:** You must show the bounding step explicitly. Simply writing "$3n^2 + 5n + 100 = O(n^2)$ because the dominant term is $n^2$" is **insufficient** for full marks. The KTU board expects a rigorous inequality.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Asymptotic Notations**

- **The Big Three ($O$, $\Omega$, $\Theta$):** $O$ = upper bound (worst case), $\Omega$ = lower bound (best case), $\Theta$ = tight bound (both).
- **The Strict Two ($o$, $\omega$):** $o$ = strict upper, $\omega$ = strict lower. Both use "for every $c > 0$" instead of "there exists $c > 0$".
- **Constant requirements:** Every proof must produce a valid pair of positive constants ($c$ and $n_0$).
- **Theta = O AND Omega:** A function is $\Theta(g)$ if and only if it is both $O(g)$ and $\Omega(g)$.
- **Polynomial rule:** Any $k$-th degree polynomial with positive leading coefficient is $\Theta(n^k)$.
- **Limit shortcut:** If $\lim_{n \to \infty} f(n)/g(n) = $ finite non-zero constant $\Rightarrow \Theta$; $= 0 \Rightarrow o$ (and $O$); $= \infty \Rightarrow \omega$ (and $\Omega$).
- **Symmetry:** $f(n) = O(g(n))$ if and only if $g(n) = \Omega(f(n))$.
- **Transitivity:** If $f = O(g)$ and $g = O(h)$, then $f = O(h)$ (applies to all notations).
- **Logarithm base is irrelevant in asymptotics:** $\log_2 n$, $\log_{10} n$, $\ln n$ are all $\Theta(\log n)$ because they differ only by a constant factor.
- **Common trap:** Never claim $n^2 = O(n)$ — this is **FALSE**. The exponent cannot be reduced.
- **Growth hierarchy (memorize this order):** $1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < n^3 < 2^n < n!$.
- **KFU exam tip:** When asked to "prove" or "show" a complexity, always show the **inequality** explicitly with chosen constants — do not just state the result.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays - Linear Search and Binary Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Arrays - Linear Search and Binary Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Array — The Foundation
An **Array** is a linear, homogeneous, contiguous-memory data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type**, accessed in constant time using an integer index.

$$A = [a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n-1}]$$

where $n$ is the size of the array and $a_i$ denotes the element at index $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Arrays form the *primary* underlying structure for Module 1. All searching and traversal operations in DSA fundamentally operate on arrays. Mastery of indexing, address calculation, and bounds checking is **non-negotiable** for the ESE.

**Address Calculation Formula (Row-Major Order):**
$$\text{Address}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + i \times \text{SizeOf}(\text{Element})$$

### 1.2 Linear Search (Sequential Search)
**Formal Definition:** *Linear Search* is a brute-force search algorithm that scans an array element-by-element from the first index to the last (or until a match is found) to locate a target value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prerequisite:** Works on **both sorted and unsorted** arrays. No preprocessing required.

### 1.3 Binary Search (Logarithmic Search)
**Formal Definition:** *Binary Search* is a divide-and-conquer search algorithm that, on a **sorted array**, repeatedly divides the search interval in half by comparing the target with the middle element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Prerequisite:** The array **MUST be sorted** in ascending or descending order. Failure to sort yields **undefined behavior** (incorrect results).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

| Algorithm | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Linear Search** | Finding a specific contact on an **unlabeled, shuffled list** — you must scan each name one by one until you spot it. |
| **Binary Search** | Looking up a word in a **dictionary** — flip to the middle, decide which half contains your word, then repeat the process in that half. |

The dictionary analogy is the *classic* KTU board-exam illustration. The dramatic difference: a 1,000-page dictionary takes at most **10 flips** (vs. up to 1,000 page-by-page checks) because $\log_2(1000) \approx 10$.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- **Time Complexity Notation (Big-O):** $O(\cdot)$ denotes the asymptotic upper bound on the number of elementary operations.
- **Comparison Count $C$:** The number of times the target element is compared against an array element.
- **Standard Reference Size:** For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ elements, binary search needs at most **20 comparisons** since $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Search — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step operational flow:**

1. **Initialize** the search index $i \leftarrow 0$.
2. **Compare** the target key $K$ with $A[i]$.
3. If $A[i] = K$ → **FOUND**, return index $i$.
4. If $A[i] \neq K$ → **increment** $i \leftarrow i + 1$.
5. If $i \geq n$ → **NOT FOUND**, return $-1$.
6. **Repeat** steps 2–5.

**Why it works (intuition):** Since the array is traversed exhaustively, *every* element gets a chance to be compared. This guarantees correctness on unsorted data, but at the cost of efficiency.

### 2.2 Binary Search — Operational Logic

**Step-by-step operational flow:**

1. **Initialize** two pointers: $\text{low} \leftarrow 0$ and $\text{high} \leftarrow n - 1$.
2. **Compute** the middle index: $\text{mid} \leftarrow \lfloor (\text{low} + \text{high}) / 2 \rfloor$.
3. **Compare** $K$ with $A[\text{mid}]$:
   - If $A[\text{mid}] = K$ → **FOUND**, return $\text{mid}$.
   - If $A[\text{mid}] > K$ → discard the right half: $\text{high} \leftarrow \text{mid} - 1$.
   - If $A[\text{mid}] < K$ → discard the left half: $\text{low} \leftarrow \text{mid} + 1$.
4. If $\text{low} > \text{high}$ → **NOT FOUND**, return $-1$.
5. **Repeat** steps 2–4.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core 'Why':** After every comparison, binary search *mathematically eliminates* half of the remaining elements. This is what produces its $O(\log_2 n)$ behavior — the search space shrinks geometrically, not linearly.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
| **Prerequisite** | None (works on sorted/unsorted) | **Sorted array mandatory** |
| **Best Case Comparisons** | $1$ (element at index $0$) | $1$ (element at $\text{mid}$ initially) |
| **Worst Case Comparisons** | $n$ | $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ |
| **Average Case Comparisons** | $\dfrac{n+1}{2}$ | $\log_2 n$ |
| **Time Complexity — Best** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Time Complexity — Average** | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| **Time Complexity — Worst** | $O(n)$ | $O(\log_2 n)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(1)$ — Iterative | $O(1)$ — Iterative / $O(\log_2 n)$ — Recursive (call stack) |
| **Stability** | Not Applicable | Not Applicable |
| **Failure Indicator** | Returns $-1$ | Returns $-1$ |
| **Mid Calculation (overflow-safe)** | N/A | $\text{mid} = \text{low} + \dfrac{\text{high} - \text{low}}{2}$ |
| **Number of elements eliminated per step** | $1$ | $\approx \dfrac{n}{2}$ |
| **Suitable for small $n$ ($\leq 16$ elements)** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (overhead dominates) |
| **Suitable for large $n$** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Linear Search:** Used in **unsorted log files, small datasets, embedded systems with no sort routine, and as a subroutine in sub-linear algorithms** (e.g., brute-force string matching).
- **Binary Search:** Powers **database indexing (B-Trees), version control `git bisect`, dictionary lookups, sorted autocompletion, and OS process scheduling tables**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Tip:** The recursive variant of binary search consumes $O(\log_2 n)$ stack memory. In real-time systems (RTOS), the **iterative variant is preferred** to avoid stack overflow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Time Complexity of Linear Search

**Setup:** Let $T(n)$ be the worst-case number of comparisons for an array of $n$ elements. Assume the target $K$ is **not in the array** (worst case) or is at the **last position**.

The recurrence relation is:
$$T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$$

Unrolling the recurrence step by step:

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$$

$$T(n-1) = T(n-2) + 1$$

$$T(n-2) = T(n-3) + 1$$

$$\vdots$$

$$T(2) = T(1) + 1$$

$$T(1) = 1 \quad \text{(base case — single comparison on the last element)}$$

**Substituting backward:**
$$T(n) = \underbrace{1 + 1 + 1 + \dots + 1}_{n \text{ times}} = n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = n \implies T(n) \in O(n)}$$

**Average Case Derivation:**
The probability of the target being at any of the $n$ positions is uniform: $P(i) = \frac{1}{n}$ for $i \in \{0, 1, \dots, n-1\}$.

The expected number of comparisons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[C] &= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i+1) \cdot P(i) \\
&= \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i+1) \cdot \frac{1}{n} \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} (i+1) \\
&= \frac{1}{n} \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
&= \frac{n+1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{E[C] = \frac{n+1}{2} \implies \text{Average Case} \in O(n)}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Time Complexity of Binary Search

**Setup:** At each step, the search space is divided in half. The target is **not present** (worst case), forcing the algorithm to recurse until the interval is empty.

The recurrence relation is:
$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

with the base case $T(1) = 1$.

**Unrolling the recurrence:**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1$$

$$T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1$$

$$T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 1$$

$$\vdots$$

After $k$ unrollings:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k$$

The recursion terminates when $\frac{n}{2^k} = 1$, i.e., $2^k = n$, which gives:

$$k = \log_2 n$$

**Substituting back:**

$$T(n) = T(1) + \log_2 n = 1 + \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \log_2 n + 1 \implies T(n) \in O(\log_2 n)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Trace Binary Search

**Problem:** Search for $K = 45$ in the sorted array $A = [12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 67, 78, 89]$ where $n = 8$.

| Step | low | high | mid | $A[\text{mid}]$ | Compare with 45 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 7 | 3 | **45** | $45 = 45$ ✅ | **FOUND at index 3** |

For a *worst-case trace* (e.g., $K = 56$):

| Step | low | high | mid | $A[\text{mid}]$ | Compare with 56 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 7 | 3 | 45 | $56 > 45$ | low $\leftarrow 4$ |
| 2 | 4 | 7 | 5 | 67 | $56 < 67$ | high $\leftarrow 4$ |
| 3 | 4 | 4 | 4 | 56 | $56 = 56$ ✅ | **FOUND at index 4** |

Total comparisons = 3 = $\lfloor \log_2 8 \rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 - 1$ ... wait, $2^3 = 8 \implies \lfloor \log_2 8 \rfloor = 3$, so worst case = $3 + 1 - 1$ ... let me restate cleanly:

For $n = 8$: $\lfloor \log_2 8 \rfloor + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ **maximum comparisons**, but in this specific trace, the element was found in 3.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification against formula:** The maximum number of comparisons is $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$. For $n = 8$, this gives $3 + 1 = 4$. Our trace needed only 3 because the element was found early — the formula is a **worst-case upper bound**.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Trace Linear Search

**Problem:** Search for $K = 67$ in $A = [12, 23, 34, 45, 56, 67, 78, 89]$.

| Step | $i$ | $A[i]$ | Compare with 67 | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 12 | $67 \neq 12$ | Continue |
| 2 | 1 | 23 | $67 \neq 23$ | Continue |
| 3 | 2 | 34 | $67 \neq 34$ | Continue |
| 4 | 3 | 45 | $67 \neq 45$ | Continue |
| 5 | 4 | 56 | $67 \neq 56$ | Continue |
| 6 | 5 | 67 | $67 = 67$ ✅ | **FOUND at index 5** |

Total comparisons = 6 = $\frac{n+1}{2} = \frac{8+1}{2} = 4.5 \approx 4\text{–}5$ on average — observed value 6 lies in the worst-case regime since the target is in the **second half**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Linear Search — Functional Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0([Start]) --> A1[Initialize i = 0]
    A1 --> A2{i less than n?}
    A2 -- No --> A3[Return -1, NOT FOUND]
    A3 --> A4([End])
    A2 -- Yes --> A5{A i equals target K?}
    A5 -- Yes --> A6[Return index i, FOUND]
    A6 --> A4
    A5 -- No --> A7[Increment i = i + 1]
    A7 --> A2
```

### 4.2 Binary Search — Functional Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0([Start]) --> B1[Initialize low = 0, high = n-1]
    B1 --> B2{low is less than or equal to high?}
    B2 -- No --> B3[Return -1, NOT FOUND]
    B3 --> B4([End])
    B2 -- Yes --> B5[Compute mid = low + floor of high minus low divided by 2]
    B5 --> B6{A mid equals target K?}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[Return mid, FOUND]
    B7 --> B4
    B6 -- No --> B8{A mid is greater than K?}
    B8 -- Yes --> B9[Set high = mid - 1]
    B8 -- No --> B10[Set low = mid + 1]
    B9 --> B2
    B10 --> B2
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Linear vs Binary Search (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LS[Linear Search Module]
        LS0[Input: Unsorted or Sorted Array A of size n] --> LS1[Set i = 0]
        LS1 --> LS2[Loop: Compare A i with Key K]
        LS2 --> LS3{Match?}
        LS3 -- Yes --> LS4[Return i FOUND]
        LS3 -- No --> LS5[Increment i]
        LS5 --> LS6{i less than n?}
        LS6 -- Yes --> LS2
        LS6 -- No --> LS7[Return minus 1 NOT FOUND]
    end

    subgraph BS[Binary Search Module]
        BS0[Input: Sorted Array A of size n] --> BS1[Set low = 0, high = n minus 1]
        BS1 --> BS2[mid = low plus high minus low over 2]
        BS2 --> BS3{A mid compared with K}
        BS3 -- Equal --> BS4[Return mid FOUND]
        BS3 -- Greater --> BS5[high = mid minus 1]
        BS3 -- Lesser --> BS6[low = mid plus 1]
        BS5 --> BS7{low less than or equal to high}
        BS6 --> BS7
        BS7 -- Yes --> BS2
        BS7 -- No --> BS8[Return minus 1 NOT FOUND]
    end
```

### 4.4 Complexity Growth Visualization (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[n equals 10] --> C1[Linear: 10 vs Binary: 4]
    N2[n equals 100] --> C2[Linear: 100 vs Binary: 7]
    N3[n equals 1,000] --> C3[Linear: 1000 vs Binary: 10]
    N4[n equals 1,000,000] --> C4[Linear: 1,000,000 vs Binary: 20]

    C1 --> OBS1[For n equal to 1 million, Binary is 50000 times faster]
    C2 --> OBS1
    C3 --> OBS1
    C4 --> OBS1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Linear Search and Binary Search algorithms. List any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Linear Search | Binary Search |
|---|---|---|
