# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Priority Queue ADT — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Code / Algorithm Layer]
    
    subgraph ADT[Priority Queue ADT Interface]
        direction TB
        Op1[Insert key, value]
        Op2[ExtractMin / ExtractMax]
        Op3[Peek / FindMin]
        Op4[DecreaseKey / IncreaseKey]
        Op5[isEmpty, Size]
    end
    
    subgraph Impl[Implementation Layer]
        direction LR
        Impl1[Unsorted Array]
        Impl2[Sorted Array]
        Impl3[Binary Heap - Min]
        Impl4[Binary Heap - Max]
        Impl5[Linked List]
        Impl6[Balanced BST - Red Black]
    end
    
    subgraph Storage[Storage Layer]
        Mem1[Array - Contiguous Memory]
        Mem2[Pointer Based Nodes]
    end
    
    Client --> Op1
    Client --> Op2
    Client --> Op3
    Client --> Op4
    Client --> Op5
    
    Op1 --> Impl3
    Op2 --> Impl3
    Op3 --> Impl3
    Op4 --> Impl3
    Op5 --> Impl3
    
    Impl1 --> Mem1
    Impl2 --> Mem1
    Impl3 --> Mem1
    Impl4 --> Mem1
    Impl5 --> Mem2
    Impl6 --> Mem2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Priority Queue is shown as a 3-tier architecture — the **Client** (algorithms like Dijkstra's), the **ADT** (abstract operations), and the **Implementation** (concrete data structures). KTU exams often ask you to choose the *best* implementation for a given use case based on which operation is most frequent.

## 4.2 Min-Heap Insertion Flow — Heapify-Up Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start Insert element x]) --> Step1[Step 1: Append x at end of array at index i = n]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: While i greater than 0 AND parent of i is greater than x]
    Step2 --> Decision1{Heap property violated?}
    Decision1 -- No --> Done1([Insertion complete])
    Decision1 -- Yes --> Step3[Step 3: Swap A at i with A at parent of i]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4: Update i = parent of i]
    Step4 --> Decision1
    Done1 --> End([End])
```

## 4.3 Min-Heap ExtractMin Flow — Heapify-Down Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start ExtractMin]) --> Step1[Step 1: Save root value min = A at 0]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2: Move last element to root, decrease size]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3: Set i = 0]
    Step3 --> Decision1{i has a child?}
    Decision1 -- No --> Done1([Return saved min])
    Decision1 -- Yes --> Step4[Step 4: Find index j of smaller child of i]
    Step4 --> Decision2{A at i less than or equal A at j?}
    Decision2 -- Yes --> Done1
    Decision2 -- No --> Step5[Step 5: Swap A at i with A at j]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6: Update i = j, go to Decision1]
    Done1 --> End([End])
```

## 4.4 Heap Property Compliance — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Heap Type?] --> Branch1{Min-Heap or Max-Heap?}
    Branch1 -- Min-Heap --> Rule1[Rule: Parent of i is less than or equal to A at i]
    Branch1 -- Max-Heap --> Rule2[Rule: Parent of i is greater than or equal to A at i]
    Rule1 --> Complete[Structural Rule: Complete Binary Tree - All levels filled left to right]
    Rule2 --> Complete
    Complete --> Result[Valid Heap]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the above diagrams are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `Start`, `Step1`, `Decision1`, `Impl3`, `Mem2`) — no reserved keywords used. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.5 Priority Queue vs Ordinary Queue — Side-by-Side Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OrdQ[Ordinary FIFO Queue]
        direction LR
        A1[A in] --> B1[B]
        B1 --> C1[C]
        C1 --> D1[D out]
    end
    
    subgraph PQ[Priority Queue]
        direction LR
        P1[Arrived 1st, priority LOW] 
        P2[Arrived 2nd, priority HIGH]
        P3[Arrived 3rd, priority MEDIUM]
        P2 -- Highest priority --> Out1[Out first]
        P3 -- Second --> Out2[Out second]
        P1 -- Third --> Out3[Out third]
    end
```

**Interpretation:** In an **ordinary queue**, insertion order = service order. In a **priority queue**, service order is determined by **priority keys**, completely independent of insertion time.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a Priority Queue. Differentiate between a Max-Priority Queue and a Min-Priority Queue with one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Priority Queue** is an abstract data type where each element has an associated *priority* and the element with the highest (or lowest) priority is served first, regardless of insertion order.

| Feature | Min-Priority Queue | Max-Priority Queue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Order of service** | Smallest key first | Largest key first |
| **Root of underlying heap** | Global minimum | Global maximum |
| **Heap property** | $\text{parent} \leq \text{child}$ | $\text{parent} \geq \text{child}$ |
| **Real-world example** | Dijkstra's algorithm (next nearest vertex) | CPU scheduler (highest priority process first) |
| **Typical `Extract` op** | `ExtractMin` | `ExtractMax` |

*(Award: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for table + 1 mark for examples)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the time complexity of the `Insert` operation in a binary heap-based Priority Queue.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The `Insert` operation in a binary heap of $n$ elements runs in $O(\log n)$ time.

**Proof Outline:**

1. Insertion appends the new element at the end of the array — **$O(1)$**.
2. `Heapify-Up` is then called: it walks up from the inserted leaf to the root, swapping with the parent whenever the heap property is violated.
3. The height of a complete binary tree with $n$ nodes is $h = \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.
4. The `Heapify-Up` traversal visits at most $h + 1$ nodes (one per level), performing constant work at each.
5. Therefore:

$$T(n) = O(h) = O(\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor) = O(\log n) \quad \blacksquare$$

*(Award: 1 mark for statement + 1 mark for steps 1–4 + 1 mark for final equation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Pattern:** Two full 14-mark questions are given as alternatives; answer **either** one. Each is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### 📝 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Construct a Min-Heap from the following array using Floyd's bottom-up `Build-Heap` algorithm. Show all intermediate steps clearly. Input: $A = [9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 7, 1, 4, 8]$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 0: Identify last non-leaf node.**
For $n = 9$ (0-indexed), the last non-leaf node is at $\lfloor 9/2 \rfloor - 1 = 3$. So we apply `Heapify-Down` at indices $3, 2, 1, 0$ in that order.

**Initial array:** $[9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 7, 1, 4, 8]$

**Step 1: Heapify-Down at $i = 3$ (value 2).**
- Children: $A[7] = 4$, $A[8] = 8$. Smaller = 4.
- $2 \leq 4$ → heap property holds. **No swap.** Move on.

Array unchanged: $[9, 5, 6, 2, 3, 7, 1, 4, 8]$

**Step 2: Heapify-Down at $i = 2$ (value 6).**
- Children: $A[5] = 7$, $A[6] = 1$. Smaller = 1.
- $6 > 1$ → **violated**. Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[6]$.
- Array: $[9, 5, 1, 2, 3, 7, 6, 4, 8]$. Node 6 is now at index 6 — leaf, stop.

**Step 3: Heapify-Down at $i = 1$ (value 5).**
- Children: $A[3] = 2$, $A[4] = 3$. Smaller = 2.
- $5 > 2$ → **violated**. Swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[3]$.
- Array: $[9, 2, 1, 5, 3, 7, 6, 4, 8]$. New index of 5 is 3.
- Children of new index 3: $A[7] = 4$, $A[8] = 8$. Smaller = 4.
- $5 > 4$ → **violated**. Swap $A[3] \leftrightarrow A[7]$.
- Array: $[9, 2, 1, 4, 3, 7, 6, 5, 8]$. Node 5 is now at index 7 — leaf, stop.

**Step 4: Heapify-Down at $i = 0$ (value 9).**
- Children: $A[1] = 2$, $A[2] = 1$. Smaller = 1.
- $9 > 1$ → **violated**. Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[2]$.
- Array: $[1, 2, 9, 4, 3, 7, 6, 5, 8]$. New index of 9 is 2.
- Children of new index 2: $A[5] = 7$, $A[6] = 6$. Smaller = 6.
- $9 > 6$ → **violated**. Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[6]$.
- Array: $[1, 2, 6, 4, 3, 7, 9, 5, 8]$. New index of 9 is 6 — leaf, stop.

**Final Min-Heap:**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{heap}} = [1, 2, 6, 4, 3, 7, 9, 5, 8]}$$

**Verification (Tree form):**

```
              1
           /     \
          2       6
         / \     / \
        4   3   7   9
       / \
      5   8
```

All parents ≤ children. ✅

*(Mark split: 1 mark identifying last non-leaf, 4 marks for each Heapify-Down step, 1 mark final verification, 1 mark for time complexity note.)*

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Perform the following sequence of operations on an initially empty Min-Priority Queue (implemented as a binary heap). Show the heap after each operation.**

Operations: `Insert(20)`, `Insert(15)`, `Insert(30)`, `Insert(5)`, `ExtractMin()`, `Insert(10)`, `ExtractMin()`.

**Model Solution:**

| Step | Operation | Heap (array form) | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `Insert(20)` | $[20]$ | Single element, valid heap. |
| 2 | `Insert(15)` | $[15, 20]$ | Heapify-Up: 15 < 20, swap. |
| 3 | `Insert(30)` | $[15, 20, 30]$ | 30 ≥ 15, no swap. |
| 4 | `Insert(5)` | $[5, 15, 30, 20]$ | Heapify-Up: 5 < 15, then 5 < 20, moves to root. |
| 5 | `ExtractMin()` | $[15, 20, 30]$, **returns 5** | Move 30 to root, Heapify-Down: 30 > 15, swap. |
| 6 | `Insert(10)` | $[10, 20, 15, 30]$ | Heapify-Up: 10 < 20, swap. |
| 7 | `ExtractMin()` | $[15, 30, 20]$, **returns 10** | Move 20 to root, Heapify-Down: 20 > 15, swap. |

**Detailed trace of Step 4 (`Insert(5)`):**
- Append 5 at index 3: $[15, 20, 30, 5]$.
- Parent of index 3 = 1, $A[1] = 20$. $5 < 20$ → swap. Array: $[15, 5, 30, 20]$.
- Parent of index 1 = 0, $A[0] = 15$. $5 < 15$ → swap. Array: $[5, 15, 30, 20]$.
- Index 0 is the root → **stop**.

**Detailed trace of Step 5 (`ExtractMin`):**
- Save root = 5.
- Move last element (20) to root: $[20, 15, 30]$. Size = 3.
- Heapify-Down at index 0: smaller child is $A[1] = 15$. $20 > 15$ → swap. Array: $[15, 20, 30]$. Stop.
- **Returns 5.**

*(Mark split: 1 mark per operation step × 7 = 7 marks. Partial credit for incomplete traces.)*

---

### 📝 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the difference between a Min-Heap and a Max-Heap. Write algorithms for `Insert` and `ExtractMin` operations on a Min-Heap. State the time complexity of each.**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference (2 marks):**

| Property | Min-Heap | Max-Heap |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Heap property | $A[\text{parent}] \leq A[\text{child}]$ | $A[\text{parent}] \geq A[\text{child}]$ |
| Root value | Global **minimum** of the heap | Global **maximum** of the heap |
| Used in | Dijkstra, Prim, Huffman | OS schedulers, max-priority scheduling |
| Extract operation | `ExtractMin` | `ExtractMax` |
| Topological order | Increasing from root to leaves | Decreasing from root to leaves |

**Algorithm: `Insert(MinHeap A, key k)`** (2 marks)
```
1. A.heapSize ← A.heapSize + 1
2. i ← A.heapSize
3. A[i] ← k
4. while i > 1 and A[parent(i)] > A[i] do
5.     swap A[i] ↔ A[parent(i)]
6.     i ← parent(i)
```
**Time complexity:** $O(\log n)$ (Heapify-Up traverses at most the height of the tree).

**Algorithm: `ExtractMin(MinHeap A)`** (3 marks)
```
1. if A.heapSize < 1 then return ERROR
2. min ← A[1]                              // save root
3. A[1] ← A[A.heapSize]                    // move last to root
4. A.heapSize ← A.heapSize − 1             // logically delete last
5. i ← 1
6. while 2i ≤ A.heapSize do                // while i has at least one child
7.     j ← 2i                              // left child
8.     if j + 1 ≤ A.heapSize and A[j+1] < A[j] then
9.         j ← j + 1                       // j is smaller child
10.    if A[i] ≤ A[j] then break           // heap property holds
11.    swap A[i] ↔ A[j]
12.    i ← j
13. return min
```
**Time complexity:** $O(\log n)$ (Heapify-Down traverses at most the height of the tree).

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm Notation Note:** The above uses 1-based indexing for clarity (matches textbook CLRS convention). To convert to 0-based indexing (used in most modern C/Java/C++ implementations), replace `parent(i) = i/2` with `(i-1)/2`, `2i` with `2i+1`, `2i+1` with `2i+2`, and start with `i = 0`.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Consider a Max-Heap implemented as an array: $A = [50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 35]$. Perform the operation `Insert(45)`. Show the array after each swap and state the resulting heap.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 0: Initial state.**
Array: $[50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 35]$. Heap size $n = 6$.

Visualization:
```
            50
          /    \
        30      40
       /  \    /
     10   20 35
```

**Step 1: Append 45 at index 6.**
Array: $[50, 30, 40, 10, 20, 35, 45]$.

**Step 2: Heapify-Up from index 6 (Max-Heap: parent must be ≥ child).**
- Parent of index 6 = $\lfloor (6-1)/2 \rfloor = 2$. $A[2] = 40$. Compare $40$ vs $45$: $40 < 45$ → **violated**, swap.
- Array: $[50, 30, 45, 10, 20, 35, 40]$. Index of 45 is now 2.
- Parent of index 2 = $\lfloor (2-1)/2 \rfloor = 0$. $A[0] = 50$. Compare $50$ vs $45$: $50 \geq 45$ → **heap property holds**. Stop.

**Step 3: Final heap.**
$$\boxed{A = [50, 30, 45, 10, 20, 35, 40]}$$

Visualization:
```
            50
          /    \
        30      45
       /  \    /  \
     10   20 35   40
```

**Verification:** All parents $\geq$ children: $50 \geq 30, 45$; $30 \geq 10, 20$; $45 \geq 35, 40$. ✅

**Time taken:** $O(\log n) = O(\log 6) \approx 2.58$ → effectively 2 comparisons. *(Mark split: 1 mark for initial state, 3 marks for Heapify-Up trace, 1 mark for final array, 1 mark for tree verification, 1 mark for complexity.)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Priority Queue Questions**
> 
> **1. Forgetting the index base.** Many KTU answer sheets use **1-based indexing** (matching CLRS textbook) while most code uses **0-based**. Mixing the two causes wrong parent/child formulas. Always **declare the indexing convention** at the start of your answer.
> 
> **2. Wrong comparison direction in `Heapify-Up`/`Heapify-Down`.** For a **Max-Heap** the parent must be $\geq$ children; for a **Min-Heap** the parent must be $\leq$ children. Mixing these up reverses the entire algorithm.
> 
> **3. Stopping `Heapify-Down` too early.** Always check that the current node is **strictly greater** (Min-Heap) or **strictly less** (Max-Heap) than the smaller/larger child **before** swapping. **Equality** means heap property holds — do not swap.
> 
> **4. Confusing $O(n)$ vs $O(n \log n)$ for `BuildHeap`.** The Floyd bottom-up algorithm is $O(n)$ because most `Heapify-Down` calls are on small sub-heaps near the leaves. Writing $O(n \log n)$ here costs 1–2 marks in derivations.
> 
> **5. Not stating the comparator.** Every Priority Queue operation assumes a defined total order. State "we use the natural ordering on integers" or similar in the first line.
> 
> **6. Drawing the heap as a general binary tree, not a complete one.** Always emphasize that a binary heap is a **complete** binary tree — this is what enables array storage and the $O(1)$ index-based navigation.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU exam!**

- ✅ A **Priority Queue** is an ADT where the element with the highest (or lowest) priority is served first, regardless of insertion order.
- ✅ It supports `Insert`, `ExtractMin/Max`, `Peek`, `DecreaseKey/IncreaseKey`, `isEmpty`, `Size`.
- ✅ A **Binary Heap** is the most common implementation; it is a **complete binary tree** satisfying the **heap property**.
- ✅ **Array-based storage** is used (cache-friendly, no pointers): 0-based formulas — $\text{parent}(i) = \lfloor (i-1)/2 \rfloor$, $\text{left}(i) = 2i+1$, $\text{right}(i) = 2i+2$.
- ✅ **Insert** = append + `Heapify-Up`. **Extract** = move last to root + `Heapify-Down`. Both take $O(\log n)$.
- ✅ `Peek` is $O(1)$ — just return the root.
- ✅ **BuildHeap** using Floyd's bottom-up algorithm takes $O(n)$, **not** $O(n \log n)$.
- ✅ A heap with $n$ nodes has height $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ and $\lceil n/2 \rceil$ leaves.
- ✅ **Min-Heap** root = global minimum. **Max-Heap** root = global maximum.
- ✅ Real-world use: **Dijkstra's algorithm, Prim's MST, Huffman coding, OS schedulers, event-driven simulation, A* search**.
- ✅ **Fibonacci Heaps** improve `Insert` to $O(1)$ amortized but make `ExtractMin` $O(\log n)$ amortized.
- ✅ **Time complexities to memorize**: `Insert` = $O(\log n)$, `ExtractMin/Max` = $O(\log n)$, `Peek` = $O(1)$, `BuildHeap` = $O(n)$.
- ✅ **Key exam trick**: When asked to "implement a PQ efficiently", the answer is **always a binary heap** unless specified otherwise.
- ✅ **Heap Sort** = `BuildHeap` (O(n)) + repeated `ExtractMin/Max` (n × O(log n)) = **$O(n \log n)$ overall** — this is how heaps connect to sorting algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip**
> When tracing a heap operation in your answer sheet, **always show three things**: (1) the array form after each swap, (2) the parent/child index calculation, and (3) the comparison being made. Answer sheets with all three present almost always receive full marks, while those missing the index calculation lose 1–2 marks routinely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double Ended Queues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double Ended Queues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Double Ended Queue (Deque / Deck)** is a generalized, sequential abstract data type (ADT) in which elements can be **inserted** and **deleted** from **both ends** (the *front* and the *rear*). It combines the flexibility of both the **Stack (LIFO)** and the **Queue (FIFO)** data structures within a single, unified ADT.

Formally, a Deque of size $n$ over a finite alphabet of elements $\Sigma$ is defined as a sequence:

$$D = \{d_0, d_1, d_2, \ldots, d_{n-1\} \quad \text{where} \quad d_i \in \Sigma$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> A Deque is treated under the "Linear Data Structures – Queues" unit in **Module 1**. Students must master the four primitive mutations (`insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear`) and the two restricted variants (Input-Restricted and Output-Restricted) which are **frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions**.

## 1.2 The Two Restricted Variants of Deque

A Deque, in its unrestricted form, allows all four mutations. However, the KTU syllabus emphasizes two constrained descendants:

### (a) Input-Restricted Deque
- **Insertion** is permitted at **only one end** (typically the *rear*).
- **Deletion** is permitted at **both ends**.
- Behaves like a **hybrid Stack–Queue**.

### (b) Output-Restricted Deque
- **Deletion** is permitted at **only one end** (typically the *front*).
- **Insertion** is permitted at **both ends**.
- Used in **sliding window** algorithms and **palindrome checkers**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Train Platform Tunnel:**
> Imagine a **two-way pedestrian tunnel** at a railway station. People can **enter from the left gate**, walk across, and **exit from the right gate** (this is a normal Queue).
> 
> Now, suppose the platform becomes too crowded. The station master decides to **open BOTH gates on EACH side** for crowd control. People can now:
> - **Enter from the left front** OR **enter from the left rear**, and
> - **Exit from the right front** OR **exit from the right rear**.
> 
> This two-way, both-ends accessible tunnel **is your Deque**. Unlike a normal queue (one-way traffic), a Deque allows traffic flow from **both directions at both ends** — hence the name **Double Ended Queue**.

## 1.4 Standard ADT Operations of a Deque

| # | Operation | Functional Role |
|---|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | `insertFront(x)` | Inserts element $x$ at the **front** position |
| 2 | `insertRear(x)` | Inserts element $x$ at the **rear** position |
| 3 | `deleteFront()` | Removes and returns element from the **front** |
| 4 | `deleteRear()` | Removes and returns element from the **rear** |
| 5 | `getFront()` | Peeks at the front element (no mutation) |
| 6 | `getRear()` | Peeks at the rear element (no mutation) |
| 7 | `isEmpty()` | Returns `TRUE` if no elements exist |
| 8 | `isFull()` | Returns `TRUE` if capacity is exhausted (array form only) |

> [!NOTE]
> The exact 8-operation ADT shown above is the **standard reference** for the KTU 2024 Board. Students writing 14-mark Part B answers **must reproduce** this ADT interface before any algorithm or code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

A Deque is conceptually a **linear sequence** with two cursor positions: `front` and `rear`. Every operation either advances or retreats these cursors. Let us dissect the operational logic step-by-step.

### 2.1.1 Initialization Phase
- Allocate a contiguous array `D[0 ... MAX - 1]` of size **MAX = N** in the heap memory.
- Initialize both pointer indices:
  - `front = -1` (signifies the Deque is empty in the non-circular model)
  - `rear = -1` (signifies the Deque is empty in the non-circular model)

### 2.1.2 Insertion Logic — Four Cases
For an **array-based deque** (often implemented as a **circular buffer** for $O(1)$ space efficiency), the index update rules are:

- **`insertFront(x)`** → Decrement `front` in **circular fashion**, then place $x$.
- **`insertRear(x)`** → Place $x$ at `rear`, then increment `rear` in **circular fashion**.
- **`deleteFront()`** → Read the element at `front`, then advance `front` circularly.
- **`deleteRear()`** → Read the element at `rear`, then retreat `rear` circularly.

The **circular modular arithmetic** keeps indices bounded within $[0, N-1]$ even after thousands of operations:

$$\text{front} \;\leftarrow\; (\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$$

$$\text{rear} \;\leftarrow\; (\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$$

### 2.1.3 Empty vs Full State Discrimination
This is the **classic KTU trick question**. In a circular buffer of size $N$, the number of stored elements is given by:

$$\text{size} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$$

- **Empty** when: $\text{size} = 0$ (i.e., `front == rear` *and* no elements added).
- **Full** when: $\text{size} = N$ (i.e., `front == rear` *but* one slot is deliberately left vacant, OR we maintain a separate `count` variable).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall (Most Common Mistake):**
> When `front == rear`, the deque can mean **EITHER empty OR full**. The classical bug-free method is to **sacrifice one slot** (i.e., capacity = $N-1$) OR to maintain an explicit `count` counter. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for not clarifying this.

## 2.2 Why Circular Implementation? — The Engineering Rationale

In a **non-circular** array implementation, repeated `insertFront` and `deleteFront` operations would cause the front pointer to **drift to negative indices** (underflow of memory). Worse, after several `deleteFront`s, the *front half* of the array becomes **wasted space** — a phenomenon called **"memory fragmentation by left drift"**.

A **circular deque** solves this by treating the array as a **logical ring**, allowing the front to wrap around to the end of the array. This achieves:

- **Space efficiency** = $\Theta(N)$ (no wastage).
- **Time efficiency** = $O(1)$ for all 4 mutations.
- **Cache locality** = Better than linked-list deque (which has $\Theta(1)$ time but poor cache performance).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Operating Systems** | Process scheduling — `A-Steal` work queue for multi-core CPU load balancing |
| **Data Streaming** | Sliding window maximum/minimum in real-time analytics (e.g., stock tickers) |
| **Browser History** | Forward/Backward navigation — front = back, rear = forward |
| **Compiler Design** | Aroma of undo/redo buffers in IDEs (Output-Restricted Deque) |
| **Algorithms** | Palindrome checking, 0/1 BFS, monotonic queue in DP |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** for the KTU 2024 ESE. Every value is a **high-probability question**.

| # | Property / Metric | Formula / Value | Unit / Domain |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | Array size allocated | $N$ (or `MAX_SIZE`) | Integer slots |
| 2 | Usable capacity (circular) | $N - 1$ (with one-slot sacrifice) | Integer slots |
| 3 | Usable capacity (with counter) | $N$ (no slot sacrifice) | Integer slots |
| 4 | Front index after `insertFront` | $(\text{front} - 1 + N) \bmod N$ | $[0, N-1]$ |
| 5 | Rear index after `insertRear` | $(\text{rear} + 1) \bmod N$ | $[0, N-1]$ |
| 6 | Current size | $(\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$ | $[0, N]$ |
| 7 | `isEmpty` condition | $\text{front} = -1$ (initial) **or** size $= 0$ | Boolean |
| 8 | `isFull` condition | size $= N$ (or $N-1$ in sacrificial model) | Boolean |
| 9 | Time complexity of all 4 mutations | $O(1)$ | Constant |
| 10 | Space complexity | $O(N)$ | Linear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Table Construction:**
> In the formulas above, the modular expression $(x + N) \bmod N$ is written as `((x % N) + N) % N` in C-style code. The double-modulo prevents the **C-negative-modulo bug** (where $-1 \% N` yields $-1$ in C99, not $N-1$).

## 2.5 Conceptual Comparison: Deque vs Queue vs Stack

| Feature | Stack | Queue | Deque |
|---------|-------|-------|-------|
| Insertion ends allowed | 1 (top) | 1 (rear) | **2 (front + rear)** |
| Deletion ends allowed | 1 (top) | 1 (front) | **2 (front + rear)** |
| Order discipline | LIFO | FIFO | **Both (configurable)** |
| Time per mutation | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| KTU board weightage | High | High | **Medium-High** |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Trace of Circular Deque Operations

Consider an array `D[8]` (i.e., $N = 8$) using the **one-slot-sacrifice** model (usable capacity = 7). Initial state: `front = 0`, `rear = 0`, slot `D[0]` is permanently wasted as a sentinel. The deque is logically **EMPTY**.

### 3.1.1 Operation Sequence Trace

| Step | Operation | Pre-state (front, rear) | Action | Post-state (front, rear) | Result / Element |
|------|-----------|--------------------------|--------|---------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | `insertRear(10)` | (0, 0) | Place 10 at `D[rear]`=D[0], rear ← (0+1)%8 = 1 | (0, 1) | 10 inserted |
| 2 | `insertRear(20)` | (0, 1) | Place 20 at D[1], rear ← 2 | (0, 2) | 20 inserted |
| 3 | `insertFront(5)` | (0, 2) | front ← (0−1+8)%8 = 7, place 5 at D[7] | (7, 2) | 5 inserted |
| 4 | `insertFront(2)` | (7, 2) | front ← (7−1+8)%8 = 6, place 2 at D[6] | (6, 2) | 2 inserted |
| 5 | `deleteFront()` | (6, 2) | Read D[6]=2, front ← (6+1)%8 = 7 | (7, 2) | Returns **2** |
| 6 | `deleteRear()` | (7, 2) | Read D[1]=20 (rear−1=1), rear ← (2−1+8)%8 = 1 | (7, 1) | Returns **20** |
| 7 | `insertRear(30)` | (7, 1) | Place 30 at D[1], rear ← 2 | (7, 2) | 30 inserted |
| 8 | `insertRear(40)` | (7, 2) | Place 40 at D[2], rear ← 3 | (7, 3) | 40 inserted |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** The columns `(front, rear)` represent the cursor positions in the circular buffer. The size formula $\text{size} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$ consistently yields the correct count. After step 4, size $= (2 - 6 + 8) \bmod 8 = 4$, which matches the 4 elements stored.

### 3.1.2 Mathematical Derivation of the Wrap-Around Formula

We need a formula that maps any integer index to a value in $[0, N-1]$. The naïve decrement $\text{front} - 1$ fails when $\text{front} = 0$ (it becomes $-1$, an out-of-bounds index). We use modular arithmetic:

$$\text{front}_{\text{new}} = \left( \text{front}_{\text{old}} - 1 + N \right) \bmod N$$

**Step-by-step proof of correctness:**

Suppose $N = 8$ and $\text{front}_{\text{old}} = 0$. Then:

$$\text{front}_{\text{new}} = (0 - 1 + 8) \bmod 8 = 7 \bmod 8 = 7 \;\; \checkmark$$

Suppose $N = 8$ and $\text{front}_{\text{old}} = 5$. Then:

$$\text{front}_{\text{new}} = (5 - 1 + 8) \bmod 8 = 12 \bmod 8 = 4 \;\; \checkmark$$

Suppose $N = 8$ and $\text{front}_{\text{old}} = 7$. Then:

$$\text{front}_{\text{new}} = (7 - 1 + 8) \bmod 8 = 14 \bmod 8 = 6 \;\; \checkmark$$

In all three cases, the result lies in $[0, 7]$ and the **logical adjacency** (decrement by 1 in the circular sense) is preserved.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Size Formula

Given `front` and `rear`, the **cardinality** of the deque is:

$$\text{size} = (\text{rear} - \text{front} + N) \bmod N$$

**Why the `+N` term?**  
Because the direct subtraction `rear − front` can be negative (e.g., `rear = 2`, `front = 6` gives $-4$). Adding $N$ shifts the difference into the non-negative range. The outer `mod N` then maps the result to $[0, N-1]$.

**Worked example:** Let $N = 8$, `front = 6`, `rear = 2$. Then:

$$\text{size} = (2 - 6 + 8) \bmod 8 = 4 \bmod 8 = 4$$

This is correct: the elements occupy slots $\{6, 7, 0, 1\}$ — exactly 4 slots.

## 3.2 Reference Implementation Skeleton (Array-Based, Circular)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#define MAX 8

int deque[MAX];
int front = -1, rear = -1;
int count = 0;   // explicit counter eliminates empty/full ambiguity

int isFull()  { return (count == MAX); }
int isEmpty() { return (count == 0);  }

void insertFront(int x) {
    if (isFull()) { printf("Overflow\n"); return; }
    if (isEmpty()) { front = rear = 0; }
    else { front = ((front - 1 + MAX) % MAX); }
    deque[front] = x;
    count++;
}

void insertRear(int x) {
    if (isFull()) { printf("Overflow\n"); return; }
    if (isEmpty()) { front = rear = 0; }
    else { rear = (rear + 1) % MAX; }
    deque[rear] = x;
    count++;
}

int deleteFront() {
    if (isEmpty()) { printf("Underflow\n"); return -1; }
    int x = deque[front];
    if (front == rear) { front = rear = -1; }
    else { front = (front + 1) % MAX; }
    count--;
    return x;
}

int deleteRear() {
    if (isEmpty()) { printf("Underflow\n"); return -1; }
    int x = deque[rear];
    if (front == rear) { front = rear = -1; }
    else { rear = ((rear - 1 + MAX) % MAX); }
    count--;
    return x;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Code Conformance:**
> The above skeleton is provided as a **reference** for students' lab work. In the **theory exam (ESE)**, the board expects the four mutation algorithms in **pseudocode or C-style format**, with clear handling of `isEmpty()` and `isFull()` boundary conditions. Failure to include both checks **invariably costs 2-3 marks**.

## 3.3 Application Walkthrough — Palindrome Checker

A **palindrome** reads the same forward and backward (e.g., `MADAM`). An **Output-Restricted Deque** is ideal:

1. Insert each character of the string at the **rear** (in order: M-A-D-A-M).
2. Compare `getFront()` and `getRear()` repeatedly:
   - Step 1: `M` vs `M` → match, `deleteFront()` and `deleteRear()`.
   - Step 2: `A` vs `A` → match.
   - Step 3: `D` vs `D` → match.
3. Deque is empty → **Palindrome confirmed** ✓.

**Time complexity:** $O(n)$ | **Space complexity:** $O(n)$ | **KTU Weightage:** High (favorite 7-mark sub-question).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Conceptual Deque ADT Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef op fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0D47A1
    classDef data fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef state fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20

    subgraph DATASTORAGE[UNDERLYING STORAGE]
        node1["ARRAY D 0 to N MINUS 1"]:::data
        node2["LINKED LIST NODES"]:::data
    end

    subgraph DEQUE_OPS[DEQUE OPERATIONS LAYER]
        opA["INSERTFRONT X"]:::op
        opB["INSERTREAR X"]:::op
        opC["DELETEFRONT"]:::op
        opD["DELETEREAR"]:::op
        opE["GETFRONT"]:::op
        opF["GETREAR"]:::op
        opG["ISEMPTY"]:::op
        opH["ISFULL"]:::op
    end

    subgraph RESTRICTED[RESTRICTED VARIANTS]
        node3["INPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]:::state
        node4["OUTPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]:::state
    end

    DEQUE_OPS --> DATASTORAGE
    DEQUE_OPS --> RESTRICTED

    opA -.uses.-> node1
    opB -.uses.-> node1
    opC -.uses.-> node1
    opD -.uses.-> node1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Circular Deque Index Behavior

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EMPTY: initialize front equals -1 and rear equals -1

    EMPTY --> ONE_ELEMENT: insertFront or insertRear
    ONE_ELEMENT --> MULTIPLE: any insert operation

    MULTIPLE --> ONE_ELEMENT: delete when size becomes 1
    ONE_ELEMENT --> EMPTY: delete last element

    state FULL_CHECK {
        [*] --> CHECK_COUNT
        CHECK_COUNT --> OVERFLOW: count equals N
        CHECK_COUNT --> NORMAL: count less than N
    }

    MULTIPLE --> FULL_CHECK: before insert
    ONE_ELEMENT --> FULL_CHECK: before insert

    OVERFLOW --> [*]: print overflow message
    NORMAL --> MULTIPLE: perform insertion
    NORMAL --> ONE_ELEMENT: perform insertion
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Logical Layout — Circular Buffer Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef slot fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef wasted fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#90A4AE,color:#000

    slot7["INDEX 7 VAL 50"]:::active
    slot0["INDEX 0 WASTED SENTINEL"]:::wasted
    slot1["INDEX 1 VAL 30"]:::active
    slot2["INDEX 2 VAL 40"]:::active
    slot3["INDEX 3 EMPTY"]:::slot
    slot4["INDEX 4 EMPTY"]:::slot
    slot5["INDEX 5 EMPTY"]:::slot
    slot6["INDEX 6 EMPTY"]:::slot

    frontPtr["FRONT POINTER AT 7"]:::ptr
    rearPtr["REAR POINTER AT 2"]:::ptr

    slot7 --- frontPtr
    slot2 --- rearPtr

    slot7 -.next.-> slot0
    slot0 -.next.-> slot1
    slot1 -.next.-> slot2
    slot2 -.next.-> slot3
    slot3 -.next.-> slot4
    slot4 -.next.-> slot5
    slot5 -.next.-> slot6
    slot6 -.next.-> slot7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The "circular" arrow from `INDEX 6` back to `INDEX 7` represents the modular wrap-around. The **wasted sentinel** at index 0 prevents the `front == rear` ambiguity (this is the **one-slot-sacrifice** strategy). The two red pointers `FRONT` and `REAR` demarcate the live data range $[7, 0, 1, 2]$ in the circular order.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Restricted Deques

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef allow fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000
    classDef deny fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef neutral fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000

    Q["DOUBLE ENDED QUEUE BASE ADT"]:::neutral

    Q --> IRD["INPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]
    Q --> ORD["OUTPUT RESTRICTED DEQUE"]

    IRD --> IRD_A["INSERT REAR ONLY ALLOWED"]:::allow
    IRD --> IRD_B["INSERT FRONT BLOCKED"]:::deny
    IRD --> IRD_C["DELETE FRONT ALLOWED"]:::allow
    IRD --> IRD_D["DELETE REAR ALLOWED"]:::allow

    ORD --> ORD_A["INSERT FRONT ALLOWED"]:::allow
    ORD --> ORD_B["INSERT REAR ALLOWED"]:::allow
    ORD --> ORD_C["DELETE FRONT ONLY ALLOWED"]:::allow
    ORD --> ORD_D["DELETE REAR BLOCKED"]:::deny
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Double Ended Queue (Deque). List its four basic operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Double Ended Queue (Deque)** is a linear data structure in which **insertion and deletion of elements can be performed at both ends** — the *front* and the *rear*. It generalizes both the Stack (LIFO) and the Queue (FIFO) by allowing four mutation operations:
>
> 1. **Insertion at the front** — `insertFront(x)`
> 2. **Insertion at the rear** — `insertRear(x)`
> 3. **Deletion at the front** — `deleteFront()`
> 4. **Deletion at the rear** — `deleteRear()`
>
> All four operations execute in **$O(1)$ time complexity**. The Deque ADT also supports auxiliary queries like `getFront()`, `getRear()`, `isEmpty()`, and `isFull()`. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for listing + 1 for complexity)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Input-Restricted and Output-Restricted Deque.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Property | Input-Restricted Deque | Output-Restricted Deque |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Insertion allowed at** | Rear end **only** | **Both** front and rear |
> | **Deletion allowed at** | **Both** front and rear | Front end **only** |
> | **Use case** | Palindrome checking, undo buffers | Sliding window, work-stealing schedulers |
> | **Behaves as** | A stack with queue-like deletion | A queue with stack-like insertion |
>
> In essence, the **input-restricted** variant restricts the *write* end to one location, whereas the **output-restricted** variant restricts the *read* end to one location. *(3 marks: 1 for each definition + 1 for the contrast table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

> **(a)** Explain the **array implementation of a Double Ended Queue (Deque)** with a suitable diagram. Discuss the **circular array technique** to overcome the limitation of the simple array approach. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the **algorithm (or C program)** to perform the following operations on a Deque implemented using a circular array of size `MAX = 5`:
> - `insertFront(item)`
> - `insertRear(item)`
> - `deleteFront()`
> - `deleteRear()`
>
> Demonstrate the working of the above functions with the sequence: `insertRear(10)`, `insertRear(20)`, `insertFront(5)`, `deleteRear()`, `insertFront(2)`, `deleteFront()`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):**  
A Deque is a linear data structure allowing insertion and deletion at both ends. In an **array implementation**, we maintain two integer pointers `front` and `rear` that index into a contiguous block `D[0 ... N-1]`.

**Simple (Non-Circular) Array — Limitation (2 marks):**  
In the simple (linear) array approach, the `front` pointer can move leftward (negative indices) or the `rear` pointer can move beyond `N-1`, leading to either **array underflow** or **array overflow** even when usable slots exist at the opposite end. This phenomenon is called **"memory drift"** or **"false overflow"**.

**Circular Array — Solution (2 marks):**  
The circular array technique treats the array as a **ring**: after index `N-1`, the pointer **wraps around** to `0`, and before index `0`, it wraps to `N-1`. The wrap-around is achieved by the modulo operator:

```
insertFront :  front = (front - 1 + N) % N
insertRear  :  rear  = (rear  + 1) % N
deleteFront :  front = (front + 1) % N
deleteRear  :  rear  = (rear  - 1 + N) % N
```

**Empty/Full Conditions (1 mark):**  
The classic ambiguity is that `front == rear` can mean **either empty or full**. Two solutions exist:
- **One-slot-sacrifice:** Usable capacity = $N - 1$. The slot at `front` is left vacant when full.
- **Explicit counter:** Maintain a `count` variable. Empty when `count == 0`, full when `count == N`.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Sketch the circular ring of $N$ slots, marking `front`, `rear`, and the wasted sentinel slot.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — 7 marks

**Algorithm Skeleton (4 marks):**

```
INSERT_FRONT(item):
    IF count == MAX THEN
        PRINT "Overflow" and RETURN
    END IF
    IF front == -1 THEN
        front = rear = 0
    ELSE
        front = ((front - 1 + MAX) % MAX)
    END IF
    D[front] = item
    count = count + 1

INSERT_REAR(item):
    IF count == MAX THEN
        PRINT "Overflow" and RETURN
    END IF
    IF rear == -1 THEN
        front = rear = 0
    ELSE
        rear = ((rear + 1) % MAX)
    END IF
    D[rear] = item
    count = count + 1

DELETE_FRONT():
    IF count == 0 THEN
        PRINT "Underflow" and RETURN -1
    END IF
    item = D[front]
    IF front == rear THEN
        front = rear = -1
    ELSE
        front = ((front + 1) % MAX)
    END IF
    count = count - 1
    RETURN item

DELETE_REAR():
    IF count == 0 THEN
        PRINT "Underflow" and RETURN -1
    END IF
    item = D[rear]
    IF front == rear THEN
        front = rear = -1
    ELSE
        rear = ((rear - 1 + MAX) % MAX)
    END IF
    count = count - 1
    RETURN item
```

**Demonstration Trace (3 marks):**

Initial: `front = -1, rear = -1, count = 0`, array empty.

| Step | Operation | Action | front | rear | count | Deque State (left → right) |
|------|-----------|--------|-------|------|-------|----------------------------|
| 1 | `insertRear(10)` | D[0]=10, rear=1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | [10] |
| 2 | `insertRear(20)` | D[1]=20, rear=2 | 0 | 2 | 2 | [10, 20] |
| 3 | `insertFront(5)` | front=(0-1+5)%5=4, D[4]=5 | 4 | 2 | 3 | [5, 10, 20] *(logically: 5 is in front)* |
| 4 | `deleteRear()` | item=D[2]=20, rear=(2-1+5)%5=1 | 4 | 1 | 2 | Returns 20. Deque: [5, 10] |
| 5 | `insertFront(2)` | front=(4-1+5)%5=3, D[3]=2 | 3 | 1 | 3 | [2, 5, 10] |
| 6 | `deleteFront()` | item=D[3]=2, front=(3+1)%5=4 | 4 | 1 | 2 | Returns 2. Deque: [5, 10] |

**Final Output Sequence:** 10 and 20 inserted; 5 inserted at front; **20 deleted** (from rear); 2 inserted at front; **2 deleted** (from front). Remaining deque: `[5, 10]`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Module Choice]

> **(a)** What is a **Double Ended Queue (Deque)**? Explain the **linked list implementation** of a Deque with diagrams. Compare it with the **array implementation**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Design a Deque-based algorithm to **check whether a given string is a palindrome** using an **Output-Restricted Deque**. Demonstrate your algorithm on the input string `DEED`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (1 mark):**  
A Deque is a linear ADT allowing insertions and deletions at both the front and rear ends.

**Linked List Implementation (4 marks):**  
A **doubly linked list** is the natural structure, where each node has:

```
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* prev;
    struct Node* next;
};
```

A single pointer `head` (or two pointers `head` and `tail`) is maintained. Each mutation is:
- `insertFront(x)`: Create node, attach as new head, update `head->prev = NULL` and (if not empty) `head->next->prev = newNode`.
- `insertRear(x)`: Traverse to tail (or use a `tail` pointer), append new node.
- `deleteFront()`: Remove `head`, advance `head = head->next`, set `head->prev = NULL`.
- `deleteRear()`: Remove tail, retreat `tail = tail->prev`, set `tail->next = NULL`.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Show the doubly linked list with `head` and `tail` pointers, and the `prev`/`next` arrows.

**Comparison with Array (1 mark):**

| Property | Array Deque | Linked List Deque |
|---|---|---|
| Memory layout | Contiguous | Scattered (heap nodes) |
| Cache locality | Excellent | Poor |
| Dynamic resizing | No (fixed) | Yes (truly dynamic) |
| Time per mutation | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Space overhead | None | 2 pointers per node |

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — 7 marks

**Algorithm (4 marks):**

```
ALGORITHM IsPalindrome(S):
    // S is the input string of length n
    Create an empty Output-Restricted Deque D
    
    FOR i = 0 TO n-1 DO
        D.insertRear(S[i])    // build the deque
    END FOR
    
    WHILE NOT D.isEmpty() DO
        IF D.getFront() != D.getRear() THEN
            RETURN FALSE
        END IF
        D.deleteFront()        // only deletion allowed at front
    END WHILE
    
    RETURN TRUE
```

> Note: In an **Output-Restricted Deque**, deletion is allowed only at the front. Hence we only need `deleteFront()`. The rear is *peeked* via `getRear()` but not deleted.

**Trace on `DEED` (3 marks):**

| Step | Operation | Deque State (front → rear) | getFront() | getRear() | Match? |
|------|-----------|---------------------------|------------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Build: insert D, E, E, D | `[D, E, E, D]` | — | — | — |
| 2 | Compare | `[D, E, E, D]` | D | D | ✓ |
| 3 | `deleteFront()` | `[E, E, D]` | E | D | mismatch? wait — getRear() now is D |
| 4 | Compare again | `[E, E, D]` | E | D | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction in trace logic:** When we delete from the front, the **rear element remains unchanged** until we ALSO delete from the rear. In an **Output-Restricted Deque**, since rear deletion is **blocked**, we must compare `getFront()` with `getRear()` and then `deleteFront()` — and ALSO **delete from the rear** by walking the list (in the linked list version) OR by using a **separate stack** as an auxiliary.

> **Refined Correct Algorithm:** For a true Output-Restricted Deque to work for palindrome checking, we must:
> 1. Build the deque (insert at both ends allowed).
> 2. Repeatedly `getFront()` and `getRear()`, then `deleteFront()`.
> 3. **Also pop from rear** by tracking the "effective rear" externally OR use a second auxiliary stack.

> **Simplified standard exam answer:** Most textbooks relax the "output-restricted" constraint for palindrome checking, allowing `deleteRear()` as well. The correct trace on `DEED` is:

| Step | Op | State | Front | Rear | Match |
|------|----|-------|-------|------|-------|
| 1 | Build | `[D, E, E, D]` | D | D | ✓ |
| 2 | `deleteFront` + `deleteRear` | `[E, E]` | E | E | ✓ |
| 3 | `deleteFront` + `deleteRear` | `[]` | — | — | ✓ |

**Result: `DEED` is a palindrome.** *(3 marks for trace + 1 for final answer)*

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ | **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Deque Questions:**
>
> 1. **Empty/Full Ambiguity (–2 marks):** Students write `isFull()` as `(rear == front)` without clarifying the **one-slot-sacrifice** or **counter-based** strategy. This confuses the examiner.
>
> 2. **Missing Underflow Check (–1 mark):** Every `delete` function MUST check `isEmpty()` first and return a sentinel like $-1$. Skipping this loses a mark.
>
> 3. **Non-Circular vs Circular Mix-up (–2 marks):** Writing `front = front - 1` *without* the modular wraparound in a circular deque is a **fatal error**. The `+ N` and `% N` are both mandatory.
>
> 4. **Confusing Stack with Output-Restricted Deque (–1 mark):** A stack only allows `top` operations; an output-restricted deque allows **two insertion ends** but **one deletion end**. Confusing these loses a definition mark.
>
> 5. **Forgetting `count` in the Linked-List Version (–1 mark):** In the linked list implementation, you must still track size, either via a `count` field or by checking `head == NULL`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Double Ended Queues**

- **Definition:** A linear ADT supporting insertion AND deletion at BOTH front and rear.
- **Four Primitive Operations:** `insertFront`, `insertRear`, `deleteFront`, `deleteRear`.
- **Two Restricted Variants:** Input-Restricted (insert only at rear) and Output-Restricted (delete only at front).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ for all four mutations (both array and linked list).
- **Space Complexity:** $O(N)$ for array; $O(n)$ for linked list (with 2 pointers per node).
- **Circular Buffer Formulas:**
  - Insert at front: `front = (front - 1 + N) % N`
  - Insert at rear: `rear = (rear + 1) % N`
  - Delete at front: `front = (front + 1) % N`
  - Delete at rear: `rear = (rear - 1 + N) % N`
- **Empty/Full Discrimination:** Use the one-slot-sacrifice OR maintain an explicit `count` integer.
- **Size Formula:** `(rear - front + N) % N` (circular).
- **Key Engineering Applications:** Sliding window maximum, palindrome checking, A-Steal scheduling, browser history, undo/redo buffers, monotonic queue in DP.
- **Deque vs Stack vs Queue:** A Deque is a *superset* of both — it can simulate a Stack (insert/delete at one end) or a Queue (insert at rear, delete at front).
- **KTU High-Yield Topics for ESE:** (1) Circular array implementation, (2) Palindrome checker using output-restricted deque, (3) Empty/Full ambiguity resolution, (4) Comparison of array vs linked list implementation.
- **Common Pitfall:** The `front == rear` ambiguity — **always clarify** which strategy you adopt.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Expressions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Evaluation of Expressions # 📘 MODULE 1: EVALUATION OF EXPRESSIONS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Expression Evaluation** is the systematic process of parsing an arithmetic (or logical) expression written in a specific notation — **Infix**, **Prefix** (Polish Notation), or **Postfix** (Reverse Polish Notation) — and computing its final numerical result using a **stack-based algorithm**. In computer science, expressions can be represented in **three distinct notations** based on the position of the **operator** relative to its **operands**: | Notation | Format | Example | |----------|--------|---------| | **Infix** | `Operand Operator Operand` | `A + B` | | **Prefix** (Polish) | `Operator Operand Operand` | `+ A B` | | **Postfix** (Reverse Polish) | `Operand Operand Operator` | `A B +` | KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus mandates understanding all three notations, conversion algorithms between them, and the **stack-based evaluation methodology** which is foundational to compiler design. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Kitchen Recipe Stack 🥘** Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish. You have: - **Ingredients** (operands) on the table - **Cooking instructions** (operators: chop, boil, fry) In **Infix** (human style): *"Take onion **+** tomato"* — operators sit between ingredients. This is **easy for humans** but **hard for computers** because of **operator precedence** and **parentheses**. In **Postfix** (microwave style): *"Onion tomato +"* — everything is **lined up in a single queue**, and the microwave processes them **left-to-right using a holding tray (stack)**. This is **easy for computers** because **no precedence rules** are needed. In **Prefix**: *"+ onion tomato"* — instruction first, ingredients later. Also stack-evaluable. **The "tray" in the microwave is exactly the STACK data structure** — Last-In, First-Out (LIFO) memory that holds intermediate results. --- ### 1.3 Operator Precedence & Associativity > [!IMPORTANT] > Computers cannot "guess" precedence. They need a **precedence table** to resolve ambiguity in infix expressions. **Standard Precedence Hierarchy (Highest → Lowest):** | Precedence Level | Operators | Associativity | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | 3 (Highest) | `^` (Exponentiation) | Right → Left | | 2 | `*`, `/`, `%` | Left → Right | | 1 (Lowest) | `+`, `-` | Left → Right | **Special Symbols:** - `(` `)` — Parentheses (override precedence) - `#` — Sentinel / End-marker (used in some algorithms) --- ### 1.4 Why is Evaluation of Expressions Critical? - **Compiler Design**: The front-end of every compiler parses and evaluates expressions. - **Calculators & Spreadsheets**: Excel formulas, scientific calculators use stack-based evaluation. - **Programming Language Interpreters**: Python, JavaScript engines use postfix internally. - **CPU Instruction Sets**: RISC architectures use stack-based evaluation pipelines. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Algorithm ### A. Postfix Expression Evaluation (Using Stack) This is the **most frequently asked** algorithm in KTU exams. The stack stores **operands**, while operators trigger **computation**. **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Initialize an **empty stack**. 2. Scan the postfix expression **left-to-right**, **one symbol at a time**. 3. For each symbol: - **If operand** → `PUSH` onto stack. - **If operator** → `POP` two operands (`op1`, `op2`), compute `result = op2 ⊕ op1` (note the order!), `PUSH result`. 4. After scanning, the stack's **top element** is the final answer. > [!NOTE] > **Order Trap**: When popping, the **second popped** operand is the **left operand**. So `op2 - op1`, `op2 / op1` are critical for non-commutative operators. --- ### B. Prefix Expression Evaluation (Using Stack) 1. Initialize an **empty stack**. 2. Scan the prefix expression **right-to-left**. 3. For each symbol: - **If operand** → `PUSH` onto stack. - **If operator** → `POP` two operands (`op1`, `op2`), compute `result = op1 ⊕ op2`, `PUSH result`. 4. Stack top = final answer. --- ### C. Infix to Postfix Conversion (Shunting-Yard Algorithm by Dijkstra) **Algorithm Steps:** 1. Initialize an **empty stack** (for operators) and an **empty output queue**. 2. Scan the infix expression **left-to-right**. 3. For each token: - **If operand** → Append to output. - **If `(`** → Push onto stack. - **If `)`** → Pop and append all operators until `(` is found. Discard both parentheses. - **If operator** → Pop and append operators from stack that have **higher or equal precedence** (for left-associative), then push current operator. 4. After scanning, pop and append **all remaining operators**. --- ### D. Infix to Prefix Conversion **Strategy:** Reverse the infix string → Convert `(a + b) * c` → `(c * b + a)`-like form → Apply infix-to-postfix → Reverse the result. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize for KTU ESE** | Concept | Rule / Formula | Stack Action | |---------|---------------|--------------| | Postfix Eval — Operand | Encountered token is variable/value | `PUSH` value | | Postfix Eval — Operator `⊕` | Found binary operator | `POP op1, POP op2`, compute `op2 ⊕ op1`, `PUSH` | | Prefix Eval — Operand | Scanned right-to-left | `PUSH` value | | Prefix Eval — Operator | Scanned right-to-left | `POP op1, POP op2`, compute `op1 ⊕ op2`, `PUSH` | | Infix→Postfix — `( ` | Left parenthesis | `PUSH` onto stack | | Infix→Postfix — `)` | Right parenthesis | Pop until `(` found | | Infix→Postfix — Operator | Higher/equal precedence on stack top | Pop stack, output, then push current | | Associativity Rule | Left-assoc: `≤` pop; Right-assoc: `<` pop | `^` is right-associative | **Stack Underflow Condition**: Trying to pop from an empty stack → **Invalid Expression Error**. **Stack Overflow Detection**: Final answer not at top after scanning → **Invalid Expression**. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Compilers (GCC, LLVM)**: Parse `int x = a + b * c;` → convert to assembly using postfix trees. - **Embedded Systems**: Real-time microcontrollers evaluate sensor equations without heavy parsing. - **Database Query Engines**: SQL `WHERE` clause evaluation. - **Spreadsheet Engines**: Excel/Google Sheets formula evaluation. - **Calculators**: HP calculators historically used **RPN (Reverse Polish Notation)** natively. - **3D Graphics**: Matrix expression evaluation in shaders. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Postfix Evaluation **Problem:** Evaluate the postfix expression: $$8 \; 3 \; 4 \; * \; + \; 9 \; -$$ **Solution — Step-by-Step (Stack Trace):** | Step | Token Scanned | Stack State (bottom → top) | Action / Reasoning | |:----:|:------------:|:---------------------------|:-------------------| | 1 | `8` | `[8]` | Operand → PUSH | | 2 | `3` | `[8, 3]` | Operand → PUSH | | 3 | `4` | `[8, 3, 4]` | Operand → PUSH | | 4 | `*` | `[8, 12]` | POP `4` (op1), POP `3` (op2); Compute `3 * 4 = 12`; PUSH 12 | | 5 | `+` | `[20]` | POP `12` (op1), POP `8` (op2); Compute `8 + 12 = 20`; PUSH 20 | | 6 | `9` | `[20, 9]` | Operand → PUSH | | 7 | `-` | `[11]` | POP `9` (op1), POP `20` (op2); Compute `20 - 9 = 11`; PUSH 11 | **Final Result:** `11` **Equivalent Infix Verification:** $$8 + (3 \times 4) - 9 = 8 + 12 - 9 = 11 \; \checkmark$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Infix to Postfix Conversion **Problem:** Convert the infix expression to postfix: $$\left(A + B\right) \times C \; - \; D \; / \; E \; + \; F$$ **Precedence:** `×`, `/` (level 2) > `+`, `-` (level 1). All left-associative. | Step | Token | Stack | Output (Postfix) | Explanation | |:----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----------------|:------------| | 1 | `(` | `(` | — | Push `(` | | 2 | `A` | `(` | `A` | Operand → Output | | 3 | `+` | `( +` | `A` | Push `+` | | 4 | `B` | `( +` | `A B` | Operand → Output | | 5 | `)` | (empty) | `A B +` | Pop until `(`; both discarded | | 6 | `×` | `×` | `A B +` | Push `×` (stack empty, no popping) | | 7 | `C` | `×` | `A B + C` | Operand → Output | | 8 | `-` | `-` | `A B + C ×` | `×` has higher prec → pop `×`; push `-` | | 9 | `D` | `-` | `A B + C × D` | Operand → Output | | 10 | `/` | `- /` | `A B + C × D` | `/` prec > `-` → just push `/` | | 11 | `E` | `- /` | `A B + C × D E` | Operand → Output | | 12 | `+` | `+` | `A B + C × D E / -` | `/` ≥ `+` prec → pop `/`; `-` ≥ `+` prec → pop `-`; push `+` | | 13 | `F` | `+` | `A B + C × D E / - F` | Operand → Output | | 14 | End | — | `A B + C × D E / - F +` | Pop all → `+` | **Final Postfix:** $$A \; B \; + \; C \; \times \; D \; E \; / \; - \; F \; +$$ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Prefix Evaluation **Problem:** Evaluate the prefix expression: $$- \; \times \; + \; 5 \; 6 \; 4 \; 9$$ **Strategy:** Scan **right-to-left**. | Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Reasoning | |:----:|:-----:|:--------------------|:----------| | 1 | `9` | `[9]` | PUSH operand | | 2 | `4` | `[9, 4]` | PUSH operand | | 3 | `6` | `[9, 4, 6]` | PUSH operand | | 4 | `5` | `[9, 4, 6, 5]` | PUSH operand | | 5 | `+` | `[9, 4, 11]` | POP `5` (op1), POP `6` (op2); Compute `5 + 6 = 11`; PUSH | | 6 | `×` | `[9, 44]` | POP `11` (op1), POP `4` (op2); Compute `11 × 4 = 44`; PUSH | | 7 | `-` | `[-35]` | POP `44` (op1), POP `9` (op2); Compute `9 - 44 = -35`; PUSH | **Final Result:** `-35` **Verification — Infix equivalent:** $$9 - (5 + 6) \times 4 = 9 - 11 \times 4 = 9 - 44 = -35 \; \checkmark$$ --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Infix to Prefix Conversion **Problem:** Convert `(A + B) * C - D / E` to prefix. **Step 1 — Reverse the infix string** (and swap each parenthesis): $$E \; / \; D \; - \; C \; * \; ) \; B \; + \; A \; ($$ Rewritten with proper structure: `E / D - C * ( B + A )` **Step 2 — Apply Infix → Postfix on reversed:** $E \; D \; / \; C \; B \; A \; + \; * \; -$ **Step 3 — Reverse the postfix result:** $$- \; * \; + \; A \; B \; C \; / \; D \; E$$ **Final Prefix:** ` - * + A B C / D E` --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Operator Precedence Popping Rules | Current Operator Incoming | Stack Top Operator | Pop Stack? | Reason | |:------------------------:|:------------------:|:----------:|:-------| | `+`, `-` | `+`, `-` | ✅ Yes | Same precedence, left-assoc | | `+`, `-` | `×`, `/`, `^` | ✅ Yes | Higher precedence already on stack | | `×`, `/` | `+`, `-` | ❌ No | Lower precedence | | `^` | `^` | ❌ No | **Right-associative** — only `<`, not `≤` | | Any | `(` | ❌ No | Parentheses block popping | | `(` | Anything | ❌ No | Just push `(` | | `)` | Anything | ✅ Pop until `(` | Flush until matching `(` | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Postfix Evaluation Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Read Postfix Expression] --> B[Initialize Empty Stack] B --> C{Read Next Token} C -->|Operand| D[PUSH Token onto Stack] D --> E{More Tokens?} C -->|Operator OP| F[POP op1 from Stack] F --> G[POP op2 from Stack] G --> H[Compute Result = op2 OP op1] H --> I[PUSH Result onto Stack] I --> E E -->|Yes| C E -->|No| J[POP Top of Stack] J --> K[Output Final Result] K --> L[End] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Infix to Postfix Conversion (Shunting-Yard) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Read Infix Expression] --> B[Initialize Empty Stack and Output Queue] B --> C{Read Next Token T} C -->|Operand| D[Append T to Output] D --> E{More Tokens?} C -->|Open Parenthesis| F[PUSH T onto Stack] C -->|Close Parenthesis| G[POP from Stack to Output until Open Parenthesis found] G --> H[Discard the Open Parenthesis] H --> E C -->|Operator OP| I{Stack Empty or Top is Open Paren?} I -->|Yes| J[PUSH OP onto Stack] I -->|No| K{Top has Higher or Equal Precedence?} K -->|Yes| L[POP Top to Output] L --> I K -->|No| J J --> E F --> E E -->|Yes| C E -->|No| M[POP All Remaining Operators to Output] M --> N[Output Final Postfix Expression] N --> O[End] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Stack Trace Visualization (Postfix Eval of `8 3 4 * + 9 -`) ```mermaid flowchart LR step1["Step 1: Read 8
Stack: 8"] step2["Step 2: Read 3
Stack: 8, 3"] step3["Step 3: Read 4
Stack: 8, 3, 4"] step4["Step 4: Read *
Pop 4 and 3, Push 3*4=12
Stack: 8, 12"] step5["Step 5: Read +
Pop 12 and 8, Push 8+12=20
Stack: 20"] step6["Step 6: Read 9
Stack: 20, 9"] step7["Step 7: Read -
Pop 9 and 20, Push 20-9=11
Stack: 11"] result["Final Result: 11"] step1 --> step2 --> step3 --> step4 --> step5 --> step6 --> step7 --> result ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Expression Evaluation Engine ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] I1["Infix Notation
(Human-Readable)"] I2["Postfix Notation
(RPN)"] I3["Prefix Notation
(Polish)"] end subgraph ParserLayer["Parser Layer"] P1["Tokenizer
Lexical Analysis"] P2["Syntax Analyzer
Precedence Resolver"] end subgraph ConversionLayer["Conversion Engine"] C1["Infix to Postfix
Shunting-Yard Algorithm"] C2["Infix to Prefix
Reverse Strategy"] end subgraph EvaluationLayer["Stack-Based Evaluator"] E1["Operand Stack"] E2["Operator Stack"] E3["Arithmetic Logic Unit
Computes op2 OP op1"] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"] O1["Numerical Result"] O2["Error Handler
Underflow / Overflow / Invalid"] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> C1 P2 --> C2 C1 --> E1 C2 --> E1 E1 --> E3 E2 --> E3 E3 --> O1 E3 --> O2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram shows how **real compilers** are architected. The Tokenizer breaks input into atoms; the Precedence Resolver handles order; the Stack-Based Evaluator performs the actual computation. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Differentiate between infix, prefix, and postfix notations with examples.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Infix | Prefix (Polish) | Postfix (Reverse Polish) | |---------|-------|----------------|--------------------------| | Operator Position | Between operands | Before operands | After operands | | Example | `A + B` | `+ A B` | `A B +` | | Human Readability | Highest | Low | Medium | | Computer Evaluation | Needs precedence rules | Needs stack | Needs stack (easiest) | | Parentheses Required | Yes | No | No | Infix is what humans write; **prefix and postfix eliminate the need for parentheses** because operator position inherently encodes precedence. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Why are postfix expressions preferred over infix expressions for computer evaluation?** **Model Answer:** 1. **No precedence parsing required** — order of operations is encoded by token position itself. 2. **No parentheses needed** — the structure is unambiguous. 3. **Single left-to-right scan** suffices, using a simple stack. 4. **Faster execution** in interpreters and compilers. 5. **Used in RISC architectures** and historical HP calculators. --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout ⚠️** > - **Operand Order Trap**: In postfix evaluation, after popping `op1` and `op2`, the formula is `op2 ⊕ op1`, NOT `op1 ⊕ op2`. Forgetting this loses **3 marks** typically. > - **Stack Pointer Direction**: Always show stack with **bottom on left, top on right**. Reverse direction loses presentation marks. > - **Missed Parentheses**: In infix-to-postfix, popping must continue until `(` is found (inclusive); many students stop one step early. > - **Empty Stack Error**: Popping when stack is empty = **invalid expression**. KTU examiners award **0 marks** if not caught. > - **Prefix Right-to-Left**: Forgetting to scan **right-to-left** in prefix evaluation is the #1 reason for incorrect answers. --- #### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Convert the following infix expression to **postfix** form using a stack. Show the stack status at every step. $$A \; + \; \left(B \; \times \; C\right) \; - \; \left(D \; / \; E\right) \; \wedge \; F$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Precedence: `^` (3) > `×`, `/` (2) > `+`, `-` (1). Left-associative for `+`, `-`, `×`, `/`; right-associative for `^`. | Step | Token | Stack | Output | |:----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-------| | 1 | `A` | — | `A` | | 2 | `+` | `+` | `A` | | 3 | `(` | `+ (` | `A` | | 4 | `B` | `+ (` | `A B` | | 5 | `×` | `+ ( ×` | `A B` | | 6 | `C` | `+ ( ×` | `A B C` | | 7 | `)` | `+` | `A B C ×` | | 8 | `-` | `-` | `A B C × +` (pop `+` due to ≥ precedence) | | 9 | `(` | `- (` | `A B C × +` | | 10 | `D` | `- (` | `A B C × + D` | | 11 | `/` | `- ( /` | `A B C × + D` | | 12 | `E` | `- ( /` | `A B C × + D E` | | 13 | `)` | `-` | `A B C × + D E /` | | 14 | `^` | `- ^` | `A B C × + D E /` | | 15 | `F` | `- ^` | `A B C × + D E / F` | | 16 | End | — | `A B C × + D E / F ^ -` | **Final Postfix:** $$A \; B \; C \; \times \; + \; D \; E \; / \; F \; \wedge \; -$$ --- **(b)** Evaluate the following **postfix** expression. Show the stack contents at each step. $$5 \; 6 \; 2 \; + \; \times \; 3 \; - \; 4 \; / \; 2 \; \wedge$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** | Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Action | |:----:|:-----:|:---------------------|:-------| | 1 | `5` | `[5]` | PUSH | | 2 | `6` | `[5, 6]` | PUSH | | 3 | `2` | `[5, 6, 2]` | PUSH | | 4 | `+` | `[5, 8]` | POP `2, 6`; `6+2=8`; PUSH | | 5 | `×` | `[40]` | POP `8, 5`; `5×8=40`; PUSH | | 6 | `3` | `[40, 3]` | PUSH | | 7 | `-` | `[37]` | POP `3, 40`; `40-3=37`; PUSH | | 8 | `4` | `[37, 4]` | PUSH | | 9 | `/` | `[9]` | POP `4, 37`; `37/4=9` (integer div); PUSH | | 10 | `2` | `[9, 2]` | PUSH | | 11 | `^` | `[81]` | POP `2, 9`; `9^2=81`; PUSH | **Final Result:** `81` --- #### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **(a)** Convert the following infix expression to **prefix** notation, showing all intermediate steps. $$\left(\left(A + B\right) \times C\right) \; - \; \left(D / E\right) \; + \; F$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Method — Reverse Infix, Convert to Postfix, Reverse:** **Step 1:** Original infix: $$\left(\left(A + B\right) \times C\right) - \left(D / E\right) + F$$ **Step 2:** Reverse the string and swap parentheses: $$F + \left(E / D\right) - \left(C \times \left(B + A\right)\right)$$ **Step 3:** Convert to postfix: | Token | Stack | Output | |:-----:|:-----:|:-------| | `F` | — | `F` | | `+` | `+` | `F` | | `(` | `+ (` | `F` | | `E` | `+ (` | `F E` | | `/` | `+ ( /` | `F E` | | `D` | `+ ( /` | `F E D` | | `)` | `+` | `F E D /` | | `-` | `-` | `F E D / +` (pop `+`) | | `(` | `- (` | `F E D / +` | | `C` | `- (` | `F E D / + C` | | `×` | `- ( ×` | `F E D / + C` | | `(` | `- ( × (` | `F E D / + C` | | `B` | `- ( × (` | `F E D / + C B` | | `+` | `- ( × ( +` | `F E D / + C B` | | `A` | `- ( × ( +` | `F E D / + C B A` | | `)` | `- ( ×` | `F E D / + C B A +` | | `)` | `-` | `F E D / + C B A + ×` | Postfix of reversed: `F E D / + C B A + × -` **Step 4:** Reverse the postfix: $$- \; \times \; + \; A \; B \; C \; + \; / \; D \; E \; F$$ **Final Prefix:** $$\boxed{- \; \times \; + \; A \; B \; C \; + \; / \; D \; E \; F}$$ --- **(b)** Evaluate the following **prefix** expression. Show the stack at each step. $$+ \; - \; \times \; 2 \; 3 \; 4 \; / \; 8 \; 2$$ **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Scan **right-to-left**. | Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Action | |:----:|:-----:|:---------------------|:-------| | 1 | `2` | `[2]` | PUSH | | 2 | `8` | `[2, 8]` | PUSH | | 3 | `/` | `[4]` | POP `8, 2`; `8/2=4`; PUSH | | 4 | `4` | `[4, 4]` | PUSH | | 5 | `3` | `[4, 4, 3]` | PUSH | | 6 | `2` | `[4, 4, 3, 2]` | PUSH | | 7 | `×` | `[4, 8]` | POP `2, 3`; `3×2=6`; PUSH — *wait recheck* | **Recheck Step 7:** Stack was `[4, 4, 3, 2]`. Pop `2` (op1), Pop `3` (op2); compute `op1 × op2 = 2 × 3 = 6`. PUSH `6`. Stack: `[4, 4, 6]` | 8 | `-` | `[4, -2]` | POP `6, 4`; `6 - 4 = 2`? No — `op1 - op2 = 6 - 4 = 2`. Wait: op1 was popped first. Let me redo. | **Corrected Steps 7–9:** | Step | Token | Stack (bottom → top) | Action | |:----:|:-----:|:---------------------|:-------| | 7 | `×` | `[4, 4, 6]` | POP `2` (op1), POP `3` (op2); `op1 ⊕ op2 = 2 × 3 = 6`; PUSH | | 8 | `-` | `[4, -2]` | POP `6` (op1), POP `4` (op2); `op1 - op2 = 6 - 4 = 2`? | Wait, the stack before step 8 was `[4, 4, 6]`. We pop `6` first (op1), then `4` (op2). For prefix, formula is `op1 ⊕ op2`. So `6 - 4 = 2`. Hmm but that gives `2`, not `-2`. Let me recheck the stack flow. Actually stack before step 7 = `[4, 4, 3, 2]`. Pop top = `2` (op1), pop next = `3` (op2). Compute `op1 × op2 = 2 × 3 = 6`. Push `6`. Stack: `[4, 4, 6]`. Step 8: Token `-`. Pop top = `6` (op1), pop next = `4` (op2). Compute `op1 - op2 = 6 - 4 = 2`. Push `2`. Stack: `[4, 2]`. | 9 | `+` | `[6]` | POP `2` (op1), POP `4` (op2); `op1 + op2 = 2 + 4 = 6`; PUSH | **Final Result:** `6` **Verification:** Original prefix `+ - × 2 3 4 / 8 2` ≡ `((2×3 - 4) + (8/2))` = `(6 - 4) + 4 = 2 + 4 = 6` ✓ --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE** - ✅ **Three notations**: Infix (`a+b`), Prefix (`+ab`), Postfix (`ab+`). - ✅ **Postfix Evaluation**: Scan **left-to-right**; operands → `PUSH`; operator → `POP` two, compute `op2 ⊕ op1`, `PUSH` result. - ✅ **Prefix Evaluation**: Scan **right-to-left**; same logic but formula is `op1 ⊕ op2`. - ✅ **Infix → Postfix**: Use stack for operators; use output queue for operands; respect precedence and associativity. - ✅ **Precedence**: `^` > `× /` > `+ -`. Associativity: `^` is right; others are left. - ✅ **Parentheses in conversion**: `( ` always pushes; `)` flushes stack until matching `(`. - ✅ **Infix → Prefix**: Reverse infix → swap parentheses → convert to postfix → reverse result. - ✅ **Stack underflow** = invalid expression; **stack overflow** at end = invalid expression. - ✅ **Order trap**: Always remember which popped value is `op1` vs `op2` — examiners test this every year. - ✅ **Application areas**: Compilers, calculators, interpreters, CPU pipelines, database engines. - ✅ **KTU weightage**: Expect 1 Part-A question (3 marks) + 1 Part-B question (7–14 marks) on this topic.

topics.title: Self-Referential Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Self-Referential Structures ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Self-Referential Structure** is a user-defined data type (typically a `struct` in C/C++) that contains a **member which is a pointer to another structure of the same type**. In essence, the structure definition references itself through a pointer — not by embedding another full structure (which would cause infinite recursion in memory), but by storing the *memory address* of another instance of itself. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2 of Data Structures and Algorithms), this concept is treated as the **foundational building block** for all dynamic, non-contiguous data structures such as **linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, and graphs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Conceptual):** > A structure that contains a pointer member of its own type is called a *self-referential structure*. It allows individual nodes to be linked together dynamically at runtime, enabling flexible memory usage that static arrays cannot provide. The general C-style syntax is: ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; // Pointer to another struct Node of the same type }; ``` ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train of Bogies" Imagine a **train** as a sequence of connected **bogies (coaches)**. - Each **bogie** is a structure that holds two things: 1. **Passengers (data)** — useful payload. 2. **A coupling hook (pointer)** — a link to the next bogie. - A bogie does **not** physically contain the next bogie inside it (that would be impossible and absurd). Instead, it simply *knows where the next bogie is* via the hook. - The **last bogie's hook is `NULL`** (empty hook), signalling the end of the train. - Bogies can be **added or removed** at runtime — the train is not fixed in size at the time of manufacture. Similarly, a self-referential structure stores **data + an address pointing to the next node of the same kind**. The chain terminates when a pointer stores `NULL` (the value `0` representing "nowhere"). > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction — Why a Pointer and Not an Embedded Structure?** > If a structure contained another full structure of its own type, the compiler would allocate **infinite memory** (a structure that contains itself contains another, and so on, ad infinitum). A pointer has a **fixed size** (typically **8 bytes** on 64-bit systems, **4 bytes** on 32-bit systems), breaking this infinite regression cleanly. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Typical Value | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Pointer size (64-bit) | **8 bytes** | Holds memory address | | Pointer size (32-bit) | **4 bytes** | Holds memory address | | `NULL` value | **0** | Sentinel marking "no next node" | | Header `` | Required for `malloc`/`free` | Dynamic memory allocation | | Header `` | Required for `printf`/`scanf` | I/O operations | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Self-Referential Structure Functions A self-referential structure operates on the following logical steps during program execution: 1. **Definition Phase (Compile Time)** - The `struct` declaration introduces a *blueprint*, not actual memory. The compiler records the layout: `data` occupies, say, 4 bytes; `next` occupies 8 bytes. **Total node size = sizeof(data) + sizeof(pointer)**, often padded to alignment boundaries. 2. **Instantiation Phase (Runtime)** - Individual nodes are created dynamically using `malloc(sizeof(struct Node))` which returns a `void *` to a freshly allocated memory block in the **heap**. 3. **Linking Phase (Runtime)** - The `next` pointer of node A is assigned the address of node B. This creates a **logical link**, not a physical one — nodes can be scattered anywhere in memory. 4. **Traversal Phase (Runtime)** - Starting from a `head` pointer, we follow `next` pointers until `NULL` is encountered, mimicking iteration over a sequence. 5. **Deallocation Phase (Runtime)** - Each node must be explicitly released using `free()` to prevent **memory leaks** — a common KTU exam pitfall. ### 2.2 Why Self-Referential Structures? The "Why" Behind the Concept - **Dynamic Size**: Unlike arrays, the number of nodes is not fixed at compile time. The structure grows/shrinks as the program runs. - **Efficient Insertion/Deletion**: Inserting or deleting a node requires changing only a few pointer assignments — **O(1)** time in linked lists, vs. **O(n)** for arrays due to shifting. - **No Wasted Memory**: Memory is allocated only when needed, unlike arrays which may reserve unused slots. - **Foundation for Advanced Structures**: Without self-referential structures, **linked lists, stacks, queues, trees, graphs, hash tables, and adjacency lists** would all be impossible to implement. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Operating Systems**: Process control blocks in kernel schedulers use linked lists. > - **Memory Allocators**: Free-block tracking in `malloc` uses self-referential structures. > - **File Systems**: FAT and linked-file systems store next-cluster pointers using this exact pattern. > - **Compilers**: Symbol tables and Abstract Syntax Trees (ASTs) rely heavily on self-referential nodes. > - **Networking**: Routing tables and adjacency lists in graph algorithms. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---|---| | Size of node (unaligned) | $\text{sizeof(node)} = \text{sizeof(data fields)} + \text{sizeof(pointer)}$ | Pointer is typically 8 B (64-bit) | | Size of node (aligned) | Rounded up to nearest multiple of largest alignment | Compiler may add padding bytes | | Number of nodes reachable from `head` | Traced by following `next` until `NULL` | Each `next` is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ dereference | | Total memory used | $n \times \text{sizeof(node)}$ where $n$ is node count | Plus per-node heap-allocation overhead | | NULL check | `if (ptr == NULL)` or `if (ptr != NULL)` | Always check before dereferencing | | Pointer arithmetic cost | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per dereference | Independent of list size | | Traversal cost (full list) | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Must visit every node sequentially | > [!NOTE] > **Common Pointers Used:** > - `head` — points to the first node. > - `tail` — points to the last node (optional, used for efficient appends). > - `NULL` — the terminator, conventionally value **0**. ### 2.4 Pointer-to-Same-Type Semantics A self-referential structure may contain **more than one** pointer to itself. For example, a **binary tree node** uses two self-pointers: ```c struct TreeNode { int data; struct TreeNode *left; struct TreeNode *right; }; ``` And a **doubly linked list node** uses two: ```c struct DNode { int data; struct DNode *prev; struct DNode *next; }; ``` This branching capability is what allows self-referential structures to model **non-linear** relationships — the entire field of tree and graph data structures rests on this single idea. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Memory Layout of a Self-Referential Node Let us derive the **exact byte-level memory layout** of a typical singly linked list node on a 64-bit system. **Given:** - `int data` → 4 bytes - `struct Node *next` → 8 bytes (64-bit pointer) - Default alignment on x86-64: `int` aligned to 4 B, `pointer` aligned to 8 B **Step 1: Determine layout order** The compiler lays out members in **declaration order**. $$\text{Offset of } data = 0$$ $$\text{Offset of } next = 4 \text{ (after data, but pointer needs 8-byte alignment)}$$ Since `data` ends at offset 4, and the next member (`next`) requires 8-byte alignment, the compiler inserts **4 padding bytes** to push `next` to offset 8. $$\text{Offset of } next = 8$$ **Step 2: Total node size** The structure extends from offset 0 to offset $8 + 8 = 16$. Since the largest alignment requirement is 8 bytes, the total size is rounded up to the next multiple of 8. $$\text{sizeof(struct Node)} = 16 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 3: Memory consumption formula for n nodes** $$\text{Total memory} = n \times \text{sizeof(struct Node)} + \text{heap overhead}$$ For a typical `malloc` implementation, each allocation has an internal header (often 8–16 bytes per block) used by the allocator.
