For KTU problems, we approximate: $$\text{Total memory} \approx n \times 16 \text{ bytes (ignoring allocator overhead)}$$ ### 3.2 Step-by-Step — Creating and Linking Three Nodes Let us walk through the **complete manual construction** of a 3-node linked list: `10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`. **Step 1: Allocate first node** ```c struct Node *head = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); head->data = 10; head->next = NULL; ``` At this moment, the heap contains one node holding `10`, with `next = NULL`. **Step 2: Allocate second node** ```c struct Node *second = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); second->data = 20; second->next = NULL; ``` **Step 3: Link head to second** ```c head->next = second; ``` Now `head->next` holds the **address** of `second`. The two nodes are logically connected. **Step 4: Allocate third node** ```c struct Node *third = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); third->data = 30; third->next = NULL; ``` **Step 5: Link second to third** ```c second->next = third; ``` **Step 6: Final chain state** $$head \rightarrow \boxed{10 \;\vert\; \cdot} \rightarrow \boxed{20 \;\vert\; \cdot} \rightarrow \boxed{30 \;\vert\; NULL}$$ **Step 7: Traversal (proof of linkage)** ```c struct Node *current = head; while (current != NULL) { printf("%d ", current->data); current = current->next; } ``` Iteration trace: | Iteration | `current` points to | `current->data` | `current->next` | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Node 1 | 10 | Address of Node 2 | | 2 | Node 2 | 20 | Address of Node 3 | | 3 | Node 3 | 30 | NULL | | 4 | NULL (loop exits) | — | — | **Output:** `10 20 30` ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | Self-Referential Structure (Linked) | Static Array | |---|---|---| | Memory allocation | Dynamic (`malloc`) | Static / Stack | | Size known at compile time? | **No** | **Yes** | | Memory contiguity | Scattered (non-contiguous) | Contiguous block | | Random access by index | **Not supported** — $\mathcal{O}(n)$ traversal | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | | Insertion at head | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (shifting) | | Deletion at head | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (shifting) | | Memory overhead per element | Extra pointer (8 B) | None | | Risk of memory leak | **Yes** (if `free` forgotten) | No | | Cache locality | Poor (scattered) | Excellent | | Implementation complexity | Higher | Lower | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** When answering theory questions, students often confuse *contiguous* (array) with *linked* (self-referential) memory. Always state explicitly: **"A self-referential structure creates a logically linked but physically non-contiguous collection of nodes."** ### 3.4 Derivation — Why sizeof(struct Node) Cannot Be Determined Recursively Consider the (illegal) definition: ```c struct Bad { int data; struct Bad child; // ILLEGAL — infinite size }; ``` If this were allowed, the compiler would attempt to compute: $$\text{sizeof(struct Bad)} = \text{sizeof(int)} + \text{sizeof(struct Bad)}$$ Let $S = \text{sizeof(struct Bad)}$: $$S = 4 + S$$ Solving: $$S - S = 4$$ $$0 = 4 \quad \text{(Contradiction!)}$$ This algebraic impossibility is precisely **why a pointer is mandatory**. A pointer introduces a known, fixed size: $$S = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes (with possible padding to 16 B)}$$ The pointer **breaks the recursive cycle** by introducing a *known terminal size*. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Structure of a Single Self-Referential Node ```mermaid graph LR A["struct Node
Address: 0x1000"] --> B["data: int
Offset 0
4 bytes"] A --> C["next: pointer
Offset 8
8 bytes"] C -.-> D["Address: 0x2000
Next Node or NULL"] D --> E["data: int
Value 20"] D --> F["next: pointer
Address 0x3000"] F -.-> G["Address: 0x3000
Final Node"] G --> H["data: int
Value 30"] G --> I["next: pointer
NULL"] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Linked List Operations ```mermaid graph TB subgraph CompileTime["COMPILE TIME - Blueprint"] CT1["struct Node definition
members: data, next"] end subgraph RunTime["RUNTIME - Execution"] RT1["malloc sizeof Node 1
data=10, next=NULL"] RT2["malloc sizeof Node 2
data=20, next=NULL"] RT3["malloc sizeof Node 3
data=30, next=NULL"] RT4["Linking: head.next = Node2"] RT5["Linking: Node2.next = Node3"] RT6["Traverse from head
Follow next pointers
Stop at NULL"] end subgraph Termination["TERMINATION - Cleanup"] TM1["free Node3"] TM2["free Node2"] TM3["free Node1
Set head=NULL"] end CT1 --> RT1 RT1 --> RT2 RT2 --> RT3 RT3 --> RT4 RT4 --> RT5 RT5 --> RT6 RT6 --> TM1 TM1 --> TM2 TM2 --> TM3 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Singly vs Doubly Self-Referential Structure ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SLL["SINGLY LINKED NODE"] S1["data A"] --- S2["next -->"] end subgraph DLL["DOUBLY LINKED NODE"] D1["prev <--"] --- D2["data B"] --- D3["next -->"] end subgraph TREE["BINARY TREE NODE"] T1["data C"] T2["left -->"] T3["right -->"] T1 --- T2 T1 --- T3 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The singly linked node has **one self-pointer** (`next`). The doubly linked node has **two self-pointers** (`prev` and `next`). The binary tree node has **two self-pointers** (`left` and `right`). All three are *self-referential* — they reference their own type via pointers rather than by value. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define a self-referential structure. Give a suitable C example and state one advantage over a static array.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **self-referential structure** is a `struct` that contains a **pointer member** referring to a structure of the **same type**. It does not embed another full structure (which would cause infinite memory), but only stores its address. ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` **Advantage over static array:** The number of nodes is **not fixed at compile time**; nodes can be created and linked dynamically at runtime using `malloc`, allowing the data structure to grow or shrink as needed without the memory waste of a pre-allocated array. *(Marking: 1 mark definition, 1 mark code, 1 mark advantage)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Why is it not possible for a structure to contain an instance of itself as a member? Explain with a memory-size argument.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** If a structure contained a full instance of itself as a member, the size calculation would lead to a **recursive equation with no base case**. $$S = \text{sizeof(int)} + S$$ $$\Rightarrow S = 4 + S \Rightarrow 0 = 4$$ This is a **contradiction**, meaning the compiler cannot determine a finite memory size. To avoid this, we use a **pointer** (fixed size of 8 bytes on 64-bit systems) instead of an embedded structure. The pointer breaks the recursion and yields a finite size: $$S = 4 + 8 = 12 \text{ bytes (or 16 B with alignment padding)}$$ *(Marking: 1 mark stating the problem, 1 mark algebraic contradiction, 1 mark pointer solution)* --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Style]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a self-referential structure in C with a neat diagram. Show how it forms the basis of a singly linked list by constructing a 3-node list with data values 5, 15, and 25. **(b) [7 Marks)** Write a C program to create a singly linked list of `n` integers, display all elements, and compute the sum of all elements. Use the self-referential structure concept. **Model Solution:** **(a) Explanation with Diagram (7 marks):** A self-referential structure is a structure that includes a **pointer to a structure of the same type** as one of its members. This pointer enables individual nodes to be linked together at runtime, forming dynamic data structures such as linked lists, trees, and graphs. **C Definition:** ```c struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; ``` **Construction of 3-node list (5 → 15 → 25 → NULL):** ```c #include #include struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; int main() { struct Node *head = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); struct Node *second = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); struct Node *third = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); head->data = 5; head->next = second; second->data = 15; second->next = third; third->data = 25; third->next = NULL; struct Node *p = head; while (p != NULL) { printf("%d -> ", p->data); p = p->next; } printf("NULL\n"); free(third); free(second); free(head); return 0; } ``` **Logical Diagram:** ``` head | v +------+------+ +------+------+ +------+------+ | 5 | o---+----> | 15 | o---+----> | 25 | NULL | +------+------+ +------+------+ +------+------+ ``` *(Mark distribution: 2 marks definition, 2 marks code for 3 nodes, 2 marks diagram, 1 mark traversal output)* --- **(b) C Program — Create, Display, and Sum (7 marks):** ```c #include #include struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; }; int main() { struct Node *head = NULL, *temp = NULL, *newNode = NULL; int n, value, sum = 0; printf("Enter number of nodes: "); scanf("%d", &n); for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { printf("Enter value for node %d: ", i + 1); scanf("%d", &value); newNode = (struct Node *)malloc(sizeof(struct Node)); newNode->data = value; newNode->next = NULL; if (head == NULL) { head = newNode; // First node temp = head; } else { temp->next = newNode; temp = newNode; } } printf("\nLinked List: "); temp = head; while (temp != NULL) { printf("%d -> ", temp->data); sum += temp->data; temp = temp->next; } printf("NULL\n"); printf("Sum of all elements = %d\n", sum); // Free memory temp = head; while (temp != NULL) { struct Node *toFree = temp; temp = temp->next; free(toFree); } return 0; } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter number of nodes: 4 Enter value for node 1: 10 Enter value for node 2: 20 Enter value for node 3: 30 Enter value for node 4: 40 Linked List: 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> NULL Sum of all elements = 100 ``` *(Mark distribution: 2 marks structure definition, 2 marks creation loop, 2 marks display + sum logic, 1 mark output and deallocation)* --- #### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]* **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare arrays and self-referential (linked) structures across **at least five parameters** (memory layout, access time, insertion cost, deletion cost, size flexibility). **(b) [7 Marks]** Design a C structure for a **student record** where each student node contains `rollNo`, `name`, and a pointer to the **next** student. Write a function `insertAtEnd` that appends a new student to the end of such a list. **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparison Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | Array | Self-Referential (Linked) Structure | |---|---|---| | **Memory Layout** | Contiguous block of memory | Non-contiguous, scattered across heap | | **Size at Compile Time** | Fixed and must be known | Dynamic, decided at runtime | | **Random Access** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ via index | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — sequential traversal only | | **Insertion (at head)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift all elements | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — update one pointer | | **Deletion (at head)** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift all elements | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — update one pointer | | **Memory Overhead** | None (just data) | Extra pointer per node (8 B on 64-bit) | | **Cache Performance** | Excellent (spatial locality) | Poor (random heap locations) | | **Risk of Memory Leak** | None | Yes — must `free` every node | **Conclusion:** Arrays win for **random access and cache efficiency**; linked structures win for **dynamic size and cheap insertions/deletions**. The choice depends on the problem's access pattern. *(Mark distribution: 1 mark per correct row × 5 rows, 2 marks for conclusion and reasoning)* --- **(b) Student Record Structure + `insertAtEnd` Function (7 marks):** ```c #include #include #include struct Student { int rollNo; char name[50]; struct Student *next; // Self-referential pointer }; void insertAtEnd(struct Student **headRef, int roll, const char *name) { // Step 1: Allocate new node struct Student *newNode = (struct Student *)malloc(sizeof(struct Student)); newNode->rollNo = roll; strcpy(newNode->name, name); newNode->next = NULL; // Step 2: If list is empty, new node becomes head if (*headRef == NULL) { *headRef = newNode; return; } // Step 3: Traverse to the last node struct Student *temp = *headRef; while (temp->next != NULL) { temp = temp->next; } // Step 4: Link new node at the end temp->next = newNode; } ``` **Why a double pointer (`struct Student **headRef`)?** A single pointer passed by value would only modify a *local copy* of `head`. When inserting the first node, we need to modify the *original* head pointer in the caller. A **pointer to a pointer** allows the function to update `head` itself when the list is initially empty. **Helper Display Function:** ```c void display(struct Student *head) { struct Student *temp = head; printf("\nStudent List:\n"); while (temp != NULL) { printf("Roll: %d Name: %s\n", temp->rollNo, temp->name); temp = temp->next; } } ``` **Sample Usage:** ```c int main() { struct Student *head = NULL; insertAtEnd(&head, 101, "Arjun"); insertAtEnd(&head, 102, "Meera"); insertAtEnd(&head, 103, "Rahul"); display(head); return 0; } ``` **Output:** ``` Student List: Roll: 101 Name: Arjun Roll: 102 Name: Meera Roll: 103 Name: Rahul ``` *(Mark distribution: 2 marks structure definition, 3 marks `insertAtEnd` logic (empty case + traversal + link), 1 mark double-pointer justification, 1 mark display or test run)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting `free()`** — Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for memory leaks in linked list programs. Always show a clean-up loop. > 2. **Not checking `malloc` return value** — If `malloc` returns `NULL` (out-of-memory) and you dereference it, the program crashes. Examiners expect a `NULL` check after every `malloc`. > 3. **Confusing `*` and `->`** — `ptr.data` is **wrong**; use `ptr->data` for pointer access. Mixing these up is a guaranteed mark loser. > 4. **Forgetting to include headers** — `` for `malloc`/`free`, `` for `printf`/`scanf`. Missing these is a common compile-error in student answers. > 5. **Not terminating with `NULL`** — The last node's `next` must be `NULL`. Forgetting this causes infinite loops during traversal. > 6. **Confusing "self-referential" with "recursive function"** — A self-referential structure is a *data definition*; a recursive function is a *code pattern*. Examiners often test this distinction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Self-Referential Structures** - ✅ A **self-referential structure** is a `struct` containing a **pointer to its own type** — never an embedded full instance. - ✅ The pointer **breaks the infinite-size recursion** that would otherwise occur (`S = 4 + S ⇒ contradiction`). - ✅ The canonical form is: `struct Node { int data; struct Node *next; };` - ✅ Memory is allocated dynamically on the **heap** using `malloc(sizeof(struct Node))`. - ✅ The chain terminates when a pointer holds `NULL` (value **0**). - ✅ Always **check** if `malloc` returned `NULL` before dereferencing. - ✅ Always **`free`** every node to prevent memory leaks. - ✅ **Size of a node** ≈ `sizeof(data fields) + sizeof(pointer)` + alignment padding (typically **16 bytes** on 64-bit for an `int` + pointer node). - ✅ **Multiple self-pointers** are allowed: doubly linked lists use 2, binary trees use 2, graphs may use many. - ✅ **Advantages over arrays**: dynamic size, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ insert/delete at head, no wasted memory. - ✅ **Disadvantages vs arrays**: no random access ($\mathcal{O}(n)$ traversal), extra pointer overhead, poor cache locality, manual memory management. - ✅ **Real-world applications**: OS process schedulers, memory allocators, file systems (FAT), compiler symbol tables, routing tables. - ✅ **Header files required**: `` for I/O, `` for `malloc`/`free`. - ✅ The `head` pointer is the **entry point** — losing it means losing the entire list (a common exam trap). - ✅ Use `->` operator for pointer-to-struct access; use `.` only for direct struct variables.

topics.title: Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Memory Allocation (DMA)** is a memory management mechanism in which the size of the data structure (or the storage required by a program) is determined **at runtime** rather than at compile-time, allowing the program to request memory from the **Heap** (free store) as and when required, and to release it back when no longer needed.

In the **C programming language**, DMA is performed using four standard library functions declared in `<stdlib.h>`:

| Function | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `malloc()` | Allocates a block of **n bytes** of *uninitialized* memory |
| `calloc()` | Allocates a block of memory for an **array** and *zero-initializes* it |
| `realloc()` | Resizes a previously allocated memory block |
| `free()` | Deallocates a previously allocated block and returns it to the heap |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must understand the difference between **Stack** (automatic, compile-time) and **Heap** (dynamic, runtime) allocation, the role of the `new` / `delete` operators in C++, and the *pros & cons* of static vs dynamic allocation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Lockers" Analogy

Imagine a railway station with two kinds of storage:

1. **Fixed Lockers (Static / Stack Memory)** — You must declare the locker size at the counter before travelling. If you pick a small locker, your bag doesn't fit. If you pick a large one, you waste money.

2. **Smart Lockers (Dynamic / Heap Memory)** — You walk up to a machine, enter the size you need *right now*, swipe your card, and a locker of *exactly* that size is assigned to you. When you leave, you tap "release" and the locker becomes available for the next traveller.

> The **smart locker machine** is the **heap manager**; your **card** is the **pointer**; the **locker number** printed on the receipt is the **memory address**.

## 1.3 The Two Memory Regions at a Glance

- **Stack Memory**: Allocates/deallocates automatically via function calls. **Fast**, **limited**, and follows **LIFO**.
- **Heap Memory**: Allocates/deallocates manually by the programmer. **Slower** (managed by the OS), **larger**, **fragmented** if misused.

> [!TIP]
> Use the stack for small, short-lived variables (e.g., loop counters). Use the heap for large, long-lived data (e.g., linked lists, trees, dynamically-sized arrays).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Four Functions

### 2.1.1 `malloc(size)` — Memory Allocation
- **Header**: `void *malloc(size_t size);`
- **Returns**: A `void *` pointer to the **first byte** of the allocated block, or `NULL` on failure.
- **Content**: The memory is **uninitialized** — contains **garbage** (whatever was previously in that heap slot).
- **Time complexity**: $O(1)$ on average (best-fit allocator), $O(n)$ worst-case for first-fit search.

### 2.1.2 `calloc(count, size)` — Contiguous Allocation
- **Header**: `void *calloc(size_t count, size_t size);`
- **Returns**: A pointer to memory sufficient for an array of `count` elements, each of `size` bytes, or `NULL`.
- **Content**: **All bits are zeroed** — guaranteed initialization to `0` (for integers) and `0.0` (for floats).
- **Mathematically equivalent to**: $\text{bytes requested} = \text{count} \times \text{size}$.

### 2.1.3 `realloc(ptr, newSize)` — Re-Allocation
- **Header**: `void *realloc(void *ptr, size_t newSize);`
- **Behaviour**:
  1. If `ptr` is `NULL`, behaves like `malloc(newSize)`.
  2. If `newSize` is `0`, behaves like `free(ptr)` (in modern C).
  3. Otherwise, attempts to **resize in-place**; if not possible, allocates a new block, **copies old data**, **frees old block**, and returns the new pointer.
- **Critical rule**: Always assign the result to a **temporary pointer** first to avoid *memory leaks* if `realloc` fails.

### 2.1.4 `free(ptr)` — Deallocation
- **Header**: `void free(void *ptr);`
- **Returns**: Nothing (`void`).
- **Behaviour**: Returns the block to the free list. After `free(ptr)`, using `ptr` is **undefined behaviour** (a classic *dangling pointer* bug).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Function Call | Bytes Requested | Initial Value | Returns | Failure Returns |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `malloc(n)` | $n$ | Undefined (garbage) | `void *` to block | `NULL` |
| `calloc(c, s)` | $c \times s$ | All zero bits | `void *` to block | `NULL` |
| `realloc(p, n)` | $n$ (new size) | Preserved old data; new bytes uninit | `void *` to new block | `NULL` |
| `free(p)` | N/A | N/A | `void` | N/A |

### 2.2.1 Fragmentation Formulas

Let $R_{\text{requested}}$ be the total memory requested and $R_{\text{allocated}}$ be the total memory actually handed out.

$$
\text{Internal Fragmentation} = R_{\text{allocated}} - R_{\text{requested}}
$$

$$
\text{External Fragmentation} = \text{Free Memory Available} - \text{Largest Contiguous Free Block}
$$

$$
\text{Memory Utilization} (\eta) = \frac{R_{\text{requested}}}{R_{\text{allocated}}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common KTU question is *"Calculate the memory wasted due to internal fragmentation when a structure of 26 bytes is allocated in a system with 8-byte alignment."* The answer is $32 - 26 = 6$ bytes.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems**: Kernel memory pools (`kmalloc` in Linux) use DMA principles.
- **Databases**: Buffer pool managers dynamically allocate pages.
- **Compilers**: Symbol tables and parse trees are built on the heap.
- **Embedded Systems**: `FreeRTOS` uses `pvPortMalloc()` for task stacks.
- **Game Engines**: Texture and mesh loaders use DMA to handle variable asset sizes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Allocating an Integer Array

**Problem:** Dynamically allocate an array of `n = 5` integers, populate it with the first five natural numbers, and print them. Calculate the total memory used.

### Step-by-Step C Implementation

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
    int n = 5;
    int *arr = (int *)malloc(n * sizeof(int));
    
    if (arr == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Memory allocation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        arr[i] = i + 1;   /* Populate 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 */
    }
    
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("%d ", arr[i]);
    }
    
    free(arr);
    arr = NULL;   /* Prevents dangling pointer */
    return 0;
}
```

### Memory Footprint Derivation

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = n \times \text{sizeof(int)}
$$

$$
\text{Total Bytes} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ bytes}
$$

Now consider the same allocation using `calloc`:
$$
\text{Initial Byte Pattern} = \underbrace{0x00}_{\text{byte 0}} \, \underbrace{0x00}_{\text{byte 1}} \, \underbrace{0x00}_{\text{byte 2}} \, \underbrace{0x00}_{\text{byte 3}} \dots
$$

Compared to `malloc`, where the byte pattern is **indeterminate** (could be leftover from a previous allocation).

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Resizing with `realloc`

**Problem:** Start with a buffer of `4` characters, then grow it to `8` characters. Show the data-preservation logic.

### Step-by-Step C Implementation

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <string.h>

int main(void) {
    char *buf = (char *)malloc(4 * sizeof(char));
    if (!buf) return 1;
    
    strcpy(buf, "Kerala");
    printf("Original: %s (len=%zu)\n", buf, strlen(buf));
    
    char *tmp = (char *)realloc(buf, 8 * sizeof(char));
    if (!tmp) {
        free(buf);
        return 1;
    }
    buf = tmp;
    
    strcpy(buf, "Kerala University");
    printf("Resized : %s (len=%zu)\n", buf, strlen(buf));
    
    free(buf);
    return 0;
}
```

### Reallocation Logic — Algebraic View

$$
\text{Old block: } B_0 \text{ of size } S_0 = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\text{New request: } S_1 = 8 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
\Delta S = S_1 - S_0 = 8 - 4 = 4 \text{ bytes (additional)}
$$

The allocator attempts **Strategy 1** first:
$$
\text{If } B_0.\text{next\_free} \geq \Delta S \implies \text{Extend in place}
$$

If not possible, **Strategy 2**:
$$
B_1 = \text{AllocateNew}(S_1)
$$

$$
\text{memcpy}(B_1, B_0, \min(S_0, S_1))
$$

$$
\text{free}(B_0)
$$

$$
\text{return } B_1
$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Fragmentation Calculation

**Problem:** A heap has 1000 bytes free, fragmented into blocks of sizes 200, 150, 100, 250, and 300 bytes. A request for **400 contiguous bytes** arrives. Calculate external fragmentation and decide if the request can be served.

### Analytical Matrix

| Block | Free Size (B) | Request 400 B Fits? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 200 | No |
| 2 | 150 | No |
| 3 | 100 | No |
| 4 | 250 | No |
| 5 | 300 | No |
| **Total Free** | **1000** | **None singly** |

**Step 1** — Compute total free memory:
$$
F_{\text{total}} = 200 + 150 + 100 + 250 + 300 = 1000 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2** — Identify the largest contiguous free block:
$$
F_{\text{max}} = \max(200, 150, 100, 250, 300) = 300 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute external fragmentation:
$$
E_{\text{frag}} = F_{\text{total}} - F_{\text{max}} = 1000 - 300 = 700 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4** — Allocation verdict:
$$
\text{Request} = 400 \text{ bytes}, \quad F_{\text{max}} = 300 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
400 > 300 \implies \text{Request DENIED, despite } F_{\text{total}} \geq 400
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the classic **external fragmentation paradox**: there is enough memory in total, but no single block is large enough to satisfy the request. A **compaction** routine would be needed to defragment.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Memory Leak Detection

**Problem:** Identify the memory leak in the following snippet.

```c
void buggy(void) {
    int *p = (int *)malloc(10 * sizeof(int));
    p = (int *)malloc(20 * sizeof(int));   /* Leak! Old 10-int block is orphaned */
    free(p);
}
```

### Leak Calculation

$$
\text{Bytes leaked} = 10 \times \text{sizeof(int)} = 40 \text{ bytes (per call)}
$$

If `buggy()` is called $k$ times:
$$
L_{\text{total}} = k \times 40 \text{ bytes}
$$

This is why the **golden rule of DMA** is: *Every `malloc` / `calloc` / `realloc` must have exactly one matching `free`.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout — Stack vs Heap

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["HIGH ADDRESS"] --> nodeB["STACK SEGMENT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Heap Grows Upward"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Free Heap Space"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["HEAP SEGMENT"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["BSS Uninitialized Data"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DATA Segment Initialized"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["TEXT Segment Code"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["LOW ADDRESS"]
```

## 4.2 Dynamic Memory Allocation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Start: Program Begins"] --> stepB{"Memory Needed at Runtime?"}
    stepB -->|No| stepC["Use Stack Allocation"]
    stepB -->|Yes| stepD["Call malloc or calloc"]
    stepD --> stepE{"Allocation Successful?"}
    stepE -->|No NULL returned| stepF["Handle Error Exit or Retry"]
    stepE -->|Yes Pointer returned| stepG["Use the Memory Block"]
    stepG --> stepH{"Need to Resize?"}
    stepH -->|Yes| stepI["Call realloc Use temporary pointer"]
    stepI --> stepJ{"Realloc Successful?"}
    stepJ -->|No| stepK["Free original Block Abort"]
    stepJ -->|Yes| stepG
    stepH -->|No| stepL{"Memory No Longer Needed?"}
    stepL -->|Yes| stepM["Call free Set pointer to NULL"]
    stepL -->|No| stepG
    stepM --> stepN["End: Block Returned to Heap"]
```

## 4.3 Fragmentation Visualization

```mermaid
graph LR
    fragA["Used 100B"] --> fragB["Free 200B"]
    fragB --> fragC["Used 150B"]
    fragC --> fragD["Free 100B"]
    fragD --> fragE["Used 250B"]
    fragE --> fragF["Free 300B"]
    fragF --> fragG["Used 200B"]

    req["Request 400B"] -.->|Denied No block big enough| fragB
    req -.->|Denied| fragD
    req -.->|Denied| fragF
```

## 4.4 Memory Lifecycle — From `malloc` to `free`

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FreeState
    FreeState --> AllocatedState : malloc / calloc
    AllocatedState --> AllocatedState : realloc resize in place
    AllocatedState --> MovedState : realloc relocate
    MovedState --> AllocatedState : pointer updated
    AllocatedState --> FreeState : free
    MovedState --> FreeState : free
    FreeState --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between static (stack) and dynamic (heap) memory allocation. Mention two advantages of dynamic allocation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static (Stack) Allocation | Dynamic (Heap) Allocation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| When decided | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Where stored | Stack segment | Heap segment |
| Lifetime | Till function returns | Until explicitly freed |
| Size | Fixed at declaration | Variable, grows on demand |
| Speed | Faster (LIFO pointer bump) | Slower (free-list search) |

**Two advantages of dynamic allocation (1.5 marks each):**
1. **Size flexibility** — the program can request exactly the memory it needs, avoiding over-allocation.
2. **Persistence across function calls** — heap memory survives function return, enabling linked structures, dynamic arrays, and shared state.
3. *(Bonus 3rd point)* Efficient use of memory when total demand is unpredictable.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the difference between `malloc()` and `calloc()`? Write the syntax of both.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Syntax (1.5 marks):**
```c
void *malloc(size_t size);
void *calloc(size_t count, size_t size_t size);
```

**Differences (1.5 marks):**

| Feature | `malloc(n)` | `calloc(c, s)` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Initialization | Garbage / uninitialized | Zero-initialized |
| Arguments | One (total bytes) | Two (count, element size) |
| Overflow safety | Caller must check `n` | Compiler/library checks `c * s` |
| Typical use | Raw buffers, performance-critical | Arrays needing default zero |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`
**(a)** Explain the four dynamic memory allocation functions in C (`malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free`) with syntax and a suitable example for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a C program to dynamically allocate memory for a structure containing a student's `name` (string of max 50 chars), `roll_no` (int), and `marks` (float). Read data for `n` students, compute the average marks, and deallocate memory properly. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

1. **`malloc(size)`** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Allocates `size` bytes of uninitialized memory.
   - Returns `void *`; returns `NULL` on failure.
   - **Example**: `int *p = (int *)malloc(5 * sizeof(int));`

2. **`calloc(count, size)`** *(1.5 marks)*
   - Allocates `count * size` bytes, all bits set to zero.
   - **Example**: `int *p = (int *)calloc(5, sizeof(int));` — array of 5 zeros.

3. **`realloc(ptr, newSize)`** *(2 marks)*
   - Resizes block; if in-place expansion fails, allocates new block, copies data, frees old.
   - **Example**:
     ```c
     int *p = (int *)malloc(4 * sizeof(int));
     int *q = (int *)realloc(p, 8 * sizeof(int));
     if (q != NULL) p = q;
     ```

4. **`free(ptr)`** *(2 marks)*
   - Deallocates the block; sets contents to indeterminate.
   - **Example**: `free(p); p = NULL;` — prevents dangling pointer.
   - Failing to call `free` causes a **memory leak**.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

typedef struct {
    char  name[50];
    int   roll_no;
    float marks;
} Student;

int main(void) {
    int n;
    printf("Enter number of students: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);
    
    /* Allocate array of n Students */
    Student *s = (Student *)malloc(n * sizeof(Student));
    if (s == NULL) {
        fprintf(stderr, "Allocation failed\n");
        return 1;
    }
    
    /* Read data */
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        printf("Student %d name: ", i + 1);
        scanf(" %49s", s[i].name);
        printf("Student %d roll no: ", i + 1);
        scanf("%d", &s[i].roll_no);
        printf("Student %d marks: ", i + 1);
        scanf("%f", &s[i].marks);
    }
    
    /* Compute average */
    float sum = 0.0f;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
        sum += s[i].marks;
    }
    float avg = sum / n;
    printf("Average marks = %.2f\n", avg);
    
    /* Deallocate */
    free(s);
    s = NULL;
    return 0;
}
```

**Mark split (7 marks):**
- Structure definition: **1 mark**
- `malloc` with NULL check: **2 marks**
- Input loop + average logic: **2 marks**
- `free` + NULL assignment: **1 mark**
- Output formatting: **1 mark**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**(a)** Define **memory fragmentation**. Distinguish between **internal** and **external** fragmentation with diagrams. A heap has free blocks of sizes 120, 200, 80, 350, and 90 bytes. A request for 300 bytes arrives. Will the request be served? Justify with calculation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the concept of **dangling pointer** and **memory leak** with examples. How can they be prevented? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** *Memory fragmentation is the phenomenon whereby free memory exists in the heap but is unusable because it is split into small, non-contiguous pieces.*

**Internal vs External (3 marks):**

- **Internal Fragmentation**: Occurs when the allocator hands out a block *larger* than what was requested (e.g., due to alignment or block-size rounding). The unused space *inside* the allocated block is wasted.
- **External Fragmentation**: Occurs when there is enough total free memory, but no single free block is large enough to satisfy the request.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given free blocks:
$$
B = \{120, 200, 80, 350, 90\}
$$

$$
F_{\text{total}} = 120 + 200 + 80 + 350 + 90 = 840 \text{ bytes}
$$

$$
F_{\text{max}} = \max(B) = 350 \text{ bytes}
$$

Request $R = 300$ bytes:
$$
R = 300 \le F_{\text{max}} = 350 \implies \text{Request SERVED using the 350-byte block}
$$

$$
\text{Residual in that block} = 350 - 300 = 50 \text{ bytes}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Dangling Pointer (3 marks):**

A *dangling pointer* is a pointer that still holds the **address of a freed memory block**. Accessing it leads to *undefined behaviour*.

```c
int *p = (int *)malloc(sizeof(int));
free(p);              /* p still holds old address */
printf("%d", *p);     /* UNDEFINED BEHAVIOUR */
```

**Prevention (1 mark):** Set `p = NULL;` immediately after `free(p);`.

**Memory Leak (3 marks):**

A *memory leak* occurs when dynamically allocated memory is **lost** — no pointer references it, so it can never be `free`d.

```c
void leak_demo(void) {
    int *p = (int *)malloc(100 * sizeof(int));
    return;   /* p goes out of scope; block is leaked */
}
```

**Prevention (1 mark):** Ensure every `malloc` / `calloc` / `realloc` has a corresponding `free`, and use tools like `Valgrind` or AddressSanitizer in development.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting `(type *)` cast in C** — In modern C, the cast is *optional* but most textbooks show it. Examiners expect the cast in the answer. (0.5 mark penalty)
> 2. **Not checking for `NULL` after `malloc`** — A frequent 1-mark deduction. Always write: `if (p == NULL) { ... }`.
> 3. **Confusing `realloc` failure semantics** — Many students think `realloc` frees the old block on failure. **It does NOT.** The original block remains valid; only the returned pointer is `NULL`. Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Using `sizeof(*ptr)` instead of `sizeof(type)`** — Both are correct, but mixing them carelessly suggests misunderstanding.
> 5. **Memory leak in `realloc` direct assignment** — Always do `tmp = realloc(p, n); if (tmp) p = tmp;` to avoid leaking the original block if `realloc` fails. Examiners actively look for this idiom.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Dynamic Memory Allocation**

- [x] **Heap** is where DMA happens; **Stack** is for automatic variables.
- [x] The four golden functions: `malloc`, `calloc`, `realloc`, `free` — all live in `<stdlib.h>`.
- [x] `malloc(n)` returns **uninitialized** memory; `calloc(c, s)` returns **zero-initialized** memory.
- [x] `realloc(p, 0)` is *not* equivalent to `free(p)` in standard C; use `free(p)` explicitly.
- [x] **Always** use a **temporary pointer** when calling `realloc` to avoid leaks on failure.
- [x] **Always** check the return value for `NULL` after any allocation.
- [x] **Always** set the pointer to `NULL` after `free` to prevent dangling-pointer bugs.
- [x] **Internal fragmentation** = unused space *inside* an allocated block (alignment waste).
- [x] **External fragmentation** = free memory exists but no single block is large enough.
- [x] Compaction, paging, and segmentation are common solutions to external fragmentation.
- [x] Memory leak = `malloc` without a matching `free`; dangling pointer = use after `free`.
- [x] In **C++**, prefer `new` / `delete` (or `new[]` / `delete[]`) — never mix with `malloc` / `free` for the same object.
- [x] Valgrind, AddressSanitizer, and Electric Fence are industry-standard leak-detection tools.
- [x] **Golden Rule**: *Every `malloc` / `calloc` / `realloc` must have exactly one matching `free`.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singly Linked List and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singly Linked List and Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **Singly Linked List (SLL)** is a fundamental linear, dynamic data structure belonging to the family of linked data structures. It consists of a sequential collection of **nodes**, where each node stores two distinct fields:

1. **DATA field** — Contains the actual value/element of the node.
2. **NEXT (Link/Pointer) field** — Stores the memory address (reference) of the immediately succeeding node in the sequence.

The list is anchored by an external pointer variable called the **HEAD** (or `start`/`first`), which points to the first node. The last node's `next` pointer is set to `NULL` (or `None`), acting as the **sentinel terminator** that marks the logical end of the list.

$$ \text{Node} = \{\text{Data}, \text{Next Pointer}\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Singly Linked List is the **most frequently asked topic** in Module 2. Questions commonly appear on insertion, deletion, traversal, and reversal operations. Mastery of pointer manipulation and edge cases (empty list, single-node list, end-of-list) is essential.

## Conceptual Analogy — The Train Compartment Intuition

Imagine a **train** where each **compartment (bogey)** is a *node*:
- The **passengers + cargo** inside a compartment represent the **DATA**.
- The **physical coupling hook** connecting one compartment to the next represents the **NEXT pointer**.
- The **locomotive (engine)** represents the **HEAD pointer** — it knows where the train starts.
- A **single track, one-way track** represents the **"Singly"** nature — you can only move **forward**, never backward.
- If the last compartment has **no hook**, the train ends — this is the **NULL** terminator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike an array where elements live in **contiguous memory**, linked list nodes are scattered across **arbitrary memory locations**, stitched together logically via pointers. This is why linked lists are called *dynamic* and *non-contiguous* structures.

## Standard Metrics and Properties

| Property | Standard Value / Description |
|---|---|
| **Node Size** | Sum of `sizeof(data) + sizeof(pointer)` — typically **8 bytes (64-bit) or 12 bytes** depending on architecture |
| **Direction of Traversal** | **Unidirectional (forward only)** |
| **Memory Allocation** | **Dynamic (Heap)** — usually via `malloc()` / `new` |
| **Access Type** | **Sequential Access Only** — no random access like arrays |
| **Time to Access i-th Element** | **O(n)** — must traverse from HEAD |
| **Head Pointer** | **Mandatory** external reference to access the list |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Node Structure Representation

In **C language** (KTU-preferred), a singly linked list node is defined using a `struct`:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;           // DATA field
    struct Node *next;  // NEXT pointer field
};
```

The visual representation of a node is:

$$
\boxed{\text{[ DATA } \mid \text{ NEXT } \rightarrow]}
$$

- The **left compartment** stores the data value.
- The **right compartment** stores the address of the next node (or `NULL`).

## 2.2 Types of Singly Linked Lists

| Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Empty List** | `HEAD = NULL` — no nodes allocated |
| **Singly Linked List (Basic)** | Standard forward-traversal list |
| **Singly Linked List with HEAD node** | A dummy/sentinel node at the front (simplifies edge cases) |
| **Circular Singly Linked List** | The last node's `next` points back to HEAD (no NULL end) |

## 2.3 Core Operations — Theoretical Breakdown

### Operation 1: **Traversal (Display)**
- **Why:** To visit every node from HEAD to the last node.
- **How:** Start at HEAD, follow `next` pointers, print data, stop when `next == NULL`.
- **Edge case:** If `HEAD == NULL` (empty list), print "List is empty".

### Operation 2: **Insertion** (3 sub-cases)
- **Case A — Insertion at Beginning:** Create new node, point its `next` to current HEAD, update HEAD.
- **Case B — Insertion at End:** Traverse to last node, point its `next` to new node, new node's `next = NULL`.
- **Case C — Insertion After a Given Node:** Traverse to target node, new node's `next = target->next`, target->next = new node.

### Operation 3: **Deletion** (3 sub-cases)
- **Case A — Deletion at Beginning:** Store HEAD in temp, move HEAD to `HEAD->next`, free temp.
- **Case B — Deletion at End:** Traverse to second-last node, free last node, set `second_last->next = NULL`.
- **Case C — Deletion of a Given Node:** Traverse to the node *before* the target, bypass the target, free it.

### Operation 4: **Search**
- **Why:** Find whether a value exists in the list.
- **How:** Linear scan from HEAD; compare each node's data with the search key.
- **Returns:** Position (1-indexed) if found, `-1` (or 0) if not found.

### Operation 5: **Reversal**
- **Why:** Flip the direction of all `next` pointers.
- **How:** Use three pointers (`prev`, `curr`, `next`) in a single pass.
- **Result:** The original HEAD becomes the tail (points to NULL); the original tail becomes the new HEAD.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **#1 high-yield revision block** for KTU exams. Memorize every cell.

| Operation | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Traversal of entire list** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Single pass from HEAD to NULL |
| **Insertion at Beginning** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | No traversal needed |
| **Insertion at End** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Must traverse to last node |
| **Insertion at End (with tail pointer)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Only if tail pointer is maintained |
| **Insertion After Node X** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Assuming X is already known/accessed |
| **Insertion at Position i** | $\mathcal{O}(i)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Traverse to (i-1)th position |
| **Deletion at Beginning** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | No traversal needed |
| **Deletion at End** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Must find second-last node |
| **Deletion of Node with value X** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Search + bypass |
| **Search for value X** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Worst case — element at end or absent |
| **Access i-th element** | $\mathcal{O}(i) = \mathcal{O}(n)$ worst | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Sequential access only |
| **Reversal of list** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Three-pointer in-place technique |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the complexity of "Insertion at End" with "Insertion at Beginning". Always clarify: **End insertion = O(n)**, **Beginning insertion = O(1)** — this is a favorite **2-mark short question**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Singly Linked Lists power several real systems:
- **Operating Systems:** Used in **process scheduling queues** (FCFS — First Come First Served) and **symbol tables** in compilers.
- **Memory Management:** **Free lists** in dynamic memory allocators (e.g., `malloc` internals).
- **Undo/Redo Mechanisms:** Simple linear history chains.
- **Music Players:** Playlist navigation (next song only).
- **Hash Map Chaining:** Collision resolution in hash tables.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Insertion at the Beginning of a Singly Linked List

**Problem Statement:** Given a singly linked list with HEAD pointer, insert a new node with value `X` at the **beginning** of the list.

### Logical Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Allocate Memory for New Node**
Dynamically request heap memory for a new node of size `struct Node`. This returns a pointer to the new node.

```c
struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
```

**Step 2 — Assign Data to the New Node**
Store the value `X` in the `data` field of the new node.

```c
newNode->data = X;
```

**Step 3 — Link the New Node to the Current HEAD**
Set the `next` field of the new node to point at the current first node (i.e., whatever HEAD is currently pointing to).

```c
newNode->next = HEAD;   // newNode now points to the old first node
```

**Step 4 — Update HEAD to the New Node**
Move the HEAD pointer to the newly created node, making it the new first node.

```c
HEAD = newNode;         // HEAD now points to the new first node
```

### Mathematical Representation of Pointer State Transition

Let the original list be:
$$ L_{\text{old}} = \text{HEAD} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL} $$

After inserting new node $n_{\text{new}}$ at the beginning:
$$ L_{\text{new}} = \text{HEAD} \rightarrow n_{\text{new}} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL} $$

The transformations in pointer values are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{new}}.\text{next} &:= \text{HEAD}_{\text{old}} \\
\text{HEAD}_{\text{new}} &:= n_{\text{new}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$
T(n) = \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{malloc}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{assign data}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{link}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{HEAD update}} = \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Deletion at the End of a Singly Linked List

**Problem Statement:** Delete the **last node** of a singly linked list and update the second-last node's `next` to NULL.

### Logical Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Handle Empty List Edge Case**
If `HEAD == NULL`, the list is empty; deletion is impossible. Return early.

**Step 2 — Handle Single-Node List Edge Case**
If `HEAD->next == NULL`, the list has exactly one node. Free it and set `HEAD = NULL`.

**Step 3 — Traverse to Second-Last Node**
Use two pointers: `temp` (current) and `prev` (previous). Move both forward until `temp->next == NULL`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initialize: } & \text{temp} = \text{HEAD}, \quad \text{prev} = \text{NULL} \\
\text{Loop while } \text{temp.next} &\neq \text{NULL:} \\
& \text{prev} = \text{temp} \\
& \text{temp} = \text{temp.next} \\
\text{End loop: } & \text{temp} = \text{last node}, \quad \text{prev} = \text{second-last node}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Update Second-Last Node's `next` Pointer**
Set `prev->next = NULL` to detach the last node.

**Step 5 — Free the Last Node**
Call `free(temp)` to release the memory.

### Mathematical Pointer Transformation

Before deletion:
$$ \text{HEAD} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_{k-1} \rightarrow n_k \rightarrow \text{NULL} $$

After deletion:
$$ \text{HEAD} \rightarrow n_1 \rightarrow n_2 \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow n_{k-1} \rightarrow \text{NULL} $$

Pointer update equation:
$$
\text{prev}.\text{next} := \text{NULL}
$$
$$
\text{where } \text{prev} = n_{k-1}
$$

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

$$
T(n) = \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(n)}_{\text{traverse to second-last}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{update } \text{next}} + \underbrace{\mathcal{O}(1)}_{\text{free}} = \mathcal{O}(n)
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Between Singly Linked List and Array

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison table is a **favorite KTU 2-mark question**. Be ready to list at least 4 differences.

| Parameter | Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Layout** | Contiguous | Non-contiguous (scattered) |
| **Size** | Fixed (static) at declaration | Dynamic — grows/shrinks at runtime |
| **Access Time (i-th element)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — random access | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — sequential only |
| **Insertion at Beginning** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift all elements | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — update HEAD |
| **Deletion at Beginning** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — shift all elements | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — update HEAD |
| **Memory Overhead** | No extra memory | Extra $\sim 4\text{–}8$ bytes per node (pointer) |
| **Cache Friendliness** | High (spatial locality) | Low (random memory accesses) |
| **Risk of Overflow** | Yes (fixed size) | No (limited by heap memory) |
| **Reverse Traversal** | Possible (e.g., decrement index) | Not possible (only forward via `next`) |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Simple | Moderate (pointer manipulation) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Singly Linked List Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD["HEAD"] --> N1["Node 1: Data=10, Next"]
    N1 --> N2["Node 2: Data=20, Next"]
    N2 --> N3["Node 3: Data=30, Next"]
    N3 --> N4["Node 4: Data=40, Next"]
    N4 --> NULL["NULL End"]
```

> **Figure Description:** A standard singly linked list with 4 nodes. The HEAD pointer anchors the list. The last node's `next` field is set to `NULL`, indicating termination.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Insertion at Beginning Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start: BeginInsertAtBeginning"] --> B["Allocate newNode via malloc"]
    B --> C{"Is malloc successful?"}
    C -- "No" --> D["Print Memory Allocation Failed. Return."]
    C -- "Yes" --> E["Assign newNode.data = X"]
    E --> F["Set newNode.next = HEAD"]
    F --> G["Set HEAD = newNode"]
    G --> H["Print Insertion Successful. End."]
    D --> H
```

> **Figure Description:** Decision-flow representation of insertion at the beginning. The `malloc` success check is a critical KTU-emphasized edge case.

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Deletion of a Node by Value

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Start: DeleteNodeWithValueX"] --> B{"Is HEAD == NULL?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Print List is Empty. End."]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Is HEAD.data == X?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["temp = HEAD; HEAD = HEAD.next; free temp; End."]
    D -- "No" --> F["Set temp = HEAD"]
    F --> G{"temp.next != NULL AND temp.next.data != X?"}
    G -- "Yes" --> H["temp = temp.next; Loop back to G"]
    G -- "No" --> I{"Is temp.next == NULL?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Print Value X Not Found. End."]
    I -- "No" --> K["target = temp.next; temp.next = target.next; free target; End."]
```

> **Figure Description:** This flowchart captures all four edge cases: empty list, deletion at HEAD, value not found, and value found in middle. A classic KTU evaluation expects students to handle all branches.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Node Memory Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Address: 1000"] --> B["Address: 1040"]
    B --> C["Address: 1080"]
    C --> D["Address: 1120"]
    A --- E["DATA = 10, NEXT = 1040"]
    B --- F["DATA = 20, NEXT = 1080"]
    C --- G["DATA = 30, NEXT = 1120"]
    D --- H["DATA = 40, NEXT = NULL"]
```

> **Figure Description:** Conceptual view of how nodes are **scattered** in memory but **logically linked** through pointer addresses (e.g., 1000, 1040, 1080, 1120). This illustrates the **non-contiguous** nature of linked lists — a key conceptual differentiator from arrays.

## 4.5 Mermaid State Diagram — Reversal Operation (Three-Pointer Technique)

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1["State 1: prev=NULL, curr=HEAD, next=NULL"] --> S2["State 2: next=curr.next, curr.next=prev, prev=curr, curr=next"]
    S2 --> S3["State 3: Repeat until curr == NULL"]
    S3 --> S4["State 4: Set HEAD = prev. End."]
```

> **Figure Description:** The reversal algorithm uses three pointers: `prev`, `curr`, `next`. Each iteration reverses one `next` pointer. After the loop, `prev` points to the new HEAD.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define a Singly Linked List. What is the role of the HEAD pointer and the NULL pointer in a singly linked list?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Singly Linked List** is a linear, dynamic data structure consisting of a sequence of nodes, where each node contains a **DATA field** and a **NEXT pointer field** that points to the next node in the sequence.

- **HEAD pointer:** It is an external pointer variable that stores the address of the **first node** of the list. It acts as the **entry point** to access any node in the list. Without HEAD, the entire list becomes inaccessible (memory leak).

- **NULL pointer:** It is a special value (typically `0` in C) stored in the `next` field of the **last node**, signifying the **end of the list**. It serves as the **sentinel terminator** that prevents the traversal from going out of bounds.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- HEAD role: 1 mark
- NULL role: 1 mark

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Compare arrays and singly linked lists based on memory allocation, access time, and insertion at the beginning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Array | Singly Linked List |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Allocation** | Static, contiguous, fixed size at compile time | Dynamic, non-contiguous, allocated at runtime via `malloc` |
| **Access Time (i-th element)** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — direct random access using index | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — sequential traversal from HEAD |
| **Insertion at Beginning** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — requires shifting all elements right by one position | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — just create new node and update HEAD pointer |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per correct comparison row.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU ESE Pattern):** KTU End-Semester Examinations follow an **Internal Choice** in Part B. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question carries **14 marks**, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Write a C function to **insert a new node at the end** of a singly linked list. Explain the algorithm with a neat diagram. State its time complexity.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Write a C function to **delete a node whose value matches a given key** from a singly linked list. Handle all edge cases.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Insertion at End (7 marks)

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Create a new node with given data.
2. If the list is empty (`HEAD == NULL`), make the new node the HEAD and return.
3. Otherwise, traverse to the **last node** (where `next == NULL`).
4. Link the last node's `next` to the new node.
5. Set new node's `next = NULL`.

**C Program:**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node **HEAD, int X) {
    // Step 1: Allocate new node
    struct Node *newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Memory allocation failed\n");
        return;
    }
    newNode->data = X;
    newNode->next = NULL;

    // Step 2: If list is empty, new node is HEAD
    if (*HEAD == NULL) {
        *HEAD = newNode;
        return;
    }

    // Step 3: Traverse to last node
    struct Node *temp = *HEAD;
    while (temp->next != NULL) {
        temp = temp->next;
    }

    // Step 4: Link last node to new node
    temp->next = newNode;
}
```

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — must traverse the entire list in the worst case (when the list has $n$ nodes).

**Diagram (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["HEAD -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> NULL"] --> B["Allocate newNode(40)"]
    B --> C["Traverse to last node (30)"]
    C --> D["Set 30.next = newNode(40)"]
    D --> E["HEAD -> 10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> NULL"]
```

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct algorithm steps: 2 marks
- Working C code: 3 marks
- Time complexity: 1 mark
- Diagram: 1 mark

---

### Part (b) — Delete Node by Value (7 marks)

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. If `HEAD == NULL`, list is empty; return.
2. If `HEAD->data == key`, delete the HEAD node and update HEAD.
3. Otherwise, traverse the list with two pointers: `prev` and `curr`.
4. If `curr->data == key`, bypass `curr` via `prev->next = curr->next`, then free `curr`.
5. If end of list reached without finding key, print "Key not found".

**C Program:**

```c
void deleteByValue(struct Node **HEAD, int key) {
    // Edge case 1: Empty list
    if (*HEAD == NULL) {
        printf("List is empty\n");
        return;
    }

    struct Node *temp = *HEAD;
    struct Node *prev = NULL;

    // Edge case 2: Key is at HEAD
    if (temp != NULL && temp->data == key) {
        *HEAD = temp->next;
        free(temp);
        return;
    }

    // Traverse to find key
    while (temp != NULL && temp->data != key) {
        prev = temp;
        temp = temp->next;
    }

    // Edge case 3: Key not found
    if (temp == NULL) {
        printf("Key %d not found in the list\n", key);
        return;
    }

    // Key found: unlink and free
    prev->next = temp->next;
    free(temp);
}
```

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — linear search in the worst case.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Edge case handling (3 cases): 3 marks
- Correct traversal logic: 2 marks
- Memory free + unlink: 1 mark
- Time complexity: 1 mark

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 marks]** Write a C function to **reverse a singly linked list** using an iterative approach. Explain the three-pointer technique with a suitable example.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Write a C function to **search for a given value** in a singly linked list and return its position (1-indexed). If not found, return -1.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — Reversal of SLL (7 marks)

**Three-Pointer Technique:** `prev`, `curr`, `next`

**Algorithm:**
1. Initialize `prev = NULL`, `curr = HEAD`.
2. Loop while `curr != NULL`:
   - Save `next = curr->next`
   - Reverse pointer: `curr->next = prev`
   - Move `prev = curr`
   - Move `curr = next`
3. After loop, set `HEAD = prev`.

**C Program:**

```c
void reverseList(struct Node **HEAD) {
    struct Node *prev = NULL;
    struct Node *curr = *HEAD;
    struct Node *next = NULL;

    while (curr != NULL) {
        next = curr->next;     // Store next node
        curr->next = prev;     // Reverse the pointer
        prev = curr;           // Move prev forward
        curr = next;           // Move curr forward
    }
    *HEAD = prev;              // Update HEAD
}
```

**Worked Example (Initial):** `HEAD → 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL`

| Iteration | prev | curr | next | Action | List State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial | NULL | 10 | — | Setup | 10 → 20 → 30 → NULL |
| 1 | NULL | 10 | 20 | Reverse 10→NULL | 10 → NULL; 20 → 30 → NULL |
| 2 | 10 | 20 | 30 | Reverse 20→10 | 20 → 10 → NULL; 30 → NULL |
| 3 | 20 | 30 | NULL | Reverse 30→20 | 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL |
| End | 30 | NULL | — | `HEAD = prev = 30` | 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL |

**Final Result:** `HEAD → 30 → 20 → 10 → NULL` ✅

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — single pass.
**Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only 3 pointers used.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Three-pointer logic: 3 marks
- Correct C code: 2 marks
- Worked example / dry run: 1 mark
- Time & space complexity: 1 mark

---

### Part (b) — Search Operation (7 marks)

**Algorithm:**
1. Start at `HEAD`, set `position = 1`.
2. Loop while `temp != NULL`:
   - If `temp->data == key`, return `position`.
   - Else, increment `position` and move `temp = temp->next`.
3. If loop ends without finding, return -1.

**C Program:**

```c
int searchValue(struct Node *HEAD, int key) {
    int position = 1;
    struct Node *temp = HEAD;

    while (temp != NULL) {
        if (temp->data == key) {
            return position;   // Found at current position
        }
        temp = temp->next;
        position++;
    }
    return -1;   // Not found
}
```

**Dry Run on List `10 → 20 → 30 → 40`:**

| Step | temp->data | key | Action | position |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 30 | 10 ≠ 30, move on | 1 → 2 |
| 2 | 20 | 30 | 20 ≠ 30, move on | 2 → 3 |
| 3 | 30 | 30 | 30 == 30, **RETURN** | 3 |

**Output:** `searchValue(HEAD, 30)` returns **3**.

**Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — linear scan.
**Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only 2 variables.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Algorithm steps: 2 marks
- C code: 3 marks
- Dry run trace: 1 mark
- Complexity analysis: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to free memory** after deletion → loses 1-2 marks (memory leak is a serious bug).
> 2. **Not handling the empty list case** (`HEAD == NULL`) → loses 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `*HEAD` vs `HEAD`** in function parameters — use **double pointer `**HEAD`** when modifying the HEAD itself (e.g., insert at beginning, delete at beginning).
> 4. **Not setting `newNode->next = NULL`** when inserting at end → causes dangling pointer / undefined behavior.
> 5. **Writing the traversal in reverse for reversal** — the `next` pointer must be saved **before** it is overwritten.
> 6. **Missing time complexity analysis** — KTU mandates it for full marks in Part B.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam!**

- 🔹 A **Singly Linked List** is a **linear, dynamic, non-contiguous** data structure of nodes connected by `next` pointers.
- 🔹 Each node has **two fields**: `data` and `next`. The `next` of the last node is `NULL`.
- 🔹 The **HEAD** pointer is the only entry point to the list.
- 🔹 **Insertion at beginning** = $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **Insertion at end** = $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (without tail pointer).
- 🔹 **Deletion at beginning** = $\mathcal{O}(1)$. **Deletion at end** = $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
- 🔹 **Search** is always $\mathcal{O}(n)$ — no random access.
- 🔹 **Reversal** uses the **three-pointer technique** (`prev`, `curr`, `next`) in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ space.
- 🔹 **Doubly linked lists** allow backward traversal (not in SLL — SLL is **unidirectional**).
- 🔹 Always use `**HEAD` (double pointer) in C functions that **modify HEAD** (e.g., insert/delete at beginning).
- 🔹 Always **free memory** after deletion to avoid memory leaks.
- 🔹 Always handle **edge cases**: empty list, single-node list, and value-not-found.
- 🔹 **Arrays vs SLL:** Arrays win in random access ($\mathcal{O}(1)$); SLLs win in dynamic insertions/deletions at the beginning ($\mathcal{O}(1)$).
- 🔹 SLL is used in **process scheduling, symbol tables, free lists, hash chaining, and playlist navigation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Doubly Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Doubly Linked List**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Doubly Linked List (DLL):** A linear, dynamic data structure consisting of a sequence of **nodes** where each node is a self-referential structure containing three fields — a **data field**, a pointer to the **previous node** (`prev` / `left` / `back`), and a pointer to the **next node** (`next` / `right` / `forward`). This bidirectional linkage allows traversal in both forward and backward directions with equal time complexity $O(1)$ per step.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEOT303)**, the Doubly Linked List is formally introduced as an extension of the Singly Linked List under **Module 2: Linear Data Structures**, addressing the inherent limitation of unidirectional traversal. The structure maintains two external pointers — a **head** pointer referencing the first node, and a **tail** (or sometimes the head itself is sufficient) pointer that may reference the last node to enable $O(1)$ rear-end operations.

The generic node definition in C (KTU Board standard) is:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* prev;
    struct Node* next;
};
```

Mathematically, a DLL of $n$ nodes is represented as the ordered tuple:

$$DLL = \langle n, L_0, L_1, L_2, \dots, L_{n-1} \rangle$$

where each $L_i$ is a node with the invariant:

$$L_i.\text{next} = L_{i+1} \quad \text{and} \quad L_{i+1}.\text{prev} = L_i \quad \forall i \in [0, n-2]$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Train With Doors on Both Sides

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a metro train where every coach (node) is connected to its neighboring coaches on **both** sides. You can walk forward toward the engine, or you can walk backward toward the tail, simply by opening the appropriate door and stepping through. Each coach carries passengers (data) and has two doors — a *front door* (next pointer) and a *rear door* (prev pointer). This is fundamentally different from a single-track hand-pulled rickshaw (Singly Linked List) where you can only move forward because the rickshaw-puller is only at the front.

## 1.3 Salient Properties of a Doubly Linked List

| Property | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Bidirectional** | Each node knows both its predecessor and successor. |
| **Dynamic Size** | Grows and shrinks at runtime; no pre-allocation needed. |
| **Non-Contiguous** | Nodes are scattered in heap memory; linked via pointers. |
| **Head-Sentinel Optional** | Many implementations use a dummy/sentinel head node to simplify edge cases. |
| **Symmetric Operations** | Operations at front and rear can both be $O(1)$ if tail pointer is maintained. |
| **Extra Memory** | Each node costs an extra pointer ($8$ bytes on a $64$-bit system) compared to SLL. |

## 1.4 Why Not Just Use Arrays?

> [!NOTE]
> Arrays have a fixed capacity defined at compile time (or costly resizing at runtime). Insertion and deletion at the beginning of an array of $n$ elements requires $O(n)$ shifts. The DLL, in contrast, performs these operations in $O(1)$ time once the position pointer is known, at the cost of pointer overhead and loss of random access ($O(n)$ to reach the $k^{th}$ element).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Node

A DLL node is a composite structure stored in the heap. For a $64$-bit architecture with `int` taking $4$ bytes and pointers taking $8$ bytes each, a node occupies **20 bytes** (plus any padding the compiler may add for alignment, often **24 bytes**).

$$\text{NodeSize} = \text{sizeof(data)} + 2 \times \text{sizeof(pointer)}$$

$$\text{NodeSize} = 4 + 2 \times 8 = 20 \text{ bytes (minimum, unaligned)}$$

$$\text{Memory for n nodes} = n \times 24 = 24n \text{ bytes (aligned on GCC)}$$

## 2.2 Operational Core — The "Why" and "How" of Each Operation

### 2.2.1 Insertion Logic (Universal Pattern)

Every insertion, regardless of position, follows a **4-link-update** choreography. Consider inserting a new node `N` between nodes `A` and `B`:

1. **Step 1 — Wire the new node forward:** `N->next = A->next;` (point N to B)
2. **Step 2 — Wire the new node backward:** `N->prev = A;` (point N to A)
3. **Step 3 — Wire B backward:** `if (A->next != NULL) A->next->prev = N;` (tell B that its predecessor is N)
4. **Step 4 — Wire A forward:** `A->next = N;` (tell A that its successor is N)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order matters!** If you change `A->next` before saving its value in Step 1, you lose the pointer to B and create a memory leak. The above order is the **KTU-recommended safe sequence**.

### 2.2.2 Deletion Logic

Deleting a node `D` (where `P` is previous and `N` is next):

1. Bypass: `P->next = N;`
2. Bypass back: `if (N != NULL) N->prev = P;`
3. Free: `free(D);`

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Time Complexity Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **gold standard** for KTU 2024 board answers. Memorize the **asymptotic complexities** verbatim.

| Operation | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List (No Tail) | Doubly Linked List (With Tail) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Access by index $k$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\min(k, n-k))$ (opt) |
| Search for value | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Insert at **head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at **tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert after node $X$ | $O(1)$ (given $X$) | $O(1)$ (given $X$) | $O(1)$ (given $X$) |
| Delete **head** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete **tail** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete node $X$ | $O(1)$ (given $X$ and its prev) | $O(1)$ (given $X$) | $O(1)$ (given $X$) |
| Reverse traversal | **Not Possible** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ |
| Extra memory per node | $1$ pointer | $2$ pointers | $2$ pointers |

### 2.3.1 Space Complexity Master Equation

$$S_{DLL}(n) = n \times (D + 2P) = n \cdot D + 2nP$$

where $D$ = size of data field, $P$ = size of pointer (typically **$8$ bytes** on a $64$-bit system).

### 2.3.2 Pointer Count Invariant

In any valid DLL of $n \geq 1$ nodes:

$$\text{Total prev-pointers pointing to valid nodes} = n - 1$$

$$\text{Total next-pointers pointing to valid nodes} = n - 1$$

The remaining $2$ pointers (head's `prev` and tail's `next`) are **NULL** (or point to a sentinel).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> DLLs are the backbone of many production systems:
> - **Browser History Navigation:** Back/Forward buttons use a doubly linked stack.
> - **LRU Cache Implementation:** Hash map + DLL gives $O(1)$ access, insertion, and eviction.
> - **Undo/Redo Functionality:** Each state is a node, prev = undo, next = redo.
> - **Music Player Playlists:** Skip forward and backward in $O(1)$.
> - **Operating System Process Scheduling:** Doubly linked run-queues in the Linux kernel.
> - **Text Editor Buffers:** Gap buffers are essentially circular DLLs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Insertion at the Beginning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Given an existing DLL with `head` pointer and $n$ nodes, insert a new node containing data value $x$ at the **front** of the list.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Initial State:** List = `[10] <-> [20] <-> [30]`, head points to `[10]`.

**Goal:** Insert `[5]` such that final list = `[5] <-> [10] <-> [20] <-> [30]`.

Let $N$ = new node, $H$ = current head, $\text{NULL}$ = empty pointer.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & N = (\text{struct Node*}) \text{malloc}(\text{sizeof}(\text{struct Node})) \\
& \text{Allocate memory for the new node on the heap.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & N \rightarrow \text{data} = x \\
& \text{Store the payload value in the data field.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & N \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{NULL} \\
& \text{The new node has no predecessor (it is becoming the new head).} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & N \rightarrow \text{next} = H \\
& \text{The new node's next pointer must reference the OLD head node.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{if } (H \neq \text{NULL}) \; H \rightarrow \text{prev} = N \\
& \text{If the list was non-empty, the OLD head's prev must now point to N.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{head} = N \\
& \text{Finally, update the external head pointer to the new node.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Verified C Implementation (KTU Board Style)

```c
void insertAtBeginning(struct Node** head, int x) {
    struct Node* N = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    N->data = x;
    N->prev = NULL;
    N->next = *head;        // Step 4
    if (*head != NULL)      // Step 5
        (*head)->prev = N;
    *head = N;              // Step 6
}
```

### 3.1.3 Pointer Diagram (Before and After)

**Before:** `NULL <- [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] -> NULL`, head → [10]

**After:** `NULL <- [5] <-> [10] <-> [20] <-> [30] -> NULL`, head → [5]

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Insertion at the End

**Problem:** Insert a new node at the tail. Assume a `tail` pointer is maintained for $O(1)$ efficiency.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & N \rightarrow \text{prev} = \text{tail} \\
& \text{N's predecessor is the current last node.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & N \rightarrow \text{next} = \text{NULL} \\
& \text{N has no successor — it becomes the new tail.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{if } (\text{tail} \neq \text{NULL}) \; \text{tail} \rightarrow \text{next} = N \\
& \text{If the list was not empty, the OLD tail's next is now N.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{tail} = N \\
& \text{Update the external tail pointer.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If a tail pointer is **not** maintained, you must traverse the entire list:

$$T_{\text{insertAtEnd (no tail)}} = O(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} 1$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Deletion of a Given Node

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Delete a node `D` (whose pointer is given) from a DLL. List may be empty (edge case).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 0:} \quad & \text{if } (D = \text{NULL}) \; \text{return} \\
& \text{Null-safety check: do nothing if pointer is NULL.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{if } (D \rightarrow \text{prev} \neq \text{NULL}) \\
& \quad D \rightarrow \text{prev} \rightarrow \text{next} = D \rightarrow \text{next} \\
& \text{Bypass D: predecessor's next now skips D.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{else} \\
& \quad \text{head} = D \rightarrow \text{next} \\
& \text{If D was the head, move the head pointer forward.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{if } (D \rightarrow \text{next} \neq \text{NULL}) \\
& \quad D \rightarrow \text{next} \rightarrow \text{prev} = D \rightarrow \text{prev} \\
& \text{Bypass D backward: successor's prev now skips D.} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{free}(D) \\
& \text{Release the memory back to the heap.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Traversal Complexity Derivation

### 3.4.1 Forward Traversal (Full List)

The traversal visits each node exactly once. For a list of $n$ nodes, the work performed is:

$$T_{\text{forward}}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} C_{\text{visit}} = n \cdot C_{\text{visit}} = O(n)$$

where $C_{\text{visit}}$ is the constant cost of processing one node (data access, pointer dereference, comparison).

### 3.4.2 Accessing the $k^{th}$ Element (0-indexed)

Without any optimization, you walk from the head:

$$T_{\text{access}}(k) = k \cdot C_{\text{step}} = O(k)$$

The worst case is reaching the last element:

$$T_{\text{access, worst}} = (n-1) \cdot C_{\text{step}} = O(n)$$

### 3.4.3 Smart Access (Bidirectional Optimization)

In a DLL with a tail pointer, you can choose the shorter path:

$$T_{\text{access, smart}}(k) = O\!\left(\min(k, \, n - 1 - k)\right)$$

For example, in a list of $100$ nodes, accessing index $5$ takes $5$ steps from the head, but accessing index $95$ takes only $4$ steps from the tail.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Complete Operation Analysis

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Auxiliary Space |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Insert at head | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail (with tail) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail (no tail) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at position $k$ | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(k)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete head | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete tail (with tail) | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete arbitrary node | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Search | $\Omega(1)$ | $\Theta(n/2)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Reverse traversal | $\Omega(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Compliance

> [!NOTE]
> All node identifiers below are alphanumeric (e.g., `n1`, `n2`) and avoid reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`). Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Single Node Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PREV["PREV POINTER"] --> NP["NULL OR ADDRESS OF PREVIOUS NODE"]
    DATA["DATA FIELD INT VARIABLE"] --> DVAL["STORES USER PAYLOAD EXAMPLE 42"]
```
