```mermaid
    NP --> META["META EACH POINTER IS 8 BYTES ON 64 BIT"]
    DVAL --> META
    NN --> META
    META --> TOT["TOTAL NODE SIZE 24 BYTES ALIGNED"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — DLL with Three Nodes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["HEAD POINTER EXTERNAL"] --> N1["NODE 1 DATA 10 PREV NULL NEXT NODE2"]
    N1 --> N2["NODE 2 DATA 20 PREV NODE1 NEXT NODE3"]
    N2 --> N3["NODE 3 DATA 30 PREV NODE2 NEXT NULL"]
    N3 --> T["TAIL POINTER EXTERNAL"]
    N1 -.BIDIRECTIONAL.-> N2
    N2 -.BIDIRECTIONAL.-> N1
    N2 -.BIDIRECTIONAL.-> N3
    N3 -.BIDIRECTIONAL.-> N2
```

## 4.4 Operational Flow — Insertion at Beginning (State Machine)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STATE1: INITIAL LIST HEAD TO NODE A
    STATE1: STATE1 LIST HAS NODES A B C
    STATE1 --> STATE2: ALLOCATE NEW NODE N
    STATE2: STATE2 N CREATED DATA X
    STATE2 --> STATE3: SET N NEXT TO HEAD
    STATE3: STATE3 N POINTS FORWARD TO A
    STATE3 --> STATE4: SET HEAD PREV TO N
    STATE4: STATE4 A RECOGNIZES N AS PREDECESSOR
    STATE4 --> STATE5: UPDATE HEAD TO N
    STATE5: STATE5 N IS NEW HEAD LIST N A B C
    STATE5 --> [*]: OPERATION COMPLETE O OF 1
```

## 4.5 Operational Flow — Deletion of an Arbitrary Node

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE
    IDLE: IDLE LIST PRED NODE Q SUCC
    IDLE --> CHECK_PREV: TARGET NODE D RECEIVED
    CHECK_PREV: CHECKPREV IS D PRED NULL
    CHECK_PREV --> BYPASS_PREV: NO
    BYPASS_PREV: BYPASSPREV PRED NEXT SUCC
    BYPASS_PREV --> CHECK_NEXT
    CHECK_PREV --> UPDATE_HEAD: YES MOVE HEAD TO SUCC
    UPDATE_HEAD: UPDATEHEAD HEAD SUCC
    UPDATE_HEAD --> CHECK_NEXT
    CHECK_NEXT: CHECKNEXT IS D NEXT NULL
    CHECK_NEXT --> BYPASS_NEXT: NO
    BYPASS_NEXT: BYPASSNEXT SUCC PREV PRED
    BYPASS_NEXT --> FREE_NODE
    CHECK_NEXT --> FREE_NODE: YES
    UPDATE_HEAD --> FREE_NODE
    FREE_NODE: FREENODE FREE D RELEASE HEAP
    FREE_NODE --> [*]: OPERATION COMPLETE O OF 1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> **Differentiate between a Singly Linked List and a Doubly Linked List. List any two advantages of a Doubly Linked List.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pointers per node | $1$ (`next`) | $2$ (`prev`, `next`) |
| Traversal direction | Forward only | Forward and backward |
| Memory per node | Less ($D + P$ bytes) | More ($D + 2P$ bytes) |
| Delete given node | $O(n)$ (need predecessor) | $O(1)$ (node carries both links) |

**Two advantages of DLL:**
1. **Bidirectional Traversal** — Can be traversed in both directions in $O(n)$ time, enabling reverse iteration.
2. **Efficient Deletion** — A node can be deleted in $O(1)$ if the pointer to it is given, since both `prev` and `next` are accessible.

*(Marks: 1 for table, 1 per advantage)*

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
> **What is a sentinel/dummy node in a doubly linked list? How does it simplify insertion and deletion operations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **sentinel (or dummy/header) node** is an extra node that does **not store any user data**; it is permanently placed at the head of the list. Its purpose is to eliminate special-case logic for the first and last nodes.

**Simplifications:**
1. **No NULL checks** — The sentinel's `prev` and the last node's `next` form a circular DLL, so no pointer is ever NULL during insertion/deletion.
2. **Uniform code path** — The same 4-link-updating logic works for inserting at the head, tail, or middle, reducing code branching and bugs.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 2 for simplifications)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper aligned with 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern]**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### ❓ Question A (Choice 1)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure of a Doubly Linked List node. Write a C function to insert a new node at the **end** of a DLL. Assume the list is non-circular and no tail pointer is maintained.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a C function to **delete a node with a given data value** from a doubly linked list. Discuss the time complexity.

---

#### Model Solution for A(a)

**Node Structure (1 mark):**

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* prev;
    struct Node* next;
};
```

**Insertion at End — Step-by-Step (6 marks):**

```c
void insertAtEnd(struct Node** head, int x) {
    // Step 1: Allocate new node
    struct Node* N = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    N->data = x;
    N->next = NULL;          // It will be the new tail
    
    // Step 2: Special case — empty list
    if (*head == NULL) {
        N->prev = NULL;
        *head = N;
        return;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Traverse to the last node
    struct Node* temp = *head;
    while (temp->next != NULL)
        temp = temp->next;
    
    // Step 4: Wire up the new node
    temp->next = N;          // Old tail's next now points to N
    N->prev = temp;          // N's prev points to old tail
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**
1. Allocate a new node `N` and assign data.
2. If the list is empty, make `N` the head.
3. Otherwise, walk the list to find the current last node (`temp->next == NULL`).
4. Set `temp->next = N` (forward link) and `N->prev = temp` (backward link).

**Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ since we must traverse the entire list to reach the tail.

#### Model Solution for A(b)

**C Function for Deletion by Value (5 marks):**

```c
void deleteByValue(struct Node** head, int key) {
    struct Node* temp = *head;
    
    // Step 1: Find the node with the given key
    while (temp != NULL && temp->data != key)
        temp = temp->next;
    
    // Step 2: Key not found
    if (temp == NULL) {
        printf("Value %d not found in the list.\n", key);
        return;
    }
    
    // Step 3: Bypass temp from the chain
    if (temp->prev != NULL)
        temp->prev->next = temp->next;
    else
        *head = temp->next;   // Deleting the head node
    
    if (temp->next != NULL)
        temp->next->prev = temp->prev;
    
    // Step 4: Free the node
    free(temp);
}
```

**Complexity Analysis (2 marks):**

$$T(n) = O(n) \text{ for search} + O(1) \text{ for unlink} = O(n)$$

- **Search phase:** $O(n)$ worst case to locate the node.
- **Unlink phase:** $O(1)$ — only constant-time pointer manipulations.
- **Auxiliary Space:** $O(1)$.

---

### ❓ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the **time complexity** of all major operations in Singly and Doubly Linked Lists using a comparison table. State two **real-world applications** of DLLs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a C function to **reverse a doubly linked list in place** (without allocating a new list). Analyze its complexity.

---

#### Model Solution for B(a)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Operation | Singly Linked List | Doubly Linked List |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Insert at head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Insert at tail | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ if tail maintained, else $O(n)$ |
| Delete head | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| Delete tail | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ if tail maintained, else $O(n)$ |
| Delete given node pointer | $O(n)$ to find prev | $O(1)$ |
| Reverse traversal | Not possible | $O(n)$ |
| Memory per node | $D + P$ | $D + 2P$ |

**Two Real-World Applications (3 marks):**

1. **Browser Navigation History:** The "Back" and "Forward" buttons are implemented as a DLL. Each visited page is a node; the current page is highlighted. Pressing Back moves the current pointer to the `prev` node; Forward moves to the `next` node — both in $O(1)$.

2. **LRU (Least Recently Used) Cache:** A combination of a **Hash Map** and a **Doubly Linked List** is used to achieve $O(1)$ lookup, insertion, and eviction. The DLL maintains the order of usage; the hash map provides $O(1)$ key-to-node lookup. When a cache miss occurs, the tail node (least recently used) is evicted in $O(1)$.

#### Model Solution for B(b)

**C Function to Reverse DLL In-Place (5 marks):**

```c
void reverseDLL(struct Node** head) {
    struct Node* curr = *head;
    struct Node* temp = NULL;
    
    // Traverse the list and swap prev and next for every node
    while (curr != NULL) {
        temp = curr->prev;          // Save current prev
        curr->prev = curr->next;    // Swap: prev becomes next
        curr->next = temp;          // Swap: next becomes old prev
        curr = curr->prev;          // Move to the "next" node (which is now in prev)
    }
    
    // Update head pointer to the original tail
    if (temp != NULL)
        *head = temp->prev;
}
```

**Complexity Analysis (2 marks):**

- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ — single pass through the list, swapping 2 pointers per node.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — only constant auxiliary variables.
- **In-Place:** Yes, no new nodes are allocated; only pointer directions are flipped.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Link Order:** In DLL insertion, students frequently write `head->prev = N` **before** setting `N->next = head`. This severs the link to the old list and causes data loss. Always save old links **first**.
> 2. **NULL Checks:** Forgetting the `if (head != NULL)` check in `insertAtBeginning` causes a segmentation fault when the list is empty. Examiners award **0 marks** for code that crashes on the first test case.
> 3. **Memory Leak:** Omitting `free(temp)` in deletion is a memory leak. KTU 2024 Scheme has dedicated rubric lines for memory management; up to **2 marks** can be deducted.
> 4. **Off-by-One in Traversal:** Using `while (temp->next != NULL)` vs `while (temp != NULL)` in different operations must be precise. Mixing them up will cost marks.
> 5. **Confusing "DLL" with "Circular DLL":** A standard DLL has `head->prev = NULL` and `tail->next = NULL`. A **circular DLL** has no NULLs — the tail's next points back to the head. Examiners deduct marks for conflating the two.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition (1-liner):** A Doubly Linked List is a linear data structure where each node contains `data`, `prev`, and `next` pointers, enabling bidirectional traversal.
- **Node Size:** $24$ bytes on a $64$-bit aligned system ($4$ bytes data + $2 \times 8$ bytes pointers + $4$ bytes padding).
- **Empty List:** `head = NULL`.
- **Single Node:** `head != NULL`, `head->prev = NULL`, `head->next = NULL`.
- **Insertion Rule of Thumb:** Always wire the new node's `prev` and `next` **before** updating the neighbors' pointers.
- **Deletion Rule of Thumb:** Bypass the node from the chain, then `free()` it. Always check for NULL before dereferencing.
- **Time Complexity Highlights to Memorize:**
  - Insert at head: $O(1)$
  - Delete a node given its pointer: $O(1)$
  - Search: $O(n)$
  - Reverse a DLL: $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space
- **SLL vs DLL:** DLL trades extra memory ($8$ bytes/node) for $O(1)$ deletion of arbitrary nodes and backward traversal.
- **Sentinel Node Trick:** Eliminates all NULL checks; the list becomes a closed loop.
- **Real-World Uses:** Browser history, LRU cache, undo-redo systems, music player playlists, OS process queues.
- **Common Exam Trap:** Question asks "delete the last node" without a tail pointer → answer is $O(n)$ because you must traverse to find the predecessor.
- **Mnemonic for Insertion Order:** **N**ew node links first, **N**eighbors links last → "N before N".
- **Circular DLL Bonus:** If asked, mention that a circular DLL has `head->prev = tail` and `tail->next = head`, which is useful for round-robin scheduling.

> **End of Module 2 — Doubly Linked List Notes | PCEOT303 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Circular Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circular Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Circular Linked List (CLL)** is a variation of the linked list data structure in which the last node does not point to a `NULL` terminator. Instead, the **next** pointer (or **prev** pointer in the doubly variant) of the final node loops back to the **first node** (head) of the list, forming a closed circular chain of nodes.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCEOT303 – Module 2)** syllabus, the Circular Linked List is introduced immediately after the Singly and Doubly Linked Lists, as it is the natural topological extension of linear linked structures. The two formally recognized variants in the syllabus are:

1. **Singly Circular Linked List (SCLL)** – Every node has exactly one pointer (`next`) that points to its successor, and the last node’s `next` points back to the head.
2. **Doubly Circular Linked List (DCLL)** – Every node has two pointers (`next` and `prev`), and the last node’s `next` points to the head, while the head’s `prev` points to the last node.

Mathematically, a Circular Linked List with $n$ nodes can be expressed as a cyclic graph:
$$G_{CLL} = (V, E), \quad |V| = n, \quad |E| = n$$
where the edge set forms a single Hamiltonian cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** A Circular Linked List may be maintained using either a **head pointer** (points to the first node) or a **tail pointer** (points to the last node). Most KTU textbook problems prefer the **tail pointer** approach because it enables $O(1)$ insertion at both the front and the end of the list.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Round-Table Meeting:**
> Imagine a group of people seated around a circular table. There is no “end” of the table — each person has exactly one person on their right (the successor) and one on their left (the predecessor). If you ask any person, “Who is after you?”, they will always be able to point to someone — no one says “nobody”. The Circular Linked List behaves in exactly the same way: the “end” of the list wraps around to the “beginning”, so traversal can continue indefinitely unless we explicitly check for the start node.

**Another Analogy — The Musical Chairs Cycle:**
> Think of a music playlist set to **Repeat One**. The last song, instead of stopping, seamlessly restarts from the first song. That endless loop is the essence of a circular structure — there is no terminal `NULL` state.

---

## 1.3 Node Structure (Memory Footprint)

For a **Singly Circular Linked List**, each node occupies memory equivalent to:
$$M_{node} = M_{data} + M_{next} + M_{overhead}$$
where $M_{data}$ is the data payload, $M_{next}$ is the address (pointer) field, and $M_{overhead}$ is allocator metadata (typically **8 to 16 bytes** in 64-bit systems).

For a **Doubly Circular Linked List**, each node occupies:
$$M_{node} = M_{data} + M_{next} + M_{prev} + M_{overhead}$$

In modern 64-bit systems with 8-byte pointers:
- **SCLL Node Size:** $\approx$ **16 bytes** (data + next) + overhead
- **DCLL Node Size:** $\approx$ **24 bytes** (data + next + prev) + overhead

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU questions frequently test the *last node’s next pointer*. Always state: *“In a circular linked list, the next pointer of the last node points back to the head node — it never becomes `NULL`.”*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy

A Circular Linked List is characterized by the following invariants:

- **Circular Invariant 1:** The `next` pointer of the last node always references the first node (head).
- **Circular Invariant 2:** There is **no `NULL` pointer** anywhere in the list (in a non-empty list).
- **Circular Invariant 3:** A traversal loop terminates only when a visited pointer equals the start pointer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for Examiners:** A common KTU board pitfall is to test whether students know that the `tail->next` field always equals `head`, not `NULL`. Even a one-node circular list is valid: `head->next = head`.

---

## 2.2 Operations and Their Time Complexities

The following structured logic sequence applies to the standard operations on a Circular Linked List:

### 2.2.1 Traversal
- **Step 1:** Initialize a temporary pointer `ptr = head`.
- **Step 2:** If `head == NULL`, the list is empty — terminate.
- **Step 3:** Use a `do-while` loop (preferred over `while` to handle the head case correctly):
  - Process `ptr->data`.
  - Move `ptr = ptr->next`.
  - Continue **while** `ptr != head`.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ is the number of nodes.

### 2.2.2 Insertion at the Front (Head)
- **Step 1:** If the list is empty, create a new node and set `head = newNode; newNode->next = head`.
- **Step 2:** Otherwise, traverse to the last node (the node whose `next == head`).
- **Step 3:** Set `newNode->next = head`.
- **Step 4:** Set `last->next = newNode`.
- **Step 5:** Update `head = newNode`.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ without a tail pointer; **$O(1)$ with a tail pointer**.

### 2.2.3 Insertion at the End (Tail)
- **Step 1:** If the list is empty, follow the head-creation logic.
- **Step 2:** Otherwise, traverse to the current last node.
- **Step 3:** Set `newNode->next = head`.
- **Step 4:** Set `last->next = newNode`.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$ without a tail pointer; **$O(1)$ with a tail pointer**.

### 2.2.4 Deletion of a Node (by value)
- **Step 1:** If the head node holds the value, advance the head and patch the last node’s `next` to the new head.
- **Step 2:** Otherwise, traverse with two pointers (`prev` and `curr`) until `curr->data == key`.
- **Step 3:** Set `prev->next = curr->next`.
- **Step 4:** Free `curr`.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Tail Pointer Matters in CLL:**
> In a Singly Linked List, reaching the last node requires a full $O(n)$ traversal because the head cannot go backwards. In a Circular Linked List, the situation is identical — except that we can now keep a persistent `tail` pointer (last node) so that *both* front and end insertions become $O(1)$. This is the single most important KTU high-yield concept about CLLs.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Operation                        | Without Tail Pointer | With Tail Pointer | Space Complexity |
|----------------------------------|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Create empty list                | $O(1)$               | $O(1)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Traversal (full)                 | $O(n)$               | $O(n)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Insert at front (head)           | $O(n)$               | $O(1)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Insert at end (tail)            | $O(n)$               | $O(1)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Insert after a given node        | $O(1)$               | $O(1)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Delete from front (head)         | $O(n)$               | $O(1)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Delete from end (tail)           | $O(n)$               | $O(n)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Delete a node by value           | $O(n)$               | $O(n)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Search for a value               | $O(n)$               | $O(n)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Count nodes                      | $O(n)$               | $O(n)$            | $O(1)$           |
| Total nodes (n) memory footprint | $n \cdot M_{node}$   | $n \cdot M_{node}$| —                |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** $M_{node}$ denotes the per-node memory size. The vertical bar for "such that" or "where" inside descriptions is intentionally replaced with `where` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax in strict parsers.

---

## 2.4 Comparison with Linear Linked List

| Property                | Singly Linked List             | Circular Linked List                |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Last node `next`        | `NULL`                         | `head`                              |
| Traversal termination   | `ptr->next == NULL`            | `ptr->next == head`                 |
| Risk of infinite loop   | None                           | High (must use `do-while` or flag)  |
| Tail pointer benefit    | Insignificant                  | $O(1)$ front + end insertion        |
| Real-world use          | Stacks, undo logs              | Round-robin scheduling, buffers     |
| Memory per node         | $M_{data} + M_{next}$          | $M_{data} + M_{next}$ (same)        |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

Circular Linked Lists are not merely academic constructs. They power several mission-critical engineering systems:

1. **Operating System Round-Robin Scheduling:** The OS maintains a circular list of ready processes. After each time quantum, the scheduler moves to the `next` process — when it reaches the end, it seamlessly loops back to the head.
2. **Circular Buffers (Ring Buffers):** Embedded systems, audio processing pipelines, and network packet queues use circular linked/array buffers to enable continuous streaming with fixed memory.
3. **Multiplayer Turn-Based Games:** Player turns rotate cyclically using a circular linked structure.
4. **Josephus Problem:** A classical competitive-programming problem (frequently asked in KTU labs) that uses circular linked lists for elegant $O(n)$ solutions.
5. **Circular Memory Pools in Firmware:** Real-time embedded firmware uses circular allocation to avoid fragmentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive, step-by-step analytical solutions for the most important CLL operations. Since this is a **Computer Science** course (PCEOT303), algorithm pseudocode and trace tables are explicitly required by the KTU 2024 module outcomes.

---

## 3.1 Insertion at the End of a Singly Circular Linked List (with Tail Pointer)

### 3.1.1 Pre-Conditions
- List has at least one node.
- `tail` is a persistent pointer to the last node.
- `head = tail->next` (in some implementations); alternatively `head` is tracked separately.

### 3.1.2 Algorithm (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Algorithm: `insertAtEnd(tail, value)`**

1. **Step 1 — Allocate a new node:**
   - Allocate memory of size $M_{node}$.
   - Set `newNode->data = value`.
   - Set `newNode->next = NULL` (temporary initialization).

2. **Step 2 — Check if the list is empty:**
   - If `tail == NULL`, then:
     - Set `newNode->next = newNode` (the new node points to itself).
     - Set `tail = newNode`.
     - Return `tail`.

3. **Step 3 — Splice the new node at the end:**
   - Set `newNode->next = tail->next` (which is `head`).
   - Set `tail->next = newNode`.
   - Set `tail = newNode`.
   - Return `tail`.

### 3.1.3 C-Style Implementation (Reference)

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};

struct Node* insertAtEnd(struct Node* tail, int value) {
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = NULL;

    if (tail == NULL) {
        newNode->next = newNode;   /* points to itself */
        tail = newNode;
        return tail;
    }

    newNode->next = tail->next;   /* newNode -> head */
    tail->next = newNode;         /* old tail -> newNode */
    tail = newNode;               /* update tail */
    return tail;
}
```

### 3.1.4 Dry-Run Trace Table

Assume we have a list: `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> (back to 10)` with `tail` pointing to 30.

We now insert `40` at the end.

| Step | Variable State (Visual)                                                  | Action / Reason                          |
|------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 1    | Allocate `newNode` with `data = 40`, `next = NULL`                       | Memory allocation                        |
| 2    | `tail != NULL`, so skip empty-list branch                                | The list is not empty                    |
| 3    | `newNode->next = tail->next = 10`                                       | `newNode` now points to head             |
| 4    | `tail->next = newNode` → `30->next = 40`                                 | Old tail now points to new node          |
| 5    | `tail = newNode` → `tail` now points to `40`                             | Update tail pointer                      |
| 6    | Final list: `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> (back to 10)`                       | Circular invariant preserved             |

**Visual Chain Transformation:**

$$10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow \text{NULL (before)}$$

After step 3:

$$10 \rightarrow 20 \rightarrow 30 \rightarrow 40$$
$$\uparrow \hspace{5em} \uparrow$$
$$head \hspace{4.5em} tail$$

With the final circular link: $40 \rightarrow 10$.

---

## 3.2 Deletion of a Node by Value (Singly Circular Linked List)

### 3.2.1 Algorithm (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Algorithm: `deleteNode(tail, key)`**

1. **Step 1 — Empty list check:**
   - If `tail == NULL`, return `NULL` (nothing to delete).

2. **Step 2 — Single-node list check:**
   - If `tail == tail->next` (only one node) and `tail->data == key`:
     - Free the node.
     - Return `NULL`.

3. **Step 3 — Head deletion case:**
   - Let `head = tail->next`.
   - If `head->data == key`:
     - Set `tail->next = head->next` (skip the head).
     - Free `head`.
     - Return `tail` (unchanged, unless the list became empty — then return `NULL`).

4. **Step 4 — General case (using two pointers):**
   - Initialize `prev = tail`, `curr = tail->next` (head).
   - Loop while `curr != tail` and `curr->data != key`:
     - Set `prev = curr`.
     - Set `curr = curr->next`.
   - If `curr == tail` and `curr->data == key` (tail deletion):
     - Set `prev->next = curr->next` (head).
     - Free `curr`.
     - Return `prev` (new tail).
   - If `curr != tail` (interior or head already handled):
     - Set `prev->next = curr->next`.
     - Free `curr`.
     - Return `tail` (unchanged).

5. **Step 5 — Key not found:** Return `tail` unchanged.

### 3.2.2 C-Style Implementation (Reference)

```c
struct Node* deleteNode(struct Node* tail, int key) {
    if (tail == NULL) return NULL;

    struct Node* head = tail->next;
    struct Node* curr = head;
    struct Node* prev = tail;

    /* Single-node case */
    if (curr == tail && curr->data == key) {
        free(curr);
        return NULL;
    }

    /* Head deletion */
    if (head->data == key) {
        tail->next = head->next;
        free(head);
        return tail;
    }

    /* Traverse to find the key */
    while (curr != tail && curr->data != key) {
        prev = curr;
        curr = curr->next;
    }

    /* Tail deletion */
    if (curr == tail) {
        prev->next = curr->next;
        free(curr);
        return prev;
    }

    /* Interior deletion */
    if (curr->data == key) {
        prev->next = curr->next;
        free(curr);
    }

    return tail;
}
```

### 3.2.3 Dry-Run Trace

Starting list: `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> 40 -> (back to 10)`, `tail = 40`. Delete `30`.

| Step | curr              | prev              | Action                                    |
|------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Init | `head = 10`       | `tail = 40`       | `prev = 40`, `curr = 10`                  |
| 1    | `curr = 10`       | `prev = 40`       | `10 != 40` and `10 != 30`, so advance     |
| 2    | `curr = 20`       | `prev = 10`       | `20 != 40` and `20 != 30`, so advance     |
| 3    | `curr = 30`       | `prev = 20`       | `30 == 30`, exit loop                     |
| 4    | `curr = 30`       | `prev = 20`       | `prev->next = curr->next` → `20->next = 40` |
| 5    | Free `30`         | —                 | Memory deallocated                        |
| 6    | Final list        | —                 | `10 -> 20 -> 40 -> (back to 10)`          |

**Result:** $n$ decreased by 1. The circular invariant is preserved: $40 \rightarrow 10$.

---

## 3.3 Josephus Problem (Classic KTU Lab Application)

The Josephus problem is a classic $O(n)$ application of a circular linked list, frequently appearing in KTU continuous-evaluation labs.

**Problem Statement:**
$n$ people stand in a circle. Starting from the first person, every $k$-th person is eliminated. Continue until only one person remains. Find the survivor's position.

**Closed-Form Recurrence (Analytical Solution):**

Let $J(n, k)$ denote the survivor's position (0-indexed). The recurrence is:

$$J(n, k) = \left( J(n-1, k) + k \right) \bmod n, \quad J(1, k) = 0$$

**Step-by-Step Derivation for $n = 7$, $k = 3$:**

| $n$ | $J(n, 3)$                                                          | Value |
|-----|--------------------------------------------------------------------|-------|
| 1   | $J(1,3)$                                                           | 0     |
| 2   | $(0 + 3) \bmod 2$                                                 | 1     |
| 3   | $(1 + 3) \bmod 3$                                                 | 1     |
| 4   | $(1 + 3) \bmod 4$                                                 | 0     |
| 5   | $(0 + 3) \bmod 5$                                                 | 3     |
| 6   | $(3 + 3) \bmod 6$                                                 | 0     |
| 7   | $(0 + 3) \bmod 7$                                                 | 3     |

**Answer:** $J(7, 3) = 3$ (0-indexed), meaning the 4th person survives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** While the recurrence solution runs in $O(n)$ time and $O(1)$ space, the CLL-based simulation runs in $O(n \cdot k)$ time. The recurrence is preferred for large $n$ and is what KTU 2024 Scheme questions typically test.

---

## 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Operation | Best Case | Average Case | Worst Case | Space   | Notes                                   |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|---------|-----------------------------------------|
| Insert at front (with tail) | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$       | $O(1)$     | $O(1)$  | Use tail pointer                        |
| Insert at end (with tail)   | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$       | $O(1)$     | $O(1)$  | Splice newNode between tail and head    |
| Delete at head              | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$       | $O(1)$     | $O(1)$  | Requires tail pointer                   |
| Delete at tail              | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$       | $O(n)$     | $O(1)$  | Must traverse to find predecessor       |
| Search                      | $O(1)$ | $O(n/2)$     | $O(n)$     | $O(1)$  | Probabilistic, worst case scans all     |
| Traversal                   | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$       | $O(n)$     | $O(1)$  | Use `do-while` loop                     |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

This section provides multiple Mermaid block diagrams illustrating the structural and operational aspects of a Circular Linked List.

---

## 4.1 Singly Circular Linked List — Basic Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[HEAD: 10] -- next --> B[Node: 20]
    B -- next --> C[Node: 30]
    C -- next --> D[TAIL: 40]
    D -- circular next --> A
    T[Tail Pointer] -.-> D
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows show `next` pointers. Notice the curved arrow from `TAIL: 40` looping back to `HEAD: 10` — this is the defining feature of a circular structure. The dashed line represents an external pointer variable (the `tail` we maintain outside the list).

---

## 4.2 Doubly Circular Linked List — Basic Structure

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[HEAD: 10] -- next --> B[Node: 20]
    B -- next --> C[Node: 30]
    C -- next --> D[TAIL: 40]
    D -- next --> A
    A -- prev --> D
    B -- prev --> A
    C -- prev --> B
    D -- prev --> C
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each node has two outgoing edges. The `next` edges go forward (clockwise) and the `prev` edges go backward (counter-clockwise). Both ends loop back, forming two opposing circular chains.

---

## 4.3 Insertion at End — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: insertAtEnd] --> B{Is tail NULL?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Allocate newNode]
    C --> D[newNode.next = newNode]
    D --> E[tail = newNode]
    E --> F[Return tail]
    B -- No --> G[newNode.next = tail.next]
    G --> H[tail.next = newNode]
    H --> I[tail = newNode]
    I --> F
    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Architecture flow explanation:** The control enters `insertAtEnd`. If the list is empty, a special case creates a self-referential node. Otherwise, the new node is spliced *between* the current tail and the head, and the tail pointer is updated.

---

## 4.4 Deletion by Value — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: deleteNode] --> B{Is tail NULL?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Return NULL]
    B -- No --> D{Is there only one node?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Does it match key?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Free node, return NULL]
    E -- No --> G[Return tail unchanged]
    D -- No --> H{Is head the key?}
    H -- Yes --> I[tail.next = head.next, free head]
    I --> J[Return tail]
    H -- No --> K[Traverse with prev and curr]
    K --> L{Found the key?}
    L -- No --> G
    L -- Yes, at tail --> M[prev.next = tail.next, free tail]
    M --> N[Return prev as new tail]
    L -- Yes, interior --> O[prev.next = curr.next, free curr]
    O --> J
    style A fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style N fill:#7ED321,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the flow:** The decision tree branches into four major cases — empty list, single-node match, head deletion, and general interior/tail deletion. The terminal green boxes are the function's return points.

---

## 4.5 Traversal Logic (do-while) — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Init
    Init --> Empty: head == NULL
    Init --> Process: head != NULL
    Process --> Advance: visit ptr
    Advance --> Process: ptr.next != head
    Advance --> Done: ptr.next == head
    Empty --> [*]
    Done --> [*]
```

> **State transition insight:** The traversal enters the `Process` state at least once (this is why `do-while` is used instead of `while`). It keeps advancing until it loops back to the head node, at which point traversal terminates safely without an infinite loop.

---

## 4.6 Memory Layout of a Node (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    Node["Node Structure"]:3
    Data["int data\n(4 bytes)"]:1
    NextPtr["Node* next\n(8 bytes)"]:1
    Padding["Padding\n(4 bytes)"]:1
    Node --- Data
    Node --- NextPtr
    Node --- Padding
```

> **Engineering Note:** In a 64-bit system with 8-byte alignment, a node containing a 4-byte `int` and an 8-byte pointer will have 4 bytes of compiler-inserted padding to maintain alignment, totaling **16 bytes per node** for an SCLL. KTU may test this concept under memory-awareness questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the circular link after deletion:** Many students patch `prev->next = curr->next` correctly but forget that the *tail* must also be updated if the deleted node was the last. This costs **2–3 marks** per KTU board paper.
> 2. **Using `while` instead of `do-while` for traversal:** With a `while` loop, if the head is the only node, the condition `ptr != head` fails *before* processing, leading to incorrect outputs. Always use `do-while`.
> 3. **Confusing `tail->next` with `NULL`:** In a CLL, the last node's `next` is the **head**, never `NULL`. Writing `NULL` in your answer will be marked as a conceptual error.
> 4. **Not freeing memory:** Examiners deduct marks for memory leaks in C-based CLL questions.
> 5. **Ignoring the empty-list case:** Insertion and deletion functions *must* handle the empty-list edge case explicitly.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Circular Linked List. How does it differ from a Singly Linked List?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Circular Linked List** is a linked data structure in which the last node's `next` pointer does not point to `NULL`, but instead points back to the first node (head) of the list, forming a closed loop. It is of two types: **Singly Circular Linked List** and **Doubly Circular Linked List**.
>
> **Differences from a Singly Linked List:**
> - In a Singly Linked List, the last node's `next` is `NULL`; in a Circular Linked List, it points to the **head**.
> - In a Circular Linked List, a **tail pointer** enables $O(1)$ insertion at both ends — a benefit not available in a standard Singly Linked List.
> - Traversal in a Circular Linked List must use a `do-while` loop with a termination condition of `ptr == head`, whereas Singly Linked List uses `ptr->next == NULL`.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**What is a tail pointer? Why is it advantageous in a Circular Linked List?**

**Model Answer:**

> A **tail pointer** is an external pointer variable that always points to the **last node** of a linked list.
>
> **Advantages in a Circular Linked List:**
> - The last node's `next` points to the head, so by maintaining a tail pointer, we have direct $O(1)$ access to *both* the head (via `tail->next`) and the tail.
> - This enables **$O(1)$ insertion at the front** and **$O(1)$ insertion at the end**, eliminating the need for a full $O(n)$ traversal.
> - It also enables **$O(1)$ deletion of the head node** (because the previous node of head *is* the tail), which is impossible without it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and contains sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer *either* the full set A *or* the full set B.

---

### ❑ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Write a C function to insert a node at the end of a Singly Circular Linked List using a tail pointer. Also write a function to display the list.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node* next;
};

/* Insert at end, returns updated tail */
struct Node* insertAtEnd(struct Node* tail, int value) {
    struct Node* newNode = (struct Node*)malloc(sizeof(struct Node));
    if (newNode == NULL) {
        printf("Memory allocation failed\n");
        return tail;
    }
    newNode->data = value;
    newNode->next = newNode;  /* self-loop as default */

    if (tail == NULL) {
        tail = newNode;
        return tail;
    }

    newNode->next = tail->next;   /* head */
    tail->next = newNode;
    tail = newNode;
    return tail;
}

/* Display the list using do-while for circular safety */
void display(struct Node* tail) {
    if (tail == NULL) {
        printf("List is empty\n");
        return;
    }
    struct Node* head = tail->next;
    struct Node* ptr = head;
    printf("Circular List: ");
    do {
        printf("%d -> ", ptr->data);
        ptr = ptr->next;
    } while (ptr != head);
    printf("(back to %d)\n", head->data);
}
```

**Trace Explanation:**

- The function `insertAtEnd` is called multiple times to build the list.
- After inserting `10, 20, 30` sequentially, the list state is `10 -> 20 -> 30 -> (back to 10)` with `tail = 30`.
- The `display` function uses a `do-while` loop, ensuring the head is processed at least once.
- The complexity of `insertAtEnd` is $O(1)$ with a tail pointer.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Josephus Problem. Solve it for $n = 5$ and $k = 2$ using the circular linked list simulation approach.**

**Model Solution:**

> **Josephus Problem Statement:** $n$ people are arranged in a circle. Starting from the first, every $k$-th person is eliminated. The process continues until only one person remains. Find the survivor.
>
> **Recurrence Relation:**
>
> $$J(n, k) = \left( J(n-1, k) + k \right) \bmod n, \quad J(1, k) = 0$$
>
> **Solving for $n = 5$, $k = 2$:**
>
> | $n$ | $J(n, 2)$ Computation       | Result |
> |-----|------------------------------|--------|
> | 1   | Base case                    | 0      |
> | 2   | $(0 + 2) \bmod 2$            | 0      |
> | 3   | $(0 + 2) \bmod 3$            | 2      |
> | 4   | $(2 + 2) \bmod 4$            | 0      |
> | 5   | $(0 + 2) \bmod 5$            | 2      |
>
> **Answer:** $J(5, 2) = 2$ (0-indexed) → the **3rd person** survives.
>
> **CLL Simulation Trace (n=5, k=2):**
> - Initial: `1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> (back to 1)`
> - Eliminate person 2 → `1 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5 -> (back to 1)`
> - Eliminate person 4 → `1 -> 3 -> 5 -> (back to 1)`
> - Eliminate person 1 → `3 -> 5 -> (back to 3)`
> - Eliminate person 5 → `3` (survivor)
>
> This confirms $J(5, 2) = 2$ (0-indexed), i.e., person **3**.

---

### ❑ Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Write a C function to delete a given node (by value) from a Singly Circular Linked List maintained by a tail pointer. Handle all edge cases.**

**Model Solution:**

```c
struct Node* deleteNode(struct Node* tail, int key) {
    if (tail == NULL) {
        printf("List is empty\n");
        return NULL;
    }

    struct Node* head = tail->next;
    struct Node* curr = head;
    struct Node* prev = tail;

    /* Single node case */
    if (curr == tail && curr->data == key) {
        free(curr);
        return NULL;
    }

    /* Head deletion */
    if (head->data == key) {
        tail->next = head->next;
        free(head);
        return tail;
    }

    /* Traverse to find key */
    while (curr != tail && curr->data != key) {
        prev = curr;
        curr = curr->next;
    }

    if (curr == tail) {
        /* Tail deletion */
        prev->next = curr->next;
        free(curr);
        return prev;     /* New tail */
    }

    if (curr->data == key) {
        /* Interior deletion */
        prev->next = curr->next;
        free(curr);
    } else {
        printf("Key %d not found\n", key);
    }

    return tail;
}
```

**Edge Cases Handled:**
1. Empty list (`tail == NULL`).
2. Single-node list.
3. Head node deletion.
4. Tail node deletion.
5. Interior node deletion.
6. Key not present in the list.

**Complexity:** $O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare Singly Circular Linked List and Doubly Circular Linked List. Mention at least four points with memory and complexity considerations.**

**Model Solution:**

> | Property                 | Singly Circular Linked List                  | Doubly Circular Linked List                          |
> |--------------------------|----------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|
> | Pointers per node        | 1 (`next`)                                   | 2 (`next` and `prev`)                                |
> | Memory per node          | $\approx$ 16 bytes (64-bit)                  | $\approx$ 24 bytes (64-bit)                          |
> | Forward traversal        | $O(n)$                                       | $O(n)$                                               |
> | Backward traversal       | Not possible                                 | $O(n)$                                               |
> | Delete a known node      | $O(n)$ (need predecessor)                    | $O(1)$ (predecessor is directly available)           |
> | Insertion at known node  | $O(1)$                                       | $O(1)$                                               |
> | Implementation complexity| Simple                                       | Higher (more pointer updates)                        |
> | Best use case            | Round-robin scheduling, ring buffers         | LRU cache, browser history (forward & back)          |
>
> **Verdict:** SCLL is more memory-efficient, while DCLL offers faster arbitrary deletions and bidirectional traversal at the cost of higher memory and pointer-management complexity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Circular Linked List**

- **Definition:** Last node's `next` points to head (and `head->prev` points to tail in DCLL).
- **Termination Condition:** `ptr == head` (not `ptr == NULL`).
- **Single-node list:** A valid CLL of size 1 has `head->next = head`.
- **Tail Pointer Power:** Enables $O(1)$ insertion at *both* head and tail positions.
- **Empty List Check:** Always test `tail == NULL` (or `head == NULL`) first.
- **Traversal Pattern:** Always use a `do-while` loop, never a `while` loop.
- **Memory per Node:** SCLL = 16 bytes, DCLL = 24 bytes (typical 64-bit systems).
- **Deletion of Tail:** Requires $O(n)$ traversal even with a tail pointer (no `prev` access in SCLL).
- **Deletion of Arbitrary Node in DCLL:** $O(1)$ because predecessor is directly available.
- **Josephus Problem:** Uses CLL; the closed-form recurrence is $J(n,k) = (J(n-1, k) + k) \bmod n$.
- **Real-World Uses:** OS round-robin scheduling, ring buffers in embedded systems, multiplayer turn rotation.
- **Common Pitfall #1:** Writing `tail->next = NULL` in a CLL — *always* set it to `head`.
- **Common Pitfall #2:** Infinite loops in traversal when the termination condition is miscoded.
- **Common Pitfall #3:** Forgetting to free the deleted node's memory.
- **KTU Favourite Question Types:**
  1. Trace-based insertion/deletion in a CLL.
  2. Time-complexity comparison (SLL vs CLL vs DCLL).
  3. Josephus problem (recurrence or simulation).
  4. Application-based question (round-robin, ring buffer).
  5. Conversion between linear and circular linked lists.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stacks and Queues Using Linked List
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stacks and Queues Using Linked List

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Stack** is a linear data structure that follows the **LIFO (Last In, First Out)** principle, where insertion and deletion operations occur at the same end, called the **TOP**. A **Queue** is a linear data structure that follows the **FIFO (First In, First Out)** principle, where insertion occurs at the **REAR** and deletion occurs at the **FRONT**.

When implemented using a **Linked List**, both structures leverage **dynamic memory allocation** via a chain of self-referential `node` structures connected through pointers, eliminating the fixed-size limitation of array-based implementations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEOT303 - Module 2):**
> The student must be able to *implement stack and queue ADTs using a linked list*, and analyze the **time complexity** of every operation. This is a **high-weightage** topic for both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Node Structure — The Building Block

Every element in a linked list–based stack or queue is a `node` containing exactly two fields:

- **DATA field** : Stores the actual user value (integer, character, struct, etc.).
- **NEXT field** : Stores the address (pointer) of the next node in the chain. For the last node, this pointer is set to `NULL`.

$$ \text{node} \;\equiv\; \langle \texttt{data} \mid \texttt{next} \rangle $$

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Stack Analogy — "A Pile of Plates in a Cafeteria"**
> Imagine a spring-loaded plate dispenser. You can only **add** (push) a plate on top, and you can only **remove** (pop) the topmost plate. The plate placed *last* is the *first* one to be used. That is LIFO. The pointer to the **TOP** of the stack is analogous to the spring mechanism that always points to the topmost plate.

> **Queue Analogy — "People Standing in Line for Movie Tickets"**
> The person who *joins first* at the rear of the line is *served first* at the front counter. Newcomers always enter from the **REAR**, and service always happens at the **FRONT**. That is FIFO. In a linked list implementation, `FRONT` is the head and `REAR` is the tail.

### 1.4 Why Use a Linked List Instead of an Array?

| Constraint | Array Implementation | Linked List Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Size** | Fixed at compile time | Dynamic — grows/shrinks at runtime |
| **Overflow** | Possible when array is full | Only on **heap memory exhaustion** |
| **Memory Utilization** | May waste unused slots | Allocates only what is needed |
| **Insert/Delete Cost** | May require shifting elements | Pure pointer manipulation — $O(1)$ at the correct end |
| **Cache Friendliness** | **High** (contiguous memory) | **Low** (scattered nodes) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** Examiners frequently ask: *"Why is a linked list preferred for stacks/queues even though arrays offer better cache performance?"* — The answer is **dynamic resizing** and **guaranteed $O(1)$ insertions/deletions** at the chosen end without any shifting overhead.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The `node` Data Type (C-Style)

A self-referential structure is the heart of every linked list:

```c
struct Node {
    int data;
    struct Node *next;
};
```

- A pointer to this structure (e.g., `struct Node *head`) acts as the **TOP** for a stack and the **FRONT** for a queue.
- A separate tail pointer (e.g., `struct Node *rear`) is maintained in queue implementations to achieve $O(1)$ enqueue.

### 2.2 Stack Using Linked List — Operation Logic

The TOP of the stack is always the **head** of the linked list. Both push and pop occur at the head for optimal $O(1)$ complexity.

#### 2.2.1 PUSH Operation (Insertion at Head)

**Why at the head?** Because we want $O(1)$ — inserting at the tail would require traversing the entire list ($O(n)$).

Step-by-step logic:

1. **Allocate** a new node in heap memory.
2. **Assign** the user's value into `newNode->data`.
3. **Link** `newNode->next = top` (point the new node to the current top).
4. **Update** `top = newNode` (the new node becomes the top).
5. **Increment** the size counter.

#### 2.2.2 POP Operation (Deletion at Head)

1. **Check** for `Underflow` — if `top == NULL`, the stack is empty.
2. **Store** a temporary pointer to the current top: `temp = top`.
3. **Read** the value: `value = top->data`.
4. **Advance** the top: `top = top->next`.
5. **Deallocate** `temp` using `free()`.
6. **Decrement** the size counter.
7. **Return** the saved value.

#### 2.2.3 PEEK Operation

Simply returns `top->data` without modifying the structure — $O(1)$.

### 2.3 Queue Using Linked List — Operation Logic

A queue requires **two pointers**: `front` (for deletion) and `rear` (for insertion).

#### 2.3.1 ENQUEUE Operation (Insertion at Rear)

1. **Allocate** a new node.
2. **Assign** `newNode->data = value` and `newNode->next = NULL`.
3. **Edge case:** If `rear == NULL` (empty queue), set `front = rear = newNode`.
4. **Otherwise:** `rear->next = newNode`, then `rear = newNode`.

#### 2.3.2 DEQUEUE Operation (Deletion at Front)

1. **Check** for `Underflow` — if `front == NULL`, the queue is empty.
2. **Store** `temp = front` and `value = front->data`.
3. **Advance** the front: `front = front->next`.
4. **Edge case:** If `front == NULL` after advancement, set `rear = NULL` (queue is now completely empty).
5. **Deallocate** `temp` and **return** the value.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table** — these are the *exact* time and space complexities KTU examiners expect in 14-mark derivation questions.

| Operation | Stack (Linked List) | Queue (Linked List) | Worst Case | Best Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Push / Enqueue** | Insert at `head` | Insert at `rear` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Pop / Dequeue** | Delete at `head` | Delete at `front` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Peek / Front** | Read `top->data` | Read `front->data` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **isEmpty** | Check `top == NULL` | Check `front == NULL` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Search** | Traverse entire list | Traverse entire list | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Space Used** | $n \cdot \text{sizeof(node)}$ | $n \cdot \text{sizeof(node)}$ | $\Theta(n)$ | — |
| **Overflow** | Only on heap exhaustion | Only on heap exhaustion | — | — |
| **Underflow** | When `top == NULL` | When `front == NULL` | — | — |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Function Call Stack:** Compilers use a stack to store return addresses, local variables, and parameters during recursive calls.
- **Undo/Redo Mechanisms:** Text editors (VS Code, MS Word) maintain an **undo stack** and a **redo stack** to reverse/redo user actions.
- **CPU Scheduling:** The **Ready Queue** in operating systems uses a queue to manage processes awaiting CPU time (FCFS scheduling).
- **Print Spooling:** Print jobs are dispatched in FIFO order through a queue.
- **Browser Back Button:** A stack maintains the navigation history; each new page is pushed, and the Back button pops the stack.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Operation Cost Justification

For every operation below, we derive the asymptotic complexity from first principles by counting pointer manipulations.

**Definition of $T(n)$ — Cost Function:**

$$ T(n) \;=\; \text{Number of pointer assignments + conditional checks} $$

**Counting Rule:**

- One pointer assignment $= 1$ primitive operation.
- One `malloc` / `free` call $= 1$ primitive operation.
- A loop that runs $k$ times contributes $k$ to $T(n)$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of PUSH Complexity (Stack)

Let $T_{\text{push}}(n)$ denote the cost of pushing onto a stack of current size $n$.

**Step 1 — Node Allocation:**

$$ T_1 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{one \texttt{malloc} call}) $$

**Step 2 — Data Assignment:**

$$ T_2 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{one assignment to \texttt{newNode->data}}) $$

**Step 3 — Link the new node to the existing top:**

$$ T_3 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{newNode->next = top}}) $$

**Step 4 — Update the top pointer:**

$$ T_4 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{top = newNode}}) $$

**Step 5 — Update size counter:**

$$ T_5 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{size++}}) $$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{push}}(n) &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 \\
&= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Bound:**

$$ T_{\text{push}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O(1) $$

The push operation is **independent of $n$** — adding the 1000th element takes the same time as adding the 1st. This is the mathematical justification for why linked-list stacks are preferred for high-throughput scenarios.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of POP Complexity (Stack)

Let $T_{\text{pop}}(n)$ denote the cost of popping from a stack of size $n$.

**Step 1 — Underflow Check:**

$$ T_1 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{one comparison \texttt{top == NULL}}) $$

**Step 2 — Store temporary pointer:**

$$ T_2 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{temp = top}}) $$

**Step 3 — Read the data:**

$$ T_3 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{value = top->data}}) $$

**Step 4 — Advance top:**

$$ T_4 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{top = top->next}}) $$

**Step 5 — Deallocate:**

$$ T_5 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{one \texttt{free(temp)} call}) $$

**Step 6 — Decrement size:**

$$ T_6 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{size--}}) $$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{pop}}(n) &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 + T_6 \\
&= 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Bound:**

$$ T_{\text{pop}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O(1) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** The constant factors (5 vs. 6) are irrelevant in asymptotic analysis. Both are $O(1)$. KTU expects you to state **why** the operation is $O(1)$ — namely, that it requires a *fixed number* of pointer operations **independent of the current size $n$**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of ENQUEUE Complexity (Queue with Front + Rear)

The queue maintains two pointers: `front` and `rear`. Insertion happens at `rear`.

**Step 1 — Allocate new node:**

$$ T_1 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{newNode = malloc(...)}}) $$

**Step 2 — Assign data and terminate link:**

$$ T_2 \;=\; 2 \quad (\text{\texttt{newNode->data = value; newNode->next = NULL;}}) $$

**Step 3 — Empty queue check:**

$$ T_3 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{if \texttt{rear == NULL}}) $$

**Step 4 — Link previous rear to new node (if not empty):**

$$ T_4 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{rear->next = newNode}}) $$

**Step 5 — Update rear pointer:**

$$ T_5 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{rear = newNode}}) $$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{enqueue}}(n) &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 \\
&= 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Bound:**

$$ T_{\text{enqueue}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O(1) $$

Without the `rear` pointer, enqueue would require traversal — yielding $O(n)$. Hence the **two-pointer invariant** is the crux of an efficient queue.

---

### 3.5 Derivation of DEQUEUE Complexity (Queue)

**Step 1 — Underflow check:**

$$ T_1 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{if \texttt{front == NULL}}) $$

**Step 2 — Store temp and read value:**

$$ T_2 \;=\; 2 \quad (\text{\texttt{temp = front; value = front->data;}}) $$

**Step 3 — Advance front:**

$$ T_3 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{front = front->next}}) $$

**Step 4 — Set rear to NULL if queue becomes empty:**

$$ T_4 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{if \texttt{front == NULL}, then \texttt{rear = NULL}}) $$

**Step 5 — Deallocate:**

$$ T_5 \;=\; 1 \quad (\text{\texttt{free(temp)}}) $$

**Total Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{dequeue}}(n) &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5 \\
&= 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic Bound:**

$$ T_{\text{dequeue}}(n) \;=\; \Theta(1) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; O(1) $$

---

### 3.6 Worked Example — Pushing Sequence

**Problem:** Given an empty stack implemented as a linked list, push the values **10, 20, 30, 40** in that order. Show the state of the linked list and the `top` pointer after every push.

**Step 1 — Push 10:**

The list is empty. Allocate node, set `data=10`, `next=NULL`, and `top` points to it.

$$ \text{top} \;\to\; [10 \mid \text{NULL}] $$

**Step 2 — Push 20:**

Allocate new node, `data=20`, `next = top` (pointing to the node containing 10). Update `top` to the new node.

$$ \text{top} \;\to\; [20 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [10 \mid \text{NULL}] $$

**Step 3 — Push 30:**

$$ \text{top} \;\to\; [30 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [20 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [10 \mid \text{NULL}] $$

**Step 4 — Push 40:**

$$ \text{top} \;\to\; [40 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [30 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [20 \mid \cdot] \;\to\; [10 \mid \text{NULL}] $$

**Observation:** The element that was inserted *last* (40) is at the `top` — confirming LIFO. A subsequent pop will return **40** and the new top will be **30**.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stack Using Linked List — Node Linkage

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[TOP Pointer] --> B[Node: data=40, next]
    B --> C[Node: data=30, next]
    C --> D[Node: data=20, next]
    D --> E[Node: data=10, next]
    E --> F((NULL))
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The `TOP` pointer anchors the entire chain. Each `next` arrow points to the *previous* element pushed. The chain terminates at `NULL`, which is the *first* element ever inserted (the bottom of the stack).

### 4.2 Queue Using Linked List — Node Linkage

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[FRONT Pointer] --> B[Node: data=10, next]
    B --> C[Node: data=20, next]
    C --> D[Node: data=30, next]
    D --> E[Node: data=40, next]
    E --> F((NULL))
    G[REAR Pointer] --> E
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Two anchors exist: `FRONT` (for dequeue) and `REAR` (for enqueue). Insertions attach new nodes to the right of the chain (at `REAR`); deletions remove from the left (at `FRONT`). This guarantees FIFO order — 10 is dequeued first, 40 is dequeued last.

### 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow — PUSH Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start: PUSH value] --> P2{Is memory available?}
    P2 -- No --> P3[Print Overflow]
    P2 -- Yes --> P4[Allocate newNode in heap]
    P4 --> P5[Set newNode data to value]
    P5 --> P6[Set newNode next to TOP]
    P6 --> P7[Update TOP to newNode]
    P7 --> P8[Increment size]
    P8 --> P9[End: Push successful]
    P3 --> P10[End: Failure]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — DEQUEUE Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start: DEQUEUE] --> Q2{Is FRONT equal to NULL?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Print Underflow]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4[Store temp pointer to FRONT node]
    Q4 --> Q5[Read value from FRONT data]
    Q5 --> Q6[Advance FRONT to FRONT next]
    Q6 --> Q7{Is new FRONT equal to NULL?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Set REAR to NULL]
    Q7 -- No --> Q9[Skip REAR update]
    Q8 --> Q10[Free temp node]
    Q9 --> Q10
    Q10 --> Q11[Return value]
    Q3 --> Q12[End: Failure]
    Q11 --> Q13[End: Dequeue successful]
```

### 4.5 Memory Layout — Snapshot of a 3-Node Stack

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HeapMemory["Heap Memory Layout"]
        H1[Address 1000: data=10, next=NULL]
        H2[Address 2048: data=20, next=1000]
        H3[Address 3072: data=30, next=2048]
    end
    subgraph StackSegment["Stack Segment"]
        S1[TOP pointer = 3072]
    end
    S1 --> H3
    H3 --> H2
    H2 --> H1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Visualization Insight:** The *addresses* in the heap are arbitrary (allocated by `malloc`). The logical order is established by the `next` pointers, not by physical memory contiguity. This is the **defining characteristic** that distinguishes a linked list from an array.
