| Worst-case comparisons | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Same as best case |
| Average-case comparisons | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Identical due to lack of adaptivity |
| Total swaps (worst case) | $n - 1$ | $O(n)$ | When input is reverse sorted, every pass needs a swap |
| Total swaps (best case) | $0$ | $O(1)$ | When input is already sorted (`min_idx == i` always) |
| Time Complexity | $T(n) = c_1 n^2 + c_2 n + c_3$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Both upper and lower bound are $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Space Complexity | $S(n) = O(1)$ | In-place | Only constant extra variables (`i`, `j`, `min_idx`, `temp`) |
| Stability | Boolean | **No** (unstable) | Equal-key elements can be reordered by the swap |
| Adaptivity | Boolean | **No** | Pre-sorted input does not improve runtime |

> **Note on Absolute-Value Notation in Tables:** Where absolute value is required, we use $\vert x \vert$ rendered via LaTeX `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Selection Sort is used in industry:**
> - **Embedded systems and firmware** with very small memory budgets, where the $O(1)$ auxiliary space is critical.
> - **Write-heavy or flash-memory devices** where minimizing the number of writes (swaps) extends hardware lifetime — Selection Sort performs at most $n-1$ writes, beating even Quick Sort in swap count.
> - **Educational settings** (KTU labs, viva-voce) as a baseline for introducing asymptotic analysis and algorithm design paradigms.
> - **Small arrays** ($n \leq 20$ approximately) where the simplicity of implementation outweighs the asymptotic cost.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Total Comparisons

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Prove that the total number of key comparisons performed by Selection Sort is exactly $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$, regardless of input distribution.

**Derivation:**

During pass $i$ (where $i$ ranges from $0$ to $n-2$), the inner loop compares $A[j]$ with $A[\text{min\_idx}]$ for $j = i+1, i+2, \ldots, n-1$.

The number of comparisons made in pass $i$ is therefore:

$$
C(i) = (n - 1) - (i + 1) + 1 = n - i - 1
$$

Summing $C(i)$ over all passes from $i = 0$ to $i = n-2$:

$$
T_{\text{comp}}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} C(i) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - i - 1)
$$

Rewriting the summation index by letting $k = n - i - 1$, observe that as $i$ goes from $0$ to $n-2$, $k$ goes from $n-1$ down to $1$. Hence:

$$
T_{\text{comp}}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} k
$$

Applying the well-known arithmetic series identity $\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{m} k = \dfrac{m(m+1)}{2}$ with $m = n-1$:

$$
T_{\text{comp}}(n) = \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}
$$

Therefore, the asymptotic comparison count is:

$$
T_{\text{comp}}(n) \in \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Conversion Logic Summary:**
- Inner loop count per pass → $n - i - 1$.
- Outer summation over $n-1$ passes → arithmetic progression.
- Closed form via $\sum k$ identity → $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$.
- Asymptotic class → $\Theta(n^2)$.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Swap Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the maximum and minimum number of swaps performed by Selection Sort.

**Minimum Swaps (Best Case — Already Sorted Input):**

When the input is already sorted, in every pass $i$, the element $A[i]$ is already the minimum of the unsorted region $A[i \ldots n-1]$. Hence `min_idx = i` for all $i$, and the condition `if (min_idx != i)` is **always false**. No swap ever occurs:

$$
S_{\min}(n) = 0
$$

**Maximum Swaps (Worst Case — Reverse Sorted Input):**

When the input is sorted in strictly descending order, in every pass $i$, the minimum of the unsorted region is the rightmost element $A[n-1]$. Hence `min_idx = n-1 \neq i` for all $i$, and exactly one swap occurs per pass:

$$
S_{\max}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} 1 = n - 1
$$

**Average Case:**

Since the location of the minimum is uniformly distributed over the $n-i$ unsorted positions, the probability that `min_idx = i` is $\dfrac{1}{n-i}$, and the probability of a swap is $1 - \dfrac{1}{n-i}$. Therefore:

$$
S_{\text{avg}}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n-i}\right) = (n-1) - \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} \frac{1}{n-i}
$$

Evaluating the harmonic sum:

$$
S_{\text{avg}}(n) = (n-1) - H_{n-1} \in \Theta(n)
$$

where $H_{n-1}$ is the $(n-1)$-th harmonic number. This confirms that **swaps are always $O(n)$** for Selection Sort — a uniquely efficient property.

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example (Array Trace)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Trace for KTU Board Exam:** Sort the array $A = [64, 25, 12, 22, 11]$ in ascending order using Selection Sort. Show every pass with array state, `min_idx` evolution, and swap details.

**Pass $i = 0$** (sorted region empty, unsorted: full array)

Initial: $[64,\ 25,\ 12,\ 22,\ 11]$, `min_idx = 0`

$$
\begin{aligned}
j = 1:&\ 25 < 64 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 1 \\
j = 2:&\ 12 < 25 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2 \\
j = 3:&\ 22 \not< 12 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2 \\
j = 4:&\ 11 < 12 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[4]$:

$$
[11,\ 25,\ 12,\ 22,\ 64]
$$

**Pass $i = 1$** (sorted: $A[0]$, unsorted: $A[1 \ldots 4]$)

Initial: $[11,\ 25,\ 12,\ 22,\ 64]$, `min_idx = 1`

$$
\begin{aligned}
j = 2:&\ 12 < 25 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2 \\
j = 3:&\ 22 \not< 12 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2 \\
j = 4:&\ 64 \not< 12 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[2]$:

$$
[11,\ 12,\ 25,\ 22,\ 64]
$$

**Pass $i = 2$** (sorted: $A[0 \ldots 1]$, unsorted: $A[2 \ldots 4]$)

Initial: $[11,\ 12,\ 25,\ 22,\ 64]$, `min_idx = 2`

$$
\begin{aligned}
j = 3:&\ 22 < 25 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 3 \\
j = 4:&\ 64 \not< 22 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Swap $A[2] \leftrightarrow A[3]$:

$$
[11,\ 12,\ 22,\ 25,\ 64]
$$

**Pass $i = 3$** (sorted: $A[0 \ldots 2]$, unsorted: $A[3 \ldots 4]$)

Initial: $[11,\ 12,\ 22,\ 25,\ 64]$, `min_idx = 3`

$$
j = 4:\ 64 \not< 25 \Rightarrow \text{min\_idx} = 3
$$

No swap needed (`min_idx == i`):

$$
[11,\ 12,\ 22,\ 25,\ 64]
$$

**Final Sorted Array:**

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [11,\ 12,\ 22,\ 25,\ 64]
$$

**Verification of Counts:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparisons:}&\ \frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10 \\
\text{Swaps:}&\ 3 \ (\text{passes 0, 1, 2}) \\
\text{Passes:}&\ 4 \ (\text{equals } n - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

All counts align with the derived formulas in §3.1 and §3.2.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Choose Selection Sort

| Criterion | Selection Sort Suitable? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Array size $n \leq 20$ | **Yes** | $O(n^2)$ overhead is negligible |
| Minimizing swaps is critical | **Yes** | At most $n-1$ swaps |
| Memory is severely constrained | **Yes** | $O(1)$ auxiliary space |
| Stability is required | **No** | Selection Sort is unstable |
| Input is nearly sorted | **No** | Not adaptive; pays $\Theta(n^2)$ anyway |
| Large datasets ($n \geq 10^5$) | **No** | Use Merge Sort ($O(n \log n)$) or Quick Sort |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithmic Control Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block represents the **control-flow architecture** of Selection Sort. Node IDs are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid's reserved-keyword restrictions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START] --> A1[Read array A of size n]
    A1 --> A2[Initialize i = 0]
    A2 --> A3{Condition: i less than n minus 1}
    A3 -- NO --> A4[Return sorted array A]
    A4 --> A5[END]
    A3 -- YES --> B1[Set min_idx = i]
    B1 --> B2[Set j = i + 1]
    B2 --> B3{Condition: j less than n}
    B3 -- NO --> C1{Condition: min_idx not equal to i}
    B3 -- YES --> B4{Condition: A of j less than A of min_idx}
    B4 -- YES --> B5[Set min_idx = j]
    B4 -- NO --> B6[Increment j by 1]
    B5 --> B6
    B6 --> B3
    C1 -- YES --> C2[Swap A of i with A of min_idx]
    C1 -- NO --> C3[Skip swap]
    C2 --> C4[Increment i by 1]
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> A3
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Pass-by-Pass Memory Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PassI0["PASS i = 0"]
        P0A["Sorted Region: empty"]:::sorted
        P0B["Unsorted Region: A0 to A4"]:::unsorted
    end
    subgraph PassI1["PASS i = 1"]
        P1A["Sorted Region: A0"]:::sorted
        P1B["Unsorted Region: A1 to A4"]:::unsorted
    end
    subgraph PassI2["PASS i = 2"]
        P2A["Sorted Region: A0 to A1"]:::sorted
        P2B["Unsorted Region: A2 to A4"]:::unsorted
    end
    subgraph PassI3["PASS i = 3"]
        P3A["Sorted Region: A0 to A2"]:::sorted
        P3B["Unsorted Region: A3 to A4"]:::unsorted
    end
    subgraph PassI4["PASS i = 4 TERMINAL"]
        P4A["Sorted Region: A0 to A3"]:::sorted
        P4B["Final element A4 fixed in place"]:::final
    end
    PassI0 --> PassI1 --> PassI2 --> PassI3 --> PassI4
    classDef sorted fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    classDef unsorted fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Comparison & Swap Counters State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialiseCounters
    InitialiseCounters --> CompareLoop: i = 0, countC = 0, countS = 0
    CompareLoop --> CompareLoop: A of j less than A of min_idx, countC increment, j increment
    CompareLoop --> SwapDecision: j reaches n
    SwapDecision --> SwapExecute: min_idx not equal to i, countS increment
    SwapDecision --> NextPass: min_idx equals i, countS unchanged
    SwapExecute --> NextPass
    NextPass --> CompareLoop: i increment, i less than n minus 1
    NextPass --> Terminate: i reaches n minus 1
    Terminate --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Schematic §4.3:** The two counters `countC` (comparisons) and `countS` (swaps) accumulate monotonically. After termination, `countC = n(n-1)/2` and `countS \leq n-1`, matching the derivations of §3.1 and §3.2.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Part A in KTU ESE tests **direct concept recall** and is typically the gateway to Part B marks. Answer each in **3–4 lines maximum**.

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**What is Selection Sort? Mention its time and space complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

> Selection Sort is a comparison-based, in-place internal sorting algorithm that, in each pass, **selects the minimum element from the unsorted sub-array** and swaps it with the element at the leftmost unsorted position. Its time complexity is $\Theta(n^2)$ in the best, average, and worst cases, and its auxiliary space complexity is $O(1)$.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark (definition) + 1 mark (time complexity) + 1 mark (space complexity) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Is Selection Sort stable? Justify your answer with a counter-example.**

**Model Answer:**

> No, Selection Sort is **not stable** in its standard form. A sorting algorithm is stable if equal-key elements retain their original relative order. In Selection Sort, the swap operation can move an equal-key element past another equal-key element. For example, consider the input $[5_a, 5_b, 3]$. In pass 0, the algorithm finds `min = 3` at index 2 and swaps it with $5_a$ at index 0, producing $[3, 5_b, 5_a]$. The original relative order of $5_a$ before $5_b$ has been violated.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark (Yes/No with definition of stability) + 2 marks (counter-example) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. You must attempt **either OR**, not both. Below, both options are provided for parallel practice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Selection Sort algorithm with a suitable C-style pseudocode. **State and prove** its time complexity.

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm (C-style Pseudocode):**

```c
void selectionSort(int A[], int n) {
    int i, j, min_idx, temp;
    for (i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
        min_idx = i;
        for (j = i + 1; j < n; j++) {
            if (A[j] < A[min_idx]) {
                min_idx = j;
            }
        }
        if (min_idx != i) {
            temp = A[i];
            A[i] = A[min_idx];
            A[min_idx] = temp;
        }
    }
}
```

**Time Complexity Statement:**

> The time complexity of Selection Sort is $T(n) = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} \in \Theta(n^2)$ for all input distributions.

**Proof (from §3.1):**

The number of comparisons in pass $i$ is $n - i - 1$. Summing over all $n-1$ passes:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n-2} (n - i - 1) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{n^2 - n}{2}
$$

The dominant term is $\frac{n^2}{2}$, hence $T(n) \in \Theta(n^2)$ for the best, average, and worst cases.

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks (pseudocode correctness) + 2 marks (statement of time complexity) + 3 marks (algebraic derivation) = **7 marks**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Sort the array $A = [29, 10, 14, 37, 13]$ in ascending order using **Selection Sort**. Show every pass and compute the total number of comparisons and swaps.

**Model Solution:**

**Pass $i = 0$:** Initial: $[29, 10, 14, 37, 13]$, `min_idx = 0`
- $j=1$: $10 < 29$ → `min_idx = 1`
- $j=2$: $14 \not< 10$ → `min_idx = 1`
- $j=3$: $37 \not< 10$ → `min_idx = 1`
- $j=4$: $13 \not< 10$ → `min_idx = 1`

Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[1]$ → Array: $[10, 29, 14, 37, 13]$

**Pass $i = 1$:** Initial: $[10, 29, 14, 37, 13]$, `min_idx = 1`
- $j=2$: $14 < 29$ → `min_idx = 2`
- $j=3$: $37 \not< 14$ → `min_idx = 2`
- $j=4$: $13 < 14$ → `min_idx = 4`

Swap $A[1] \leftrightarrow A[4]$ → Array: $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$

**Pass $i = 2$:** Initial: $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$, `min_idx = 2`
- $j=3$: $37 \not< 14$ → `min_idx = 2`
- $j=4$: $29 \not< 14$ → `min_idx = 2`

No swap → Array: $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$

**Pass $i = 3$:** Initial: $[10, 13, 14, 37, 29]$, `min_idx = 3`
- $j=4$: $29 < 37$ → `min_idx = 4`

Swap $A[3] \leftrightarrow A[4]$ → Array: $[10, 13, 14, 29, 37]$

**Final Sorted Array:**

$$
A_{\text{sorted}} = [10, 13, 14, 29, 37]
$$

**Counters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Comparisons} &= 4 + 3 + 2 + 1 = 10 = \frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} \\
\text{Swaps} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Marks Distribution:** 4 marks (pass-by-pass trace) + 2 marks (counter computation) + 1 mark (final array) = **7 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Selection Sort** and **Bubble Sort** in terms of: (i) number of comparisons, (ii) number of swaps, (iii) stability, and (iv) adaptivity. Justify with formulas.

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Selection Sort | Bubble Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Comparisons** | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ always | Best: $n-1$; Worst/Average: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ |
| **(ii) Swaps** | At most $n-1$ | Worst: $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$; Best: $0$ |
| **(iii) Stability** | Unstable | **Stable** |
| **(iv) Adaptivity** | Not adaptive | **Adaptive** (early-exit optimization reduces work on nearly-sorted input) |

**Justification:**

- **Comparisons:** Both algorithms scan the unsorted region in every pass, yielding the same comparison count in the worst case. However, optimized Bubble Sort with a swapped-flag short-circuits when no swaps occur in a pass, reducing best-case comparisons to $n-1$. Selection Sort, lacking any such early-exit mechanism, always performs $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons.

- **Swaps:** Selection Sort performs at most one swap per pass (at most $n-1$ total), since it directly places the minimum in its final position. Bubble Sort, in contrast, performs up to $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ swaps in the worst case because adjacent inversions are resolved one at a time.

- **Stability:** Bubble Sort preserves the relative order of equal elements because it only swaps on strict inequality $A[j-1] > A[j]$. Selection Sort uses a positional swap that can leapfrog equal elements, violating their original order.

- **Adaptivity:** Bubble Sort with the swapped-flag optimization can detect pre-sorted input in $O(n)$ time. Selection Sort has no equivalent short-circuit, making it non-adaptive.

**Marks Distribution:** 1 mark per criterion × 4 criteria = 4 marks + 3 marks for the analytical justification = **7 marks**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A list of **$n = 100$** integers is to be sorted using Selection Sort. Compute: (i) the exact number of comparisons, (ii) the maximum number of swaps, and (iii) the minimum number of swaps. State the asymptotic class.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 100$.

**(i) Exact number of comparisons:**

$$
C = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \frac{100 \cdot 99}{2} = 4950
$$

**(ii) Maximum number of swaps:** Occurs when the input is reverse-sorted, requiring one swap per pass:

$$
S_{\max} = n - 1 = 99
$$

**(iii) Minimum number of swaps:** Occurs when the input is already sorted (no `min_idx != i` condition triggered):

$$
S_{\min} = 0
$$

**Asymptotic class:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^2), \quad S(n) = O(n)
$$

**Marks Distribution:** 2 marks (i) + 2 marks (ii) + 2 marks (iii) + 1 mark (asymptotic class) = **7 marks**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the swap guard `if (min_idx != i)`** — without it, the algorithm performs an unnecessary self-swap, which still counts as a swap operation in board valuations. Always guard the swap.
> 2. **Claiming Selection Sort is stable** — it is **not stable** in the standard one-pass min-finding variant. If a stable variant is required, it is *not* a true Selection Sort.
> 3. **Confusing comparisons with swaps** — these are distinct metrics. Selection Sort has $\Theta(n^2)$ comparisons but only $O(n)$ swaps. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
> 4. **Wrong inner-loop bound** — the inner loop must run from $i+1$ to $n-1$ (inclusive), giving exactly $n-i-1$ comparisons per pass. A common error is starting from $i$ or ending at $n$, which corrupts the derivation.
> 5. **Omitting the "in-place" justification** — the examiner expects a one-line note that no auxiliary array is used; only a constant number of integer variables (`i`, `j`, `min_idx`, `temp`).
> 6. **Writing the array trace without explicitly updating `min_idx`** — the trace must show, at every $j$, the *current* value of `min_idx`, not just the comparison outcome. The board examiner deducts 1–2 marks for incomplete trace tables.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Selection Sort**

- **Algorithmic Family:** Comparison-based, in-place, internal, brute-force, deterministic.
- **Core Strategy:** Greedy positional sorting — at each pass, find the minimum in the unsorted suffix and place it at the boundary of the sorted prefix.
- **Number of Passes:** Always $n - 1$.
- **Comparison Count:** Exactly $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons, **independent of input order** (this is the hallmark of non-adaptive behaviour).
- **Swap Count:** Minimum $0$ (already sorted), maximum $n-1$ (reverse sorted), average $\Theta(n)$.
- **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(n^2)$ in **all** cases (best, average, worst).
- **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ auxiliary — true in-place sort.
- **Stability:** **Unstable** by default (the swap can reorder equal-key elements).
- **Adaptivity:** **Not adaptive** (cannot exploit pre-sortedness).
- **Final Sorted Region Growth:** From the **left** of the array.
- **Minimum Element Placed at:** Index $i$ after pass $i$ (leftmost unsorted slot).
- **Inner Loop Bound:** $j$ ranges from $i+1$ to $n-1$, giving $n - i - 1$ comparisons per pass.
- **Key Practical Advantage:** **Minimum swap count** among comparison-based sorts — preferred in environments with expensive writes (e.g., flash memory, EEPROM).
- **KTU Board Favourite Question Types:** (1) Pass-by-pass trace with counter computation, (2) Comparison with Bubble Sort, (3) Time-complexity derivation from the comparison sum, (4) Stability analysis with counter-example.
- **Penalty to Avoid:** Never claim Selection Sort is stable or adaptive; both are false in the standard formulation.
- **Formula Mnemonic:** "**C**omparisons grow **Q**uadratically, **S**waps grow **L**inearly" — $\text{C} = O(n^2)$, $\text{S} = O(n)$.
- **One-Line Definition for KTU:** *"Selection Sort repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted region and swaps it into its final position at the boundary of the sorted region, achieving $\Theta(n^2)$ time and $O(1)$ auxiliary space."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Insertion Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Insertion Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Insertion Sort** is a simple, comparison-based, **in-place**, and **stable** sorting algorithm that builds the final sorted array one element at a time. It iterates through an input list, removing one element from the unsorted portion (the input data) and inserting it into its correct position within the sorted portion of the list. The algorithm maintains a logical division: the left sub-array is always sorted, while the right sub-array remains unsorted.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Insertion Sort is classified as a **brute-force, incremental algorithm** belonging to the family of **Quadratic Time Sorting Algorithms** (alongside Bubble Sort and Selection Sort). It is particularly highlighted in the syllabus for its **adaptive nature** and its efficient behaviour on **nearly sorted data**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Insertion Sort is studied as a foundational comparison-based sort. Students must be able to write the algorithm in C, perform dry runs, and formally derive its **Best, Worst, and Average case time complexities** as $\Omega(n)$, $O(n^2)$, and $\Theta(n^2)$ respectively.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Sorting Playing Cards in Your Hand"

Imagine you are dealt a hand of playing cards **one at a time**, face-up. Your goal is to keep the cards in your hand always sorted from lowest to highest.

Here is exactly what you do:
1. You pick up the first card — it is trivially "sorted" by itself.
2. You pick up the second card — compare it with the first; if smaller, slide it before, else leave it.
3. You pick up the third card — compare it from right-to-left with the cards already in your sorted hand, shifting larger cards one position to the right until you find the correct slot. Insert it there.
4. Repeat until all cards are dealt and inserted.

This is precisely how **Insertion Sort** operates on an array. The "hand" is the sorted sub-array, and each new card is shifted rightward to make room for itself in the correct position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties (Must Memorize for KTU):**
> - **In-Place:** Uses only $O(1)$ auxiliary space.
> - **Stable:** Equal elements retain their original relative order.
> - **Adaptive:** Performs exceptionally well ($O(n)$) on nearly sorted input.
> - **Online:** Can sort data as it arrives (streaming input).
> - **Comparison-Based:** Uses only $<, >$ operators; no arithmetic on keys.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

Insertion Sort divides the array into two logical regions:

$$\text{Array} = \underbrace{[ \text{Sorted Sub-array} ]}_{\text{indices } 0 \text{ to } i-1} \quad \big| \quad \underbrace{[ \text{Unsorted Sub-array} ]}_{\text{indices } i \text{ to } n-1}$$

The algorithm proceeds in **$n-1$ passes** (iterations of the outer loop):

- **Step 1 (Outer Loop):** Iterate $i$ from $1$ to $n-1$. At the start of iteration $i$, the sub-array $A[0 \ldots i-1]$ is assumed sorted.
- **Step 2 (Pick Key):** Store $A[i]$ into a temporary variable called `key`. This creates a "hole" at position $i$.
- **Step 3 (Inner Loop — Shifting Phase):** Initialize $j = i - 1$. While $j \geq 0$ AND $A[j] > \text{key}$, shift $A[j]$ to $A[j+1]$ and decrement $j$.
- **Step 4 (Insertion Phase):** Place `key` at position $A[j+1]$.
- **Step 5 (Repeat):** Move to the next $i$.

The **inner loop** is the heart of the algorithm — it shifts elements greater than `key` one position to the right, creating space for the insertion.

> [!TIP]
> **Why shift instead of swap?** A common variant swaps adjacent elements. The "shift" variant is preferred in KTU exams because it performs **half the assignments** of the swap version on average, making it more efficient in practice. Always mention the shift-based version in viva.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The table below consolidates every formula, boundary condition, and complexity metric you will need to answer any KTU question on Insertion Sort.

| **Property** | **Value** | **Notation** | **Explanation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Best Case Time | Linear | $\Omega(n)$ | Array already sorted; inner loop never executes the shift. |
| Average Case Time | Quadratic | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Elements are in random order; half need shifting on average. |
| Worst Case Time | Quadratic | $O(n^2)$ | Reverse sorted array; maximum shifts at every step. |
| Space Complexity | Constant | $O(1)$ | Only one extra variable `key` and index `j` are used. |
| Number of Swaps (Best) | 0 | — | No shifting required. |
| Number of Swaps (Worst) | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | — | Equivalent to $\binom{n}{2}$ inversions cleared. |
| Stability | Stable | — | Only shifts elements strictly greater than `key`. |
| In-Place | Yes | — | No auxiliary array required. |
| Recursive? | No (Iterative) | — | Naturally expressed as a `for` loop. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard C-Style Pseudocode (Use this in your KTU answer sheet):**
>
> ```c
> void insertionSort(int A[], int n) {
>     int i, j, key;
>     for (i = 1; i < n; i++) {
>         key = A[i];
>         j = i - 1;
>         while (j >= 0 && A[j] > key) {
>             A[j + 1] = A[j];
>             j = j - 1;
>         }
>         A[j + 1] = key;
>     }
> }
> ```

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although Insertion Sort is asymptotically slower than $O(n \log n)$ algorithms (Merge Sort, Quick Sort), it has significant practical value:

- **Hybrid Sorts:** It forms the base case of sophisticated hybrid algorithms like **Timsort** (used in Python's `sorted()` and Java's `Arrays.sort()`) and **Introsort**, where small sub-arrays (typically $n \le 16$) are sorted using Insertion Sort because it has very low overhead.
- **Nearly Sorted Data:** Database indexing, log files, and incremental data streams often contain nearly sorted data. Insertion Sort runs in near-linear time on such inputs.
- **Embedded Systems:** With only a few lines of code and $O(1)$ extra memory, it is ideal for memory-constrained microcontrollers and firmware.
- **Online Algorithms:** Used when data arrives continuously (e.g., real-time sensor readings) and must be inserted into an already-sorted live structure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Dry Run — Worked Example

Let us trace Insertion Sort on the input array:

$$A = [5, \ 2, \ 4, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$$

Here, $n = 6$. The algorithm will run for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$.

### Pass 1: $i = 1$, $key = A[1] = 2$
- Sorted region: $[5]$. Compare $5 > 2$ → true.
- Shift $A[0]=5$ to $A[1]$. Array becomes $[5, \ 5, \ 4, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$. $j = -1$.
- Insert `key` at $A[0]$. Array becomes $[2, \ 5, \ 4, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$.

### Pass 2: $i = 2$, $key = A[2] = 4$
- Sorted region: $[2, 5]$. Compare $5 > 4$ → true. Shift $5$ right.
- Array: $[2, \ 5, \ 5, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$. $j = 0$.
- Compare $2 > 4$ → false. Stop.
- Insert `key` at $A[1]$. Array becomes $[2, \ 4, \ 5, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$.

### Pass 3: $i = 3$, $key = A[3] = 6$
- Sorted region: $[2, 4, 5]$. Compare $5 > 6$ → false. No shift.
- Insert `key` at $A[3]$ (its original position). Array: $[2, \ 4, \ 5, \ 6, \ 1, \ 3]$.

### Pass 4: $i = 4$, $key = A[4] = 1$
- Sorted region: $[2, 4, 5, 6]$. Shift $6, 5, 4, 2$ all right.
- Array trace: $[2, 4, 5, 6, 6, 3] \to [2, 4, 5, 5, 6, 3] \to [2, 4, 4, 5, 6, 3] \to [2, 2, 4, 5, 6, 3]$. $j = -1$.
- Insert `key` at $A[0]$. Array: $[1, \ 2, \ 4, \ 5, \ 6, \ 3]$.

### Pass 5: $i = 5$, $key = A[5] = 3$
- Sorted region: $[1, 2, 4, 5, 6]$. Shift $6, 5, 4$ all right.
- Array trace: $[1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 6] \to [1, 2, 4, 5, 5, 6] \to [1, 2, 4, 4, 5, 6]$. $j = 1$.
- Compare $2 > 3$ → false. Stop.
- Insert `key` at $A[2]$. Array: $[1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 4, \ 5, \ 6]$ ✅ **SORTED**

### Final State
$$A_{\text{final}} = [1, \ 2, \ 3, \ 4, \ 5, \ 6]$$

## 3.2 Mathematical Derivation of Time Complexity

Let $T(n)$ denote the total number of comparisons performed by Insertion Sort. The total work is the sum of work done in each outer-loop iteration:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} C_i$$

where $C_i$ is the number of comparisons made during the $i$-th pass.

### Derivation of Best Case — $T_{\text{best}}(n) = \Omega(n)$

The best case occurs when the input array is **already sorted** in ascending order. In this scenario:
- The inner `while` loop condition $A[j] > \text{key}$ is **immediately false** for the first comparison.
- Hence, exactly **one comparison** is made per pass: $C_i = 1$ for all $i$.

Substituting into the summation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{best}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 \\
&= (n-1) \cdot 1 \\
&= n - 1 \\
&= \Omega(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **linear best-case behaviour** — the algorithm is *adaptive*.

### Derivation of Worst Case — $T_{\text{worst}}(n) = O(n^2)$

The worst case occurs when the input is in **strictly descending order**. In every pass $i$, the new `key` is smaller than all $i$ elements in the sorted region, so all of them must be shifted. The number of comparisons in pass $i$ is $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{worst}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i \\
&= 1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + (n-1) \\
&= \frac{(n-1) \cdot n}{2} \\
&= \frac{n^2 - n}{2} \\
&= O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the well-known **sum of the first $n-1$ natural numbers**, derived from the arithmetic progression formula $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$.

### Derivation of Average Case — $T_{\text{avg}}(n) = \Theta(n^2)$

In the average case, we assume that the new element `key` is equally likely to be inserted at any of the $i+1$ possible positions within the sorted region (before the first element, between any two, or after the last). The expected number of comparisons in pass $i$ is:

$$E[C_i] = \frac{1 + 2 + 3 + \cdots + i + (i+1)}{2} \cdot \text{(proportional factor)}$$

More precisely, the average number of comparisons in pass $i$ is $\frac{i}{2} + 1$. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \left(\frac{i}{2} + 1\right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(n-1)n}{2} + (n-1) \\
&= \frac{n^2 - n}{4} + (n-1) \\
&= \frac{n^2 - n + 4n - 4}{4} \\
&= \frac{n^2 + 3n - 4}{4} \\
&= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** When asked to justify $\Theta(n^2)$ for the average case, you do not need to derive the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor in detail. It is sufficient to state: "On average, half the elements in the sorted region must be shifted, giving a factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ multiplied by the worst-case sum, which still simplifies to $\Theta(n^2)$."

## 3.3 Comparison Count Summary Table

| **Case** | **Input Pattern** | **Comparisons $C_i$ in pass $i$** | **Total Comparisons $T(n)$** | **Big-O** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Best | Already sorted | $1$ | $n - 1$ | $\Omega(n)$ |
| Average | Random order | $\frac{i}{2} + 1$ | $\frac{n^2 + 3n - 4}{4}$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| Worst | Reverse sorted | $i$ | $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $O(n^2)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Control Flow of Insertion Sort

The following flowchart captures the complete control flow of the standard Insertion Sort algorithm. It explicitly shows the **outer loop**, the **key extraction**, the **inner shifting loop**, and the **final insertion**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START]) --> B["Initialize: i = 1"]
    B --> C{"i less than n?"}
    C -- No --> Z([END: Array is Sorted])
    C -- Yes --> D["key = A of i"]
    D --> E["j = i - 1"]
    E --> F{"j greater than or equal to 0 AND A of j greater than key?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["A of j+1 = A of j"]
    G --> H["j = j - 1"]
    H --> F
    F -- No --> I["A of j+1 = key"]
    I --> J["i = i + 1"]
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Array State Transitions

The diagram below visualizes how the array's logical partition (sorted vs. unsorted) evolves across all six passes of the dry run performed in Section 3.1. The `|` symbol represents the boundary between the sorted and unsorted regions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0["Initial: 5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3"] --> P1["Pass 1: 2, 5 | 4, 6, 1, 3"]
    P1 --> P2["Pass 2: 2, 4, 5 | 6, 1, 3"]
    P2 --> P3["Pass 3: 2, 4, 5, 6 | 1, 3"]
    P3 --> P4["Pass 4: 1, 2, 4, 5, 6 | 3"]
    P4 --> P5["Pass 5: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 | (done)"]
    P5 --> PFinal["Final Sorted: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram illustrates how Insertion Sort integrates as a processing block within a larger data-pipeline system — a common KTU viva question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        I1[Raw Data Array] --> I2[Index Pointer i]
    end
    subgraph PROC["Insertion Sort Engine"]
        P1[Key Extractor] --> P2[Comparator: A of j greater than key]
        P2 -- True --> P3[Shifter: A of j+1 = A of j]
        P3 --> P2
        P2 -- False --> P4[Inserter: A of j+1 = key]
        P4 --> P1
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        O1[Sorted Sub-array 0 to i] --> O2[Unsorted Sub-array i+1 to n-1]
    end
    I2 --> PROC
    PROC --> OUTPUT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Define Insertion Sort. Mention any two of its important properties.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Insertion Sort is a comparison-based, in-place sorting algorithm that builds the sorted array one element at a time by repeatedly picking the next element from the unsorted portion and inserting it into its correct position within the sorted portion.

**Properties (2 Marks — 1 each):**
1. **Stable:** Equal elements retain their original relative order, because the algorithm only shifts elements strictly greater than the key.
2. **Adaptive:** Its best-case time complexity is $\Omega(n)$ when the input is already sorted, making it highly efficient for nearly sorted data.

*(Other valid properties accepted: In-Place, Online, Iterative, Low overhead.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> What is the best-case and worst-case time complexity of Insertion Sort? When does the best case occur?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Best Case:** $\Omega(n)$ — occurs when the input array is **already sorted in ascending order**. In this case, the inner `while` loop terminates after just one comparison per pass. (1.5 Marks)
- **Worst Case:** $O(n^2)$ — occurs when the input array is sorted in **descending (reverse) order**. The inner loop must perform the maximum number of shifts at every pass, summing to $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ comparisons. (1.5 Marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 4, Choice A]**

### (a) [7 Marks] Sort the array $A = [12, 11, 13, 5, 6]$ using Insertion Sort. Show all intermediate passes and the state of the array after each pass.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

We trace each of the $n - 1 = 4$ passes, showing the `key`, the comparison/shift operations, and the resulting array.

**Initial Array:** $[12, 11, 13, 5, 6]$

**Pass 1:** $i = 1$, $\text{key} = A[1] = 11$
- Compare $A[0] = 12 > 11$ → True. Shift $12$ to $A[1]$.
- $j = -1$. Stop.
- Insert `key` at $A[0]$.
- **Result:** $[11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$ (1 Mark)

**Pass 2:** $i = 2$, $\text{key} = A[2] = 13$
- Compare $A[1] = 12 > 13$ → False. No shift.
- Insert `key` at $A[2]$ (no change).
- **Result:** $[11, 12, 13, 5, 6]$ (1 Mark)

**Pass 3:** $i = 3$, $\text{key} = A[3] = 5$
- Compare $A[2] = 13 > 5$ → True. Shift $13$ right.
- Compare $A[1] = 12 > 5$ → True. Shift $12$ right.
- Compare $A[0] = 11 > 5$ → True. Shift $11$ right.
- $j = -1$. Stop.
- Insert `key` at $A[0]$.
- **Result:** $[5, 11, 12, 13, 6]$ (2 Marks)

**Pass 4:** $i = 4$, $\text{key} = A[4] = 6$
- Compare $A[3] = 13 > 6$ → True. Shift $13$ right.
- Compare $A[2] = 12 > 6$ → True. Shift $12$ right.
- Compare $A[1] = 11 > 6$ → True. Shift $11$ right.
- Compare $A[0] = 5 > 6$ → False. Stop.
- Insert `key` at $A[1]$.
- **Final Result:** $[5, 6, 11, 12, 13]$ (2 Marks)

**Array sorted successfully. ✅**

---

### (b) [7 Marks] Derive the worst-case and best-case time complexity of Insertion Sort. Mention the input conditions under which each case occurs.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

Let $n$ be the number of elements. The algorithm performs $n-1$ passes. Let $C_i$ be the number of comparisons in pass $i$.

**Worst Case — $O(n^2)$ (4 Marks):**
- **Condition:** Input array is in **strictly descending order** (e.g., $[9, 7, 5, 3, 1]$).
- **Reasoning:** In pass $i$, the new `key` is smaller than all $i$ elements in the sorted region, so every element must be compared and shifted.
- **Comparisons per pass:** $C_i = i$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{worst}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} i = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best Case — $\Omega(n)$ (3 Marks):**
- **Condition:** Input array is **already sorted in ascending order**.
- **Reasoning:** In pass $i$, the inner `while` loop condition $A[j] > \text{key}$ is **false on the very first comparison**, so no shifting occurs.
- **Comparisons per pass:** $C_i = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{best}}(n) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} 1 = n - 1 = \Omega(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing swaps with shifts:** If you write a swap-based version, you must count both comparisons *and* swaps. The shift-based version is preferred and scores more marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the off-by-one:** The outer loop runs from $i = 1$ to $i = n - 1$, which is $n - 1$ passes — not $n$ passes. The summation upper limit must be $n - 1$.
> 3. **Omitting the condition for best case:** Simply writing $\Omega(n)$ without stating "when the array is already sorted" will cost you at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Not closing the inner loop properly:** In the dry run, always show the value of $j$ after the loop terminates, then explicitly state the insertion at $A[j+1]$. Examiners look for this final step.

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4, Choice B]**

### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Insertion Sort algorithm with a neat algorithm/pseudocode. How is it different from Bubble Sort in terms of the number of swaps performed?

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Algorithm (5 Marks):**

```
ALGORITHM: InsertionSort(A[0..n-1])
INPUT: An array A of n elements
OUTPUT: Array A sorted in ascending order

1. FOR i = 1 TO n-1 DO
2.     key = A[i]
3.     j = i - 1
4.     WHILE j >= 0 AND A[j] > key DO
5.         A[j+1] = A[j]        // Shift, not swap
6.         j = j - 1
7.     END WHILE
8.     A[j+1] = key              // Place key in correct position
9. END FOR
10. RETURN A
```

**Explanation (1 Mark):** At the start of iteration $i$, elements $A[0], A[1], \ldots, A[i-1]$ form a sorted sub-array. The algorithm extracts $A[i]$ as `key` and shifts all elements greater than `key` one position right, creating a vacant slot at $A[j+1]$ where `key` is inserted.

**Comparison with Bubble Sort (1 Mark):**
- **Bubble Sort** performs $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ **swaps** in the worst case, and each swap costs 3 assignments.
- **Insertion Sort** performs at most $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ **shifts** in the worst case, and each shift costs only 1 assignment.
- Hence, **Insertion Sort is approximately 3× faster than Bubble Sort in practice** for the same worst-case input.

---

### (b) [7 Marks] Sort the array $A = [8, 3, 9, 1, 5]$ using Insertion Sort. Count the total number of comparisons and shifts performed.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Initial:** $A = [8, 3, 9, 1, 5]$. Total $n = 5$.

| **Pass $i$** | **Key** | **Comparisons** | **Shifts** | **Array After Pass** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $3$ | $1$ ($8>3$) | $1$ | $[3, 8, 9, 1, 5]$ |
| 2 | $9$ | $1$ ($8>9$ is false) | $0$ | $[3, 8, 9, 1, 5]$ |
| 3 | $1$ | $3$ ($9>1, 8>1, 3>1$) | $3$ | $[1, 3, 8, 9, 5]$ |
| 4 | $5$ | $3$ ($9>5, 8>5, 3>5$ is false) | $2$ | $[1, 3, 5, 8, 9]$ |

- **Total Comparisons:** $1 + 1 + 3 + 3 = 8$ (2 Marks for tabulation)
- **Total Shifts:** $1 + 0 + 3 + 2 = 6$ (1 Mark)
- **Final Sorted Array:** $[1, 3, 5, 8, 9]$ ✅ (1 Mark)
- **Verification formula:** Worst-case shifts for $n=5$ would be $\frac{5 \cdot 4}{2} = 10$. We got only $6$ shifts because the input is neither sorted nor reverse-sorted. (1 Mark)

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 Important Things to Remember Before Your KTU Exam:**
>
> ✅ **Core Idea:** Build the sorted array incrementally by inserting each new element into its correct position in the sorted sub-array.
>
> ✅ **Algorithm Structure:** One outer `for` loop ($i = 1$ to $n-1$) + one inner `while` loop for shifting.
>
> ✅ **Three Complexities to Memorize:**
> - Best Case: $\Omega(n)$ — input already sorted
> - Average Case: $\Theta(n^2)$ — random input
> - Worst Case: $O(n^2)$ — input in reverse order
>
> ✅ **Space Complexity:** $O(1)$ — strictly in-place, no extra array.
>
> ✅ **Stability:** Insertion Sort is **stable** because it shifts only elements strictly greater than the key (uses `$>$`, not `$\geq$`).
>
> ✅ **Adaptive Property:** Linear time on nearly sorted data — this is its biggest advantage over Bubble and Selection Sort.
>
> ✅ **Online Nature:** Can process streaming input element by element without needing the full array in memory.
>
> ✅ **Worst-Case Comparison Count:** $T(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = \binom{n}{2}$, derived from the arithmetic series sum.
>
> ✅ **Always present the dry run as a table** with columns: Pass | Key | Comparisons | Shifts | Resulting Array. Examiners reward clarity.
>
> ✅ **In viva:** Be ready to explain why the inner loop uses `$j \geq 0$` (boundary safety) and why we insert at `$A[j+1]$` (because $j$ is decremented one extra time after the last successful shift).
>
> ✅ **Comparison to siblings:** Insertion Sort ≫ Bubble Sort (in practice, due to fewer assignments) and Insertion Sort ≫ Selection Sort (in best case, due to adaptive nature).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quick Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quick Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quick Sort** is an efficient, comparison-based, in-place, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm developed by British computer scientist **Sir Charles Antony Richard Hoare** in **1959**. It operates by selecting an element from the array called the **pivot**, and then **partitioning** the array into two sub-arrays: one containing elements *less than or equal to* the pivot, and another containing elements *greater than* the pivot. The pivot is then placed in its **final sorted position**, and the algorithm recursively sorts the two sub-arrays.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim essence):**
> *Quick Sort is a recursive divide-and-conquer algorithm that sorts an array by selecting a pivot element and rearranging the array such that all elements smaller than the pivot are on its left and all elements greater are on its right. The process is then applied recursively to the two sub-arrays formed on either side of the pivot.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Shelf" Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian tasked with arranging **100 books of varying thicknesses** on a shelf in ascending order of thickness. Instead of comparing every pair of books (which would take forever), you:

1. **Pick a reference book** (the *pivot*) — say, the 5th book on the shelf.
2. **Sweep the entire shelf once**: place all thinner books to its left, all thicker books to its right.
3. The reference book is now in its **exact final position** forever.
4. You then **repeat the same process** independently on the left pile and the right pile, until every pile has only one book (or none).

Quick Sort does exactly this — one efficient linear sweep puts the pivot in its permanent place, and the recursive calls handle the rest. This is the magic of Quick Sort: **one pass gives you one element in its absolute correct position**.

## 1.3 Why Quick Sort? (Key Properties)

| Property | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **In-Place** | **Yes** (uses $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ stack space) | Memory-efficient, ideal for embedded/constrained systems |
| **Stable** | **No** | Original order of equal keys may be disturbed |
| **Adaptive** | Partially (faster on randomized data) | Performance varies with input distribution |
| **Comparison-Based** | **Yes** | Lower bound $\Omega(n \log n)$ applies |
| **Practical Speed** | **Fastest general-purpose sort** in most cases | Used in C `qsort`, Unix sort, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU:** Quick Sort's **average-case** complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$, which matches the theoretical lower bound for comparison-based sorting. This is why it is the *de-facto* choice in real-world engineering (e.g., database engines, language runtimes, OS kernels).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Quick Sort

### **Pillar 1: Pivot Selection**

The pivot can be chosen in several ways. The choice dramatically affects performance.

| Strategy | Description | Worst Case Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **First Element** | $pivot = A[\text{low}]$ | High (sorted input) |
| **Last Element** | $pivot = A[\text{high}]$ | High (sorted input) |
| **Middle Element** | $pivot = A[\text{mid}]$ | Moderate |
| **Random Element** | $pivot = A[\text{random}]$ | **Low** (probabilistic) |
| **Median-of-Three** | Median of first, middle, last | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite:** The **Median-of-Three** pivot selection is the most exam-relevant. It picks the median of $A[\text{low}]$, $A[\text{mid}]$, and $A[\text{high}]$, giving near-optimal balance and avoiding the pathological $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ case on already-sorted data.

### **Pillar 2: Partition Algorithms**

Two partition schemes are universally tested in KTU examinations:

#### **(A) Lomuto Partition** (Simpler, easier to trace in exams)

```
Algorithm LomutoPartition(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[high]
    i ← low - 1
    for j ← low to high - 1 do
        if A[j] ≤ pivot then
            i ← i + 1
            swap(A[i], A[j])
        end if
    end for
    swap(A[i + 1], A[high])
    return i + 1
```

#### **(B) Hoare's Partition** (Faster, more swaps avoided)

```
Algorithm HoarePartition(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[low]
    i ← low - 1
    j ← high + 1
    loop
        do i ← i + 1 while A[i] < pivot
        do j ← j - 1 while A[j] > pivot
        if i ≥ j then
            return j
        end if
        swap(A[i], A[j])
    end loop
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference (Exam Tip):** Lomuto places the pivot in its **exact final position** and returns the index of the pivot. Hoare's partition does **NOT** guarantee the pivot's final position; it returns the *split point* of the two partitions.

### **Pillar 3: Recursive Divide-and-Conquer**

After partitioning, Quick Sort recursively sorts the left and right sub-arrays.

```
Algorithm QuickSort(A, low, high):
    if low < high then
        p ← Partition(A, low, high)   // Returns pivot's final index
        QuickSort(A, low, p - 1)        // Sort left sub-array
        QuickSort(A, p + 1, high)       // Sort right sub-array
    end if
```

The base case is `low ≥ high` (sub-array has 0 or 1 element — already sorted).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Property | Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Best-Case Recurrence** | $T(n) = 2\,T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | Pivot is exact median |
| **Average-Case Recurrence** | $T(n) = 2\,T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | Randomized or median-of-three |
| **Worst-Case Recurrence** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ | Pivot is min/max every time |
| **Best-Case Complexity** | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ | Ideal partitioning |
| **Average-Case Complexity** | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ | Expected with random pivot |
| **Worst-Case Complexity** | $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ | Already-sorted input + bad pivot |
| **Auxiliary Space** | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ | Recursion stack depth |
| **Number of Comparisons (avg)** | $\approx 2\,n \ln n \approx 1.386\,n \log_2 n$ | Standard average |
| **Partition Cost** | $\Theta(n)$ | Single linear pass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Rule:** Notice that all absolute-value and magnitude expressions are written using $\Theta(\cdot)$, $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$, etc. — **never** use the literal vertical bar `|` symbol inside KTU tables, as it breaks markdown parsing. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if needed in prose.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Quick Sort is the **workhorse of systems software** due to its:
- **Cache locality** — sequential access patterns are friendly to CPU caches.
- **Low constant factor** — fewer data movements than Merge Sort.
- **In-place operation** — no auxiliary arrays (unlike Merge Sort's $\mathcal{O}(n)$ extra space).

**Real-world deployments:** C/C++ standard library `qsort`, Java's `Arrays.sort` for primitive types (uses Dual-Pivot Quick Sort since Java 7), Linux kernel's in-memory sorting, V8 JavaScript engine's sort for arrays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Time Complexity (All Three Cases)

### **Case 1: Best Case — $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$**

When the pivot is the **exact median** at every recursive call, the array is split into two equal halves of size $n/2$ each.

$$
T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c\,n
$$

**Solving via Master Theorem** (where $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = c\,n$):

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$, we are in **Case 2** of the Master Theorem.

$$
T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \log n\right) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### **Case 2: Worst Case — $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$**

When the pivot is always the **smallest (or largest)** element, the partition is maximally unbalanced: one sub-array of size $n-1$ and one of size $0$.

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + c\,n = T(n-1) + c\,n
$$

**Solving by unrolling the recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + c\,n \\
     &= T(n-2) + c(n-1) + c\,n \\
     &= T(n-3) + c(n-2) + c(n-1) + c\,n \\
     &\;\;\vdots \\
     &= T(1) + c\sum_{k=2}^{n} k \\
     &= T(1) + c\!\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

### **Case 3: Average Case — $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$**

Assuming **uniform random distribution** of pivot positions, every split ratio from $0:n-1$ to $n-1:0$ is equally likely.

$$
T(n) = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{q=0}^{n-1}\bigl[T(q) + T(n-q-1)\bigr] + c\,n
$$

By symmetry, this simplifies to:

$$
T(n) = \frac{2}{n}\sum_{q=0}^{n-1} T(q) + c\,n
$$

Multiplying by $n$ and using the standard induction technique, the result is:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why average is so good:** Even though a *single* bad split is costly, the probability of repeatedly bad splits is astronomically small. The **expected** number of comparisons is $2\,n \ln n$, which is only about $39\%$ more than the theoretical lower bound.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Lomuto Partition

**Sort the array:** $A = [10,\; 7,\; 8,\; 9,\; 1,\; 5]$ using Quick Sort (Lomuto, last element as pivot).

### **Initial Call:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 5)`

- $pivot = A[5] = 5$
- $i = -1$

| Step $j$ | $A[j]$ | $A[j] \le 5$? | Action | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $j=0$ | $10$ | No | Continue | $[10,7,8,9,1,5]$ |
| $j=1$ | $7$  | No | Continue | $[10,7,8,9,1,5]$ |
| $j=2$ | $8$  | No | Continue | $[10,7,8,9,1,5]$ |
| $j=3$ | $9$  | No | Continue | $[10,7,8,9,1,5]$ |
| $j=4$ | $1$  | **Yes** | $i=0$, swap $A[0],A[4]$ | $[\mathbf{1},7,8,9,\mathbf{10},5]$ |

**After loop:** swap $A[i+1]=A[0]$ and $A[\text{high}]=A[5]$:

$$
A = [1, \mathbf{5}, 8, 9, 10, 7]
$$

Pivot $5$ is at index $1$ (its **final sorted position** ✓). Partition point $p = 1$.

### **Recursive Call 1:** `QuickSort(A, 0, 0)` — base case, returns.

### **Recursive Call 2:** `QuickSort(A, 2, 5)` — sort right sub-array $[8,9,10,7]$

- $pivot = A[5] = 7$
- $i = 1$

| Step $j$ | $A[j]$ | $A[j] \le 7$? | Action | Array State |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $j=2$ | $8$ | No | Continue | $[1,5,8,9,10,7]$ |
| $j=3$ | $9$ | No | Continue | $[1,5,8,9,10,7]$ |
| $j=4$ | $10$ | No | Continue | $[1,5,8,9,10,7]$ |

**After loop:** swap $A[2]$ and $A[5]$:

$$
A = [1, 5, \mathbf{7}, 9, 10, 8]
$$

Pivot $7$ at index $2$ ✓. Partition point $p = 2$.

### **Recursive Call 3:** `QuickSort(A, 3, 5)` — sort $[9,10,8]$

- $pivot = A[5] = 8$, $i = 2$
- $j=3$: $9 > 8$, continue
- $j=4$: $10 > 8$, continue
- After loop: swap $A[3]$ and $A[5]$:

$$
A = [1, 5, 7, \mathbf{8}, 10, 9]
$$

### **Recursive Call 4:** `QuickSort(A, 4, 5)` — sort $[10, 9]$

- $pivot = A[5] = 9$, $i = 3$
- $j=4$: $10 > 9$, continue
- After loop: swap $A[4]$ and $A[5]$:

$$
A = [1, 5, 7, 8, \mathbf{9}, 10]
$$

### **Final Sorted Array:**

$$
A = [1, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10] \;\;\blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Quick Sort vs. Other Sorts

| Algorithm | Best | Average | Worst | Space | Stable | Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quick Sort** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ | No | Divide & Conquer |
| **Merge Sort** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | Yes | Divide & Conquer |
| **Heap Sort** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | No | Selection-based |
| **Insertion Sort** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Yes | Incremental |
| **Bubble Sort** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Yes | Exchange-based |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Quick Sort — Top-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below shows the **control flow architecture** of Quick Sort — the call hierarchy, partition logic, and the recursive decomposition that is the heart of the algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Call QuickSort A low high]) --> Check{low less than high}
    Check -- No --> Base([Base case return])
    Check -- Yes --> CallPart[Invoke Partition A low high]
    CallPart --> GetPivot[Select pivot element]
    GetPivot --> Scan[Linear scan j from low to high minus 1]
    Scan --> Compare{A j less than or equal to pivot}
    Compare -- Yes --> IncI[Increment i and swap]
    Compare -- No --> Skip[Continue]
    IncI --> MoreJ{More j values}
    Skip --> MoreJ
    MoreJ -- Yes --> Scan
    MoreJ -- No --> PlacePivot[Swap A i plus 1 with A high]
    PlacePivot --> ReturnP[Return pivot index p]
    ReturnP --> RecLeft[Recursive call QuickSort A low p minus 1]
    ReturnP --> RecRight[Recursive call QuickSort A p plus 1 high]
    RecLeft --> Check
    RecRight --> Check
    Base --> End([Return])
    Check -.-> End
```

## 4.2 Partitioning Process — Block-Level Schematic

The following block diagram illustrates how a single partition step transforms an unsorted array into two sub-arrays relative to the pivot. The pivot is shown as the reference marker that all comparisons are made against.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input["INPUT ARRAY A"]
        A1[10] --> A2[7] --> A3[8] --> A4[9] --> A5[1] --> A6[5 PIVOT]
    end
    subgraph Process["PARTITION SCAN"]
        P1[Initialize i equals low minus 1] --> P2[Loop j from low to high minus 1]
        P2 --> P3{A j less than or equal to pivot}
        P3 -- Yes --> P4[Increment i and swap A i with A j]
        P3 -- No --> P2
        P4 --> P2
        P2 -- End of loop --> P5[Swap A i plus 1 with pivot]
    end
    subgraph Output["PARTITIONED RESULT"]
        L1[1] --> L2[5 PIVOT FINAL] --> R1[8] --> R2[9] --> R3[10] --> R4[7]
    end
    Input --> Process
    Process --> Output
    style L2 fill:yellow
    style A6 fill:yellow
```

## 4.3 Recursion Tree — Worked Example Trace

The recursion tree below shows the complete execution trace of the worked example from Section 3.2. Each node represents one call to `QuickSort`, and the sub-array ranges being processed.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["QuickSort 0 to 5 — Array 10 7 8 9 1 5"]
    R0 --> R1["QuickSort 0 to 0 — Base case"]
    R0 --> R2["QuickSort 2 to 5 — Array 8 9 10 7"]
    R2 --> R3["QuickSort 2 to 1 — Base case"]
    R2 --> R4["QuickSort 3 to 5 — Array 9 10 8"]
    R4 --> R5["QuickSort 3 to 2 — Base case"]
    R4 --> R6["QuickSort 4 to 5 — Array 10 9"]
    R6 --> R7["QuickSort 4 to 3 — Base case"]
    R6 --> R8["QuickSort 6 to 5 — Base case"]
    style R0 fill:lightblue
    style R2 fill:lightblue
    style R4 fill:lightblue
    style R6 fill:lightblue
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the tree:** Each `QuickSort(low, high)` node spawns two children. **Left child** is the call to sort elements *before* the pivot; **right child** sorts elements *after* the pivot. Leaves (single-element or empty ranges) terminate recursion. The depth of this tree is $\Theta(\log n)$ in the average case, which is why auxiliary space is $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

## 4.4 Pivot Selection Strategy — Decision Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Choose pivot strategy] --> Q1{Is input randomized}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S1[Use random pivot]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is input sorted or nearly sorted}
    Q2 -- Yes --> S2[Use median of three]
    Q2 -- No --> S3[Use last or first element Lomuto]
    style S1 fill:lightgreen
    style S2 fill:lightgreen
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Quick Sort. Explain the role of the pivot element in Quick Sort with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

Quick Sort is a recursive, **divide-and-conquer**, in-place comparison-based sorting algorithm developed by **C.A.R. Hoare** in 1959.

**Role of the pivot:**
- The pivot acts as a **reference element** that is used to partition the array into two sub-arrays.
- After partitioning, all elements **less than or equal to** the pivot are placed to its **left**, and all elements **greater than** the pivot are placed to its **right**.
- The pivot is then in its **final sorted position**, and Quick Sort is recursively applied to the two sub-arrays.

**Example:** For array $[3, 8, 1, 7, 2]$ with pivot $= 2$, after partitioning we get $[1, \mathbf{2}, 8, 7, 3]$. The element $2$ is now permanently in its correct position.

> **[Marks Distribution:** Definition 1M, Role of pivot 1M, Example 1M**]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List and briefly explain any three strategies for selecting the pivot element in Quick Sort.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **First Element as Pivot:** $pivot = A[\text{low}]$. Simple to implement, but degrades to $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ on already-sorted input.

2. **Random Element as Pivot:** $pivot = A[\text{random index}]$. Probabilistically guarantees $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ expected time, immune to input order.

3. **Median-of-Three as Pivot:** The median of $A[\text{low}]$, $A[\text{mid}]$, and $A[\text{high}]$ is chosen. Combines robustness and simplicity — excellent average performance and good resistance to worst-case inputs.

> **[Marks Distribution:** Each strategy 1M**]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is provided between two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the Quick Sort algorithm in detail. Write the algorithm using the **Lomuto partition** scheme. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Logic (Step-by-step):**

1. If the array has more than one element (i.e., $low < high$), select a pivot.
2. **Partition** the array around the pivot using Lomuto's scheme:
   - Choose $pivot = A[\text{high}]$ (last element).
   - Maintain index $i$ of the "smaller element" boundary.
   - Traverse $j$ from $low$ to $high - 1$. Whenever $A[j] \le pivot$, increment $i$ and swap $A[i]$ with $A[j]$.
   - After the loop, place the pivot in its correct position by swapping $A[i+1]$ with $A[\text{high}]$.
3. Recursively call Quick Sort on the left sub-array ($low$ to $p-1$).
4. Recursively call Quick Sort on the right sub-array ($p+1$ to $high$).

**Algorithm:**

```
Algorithm QuickSort(A, low, high):
    if low < high then
        p ← LomutoPartition(A, low, high)
        QuickSort(A, low, p - 1)
        QuickSort(A, p + 1, high)
    end if

Algorithm LomutoPartition(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[high]
    i ← low - 1
    for j ← low to high - 1 do
        if A[j] ≤ pivot then
            i ← i + 1
            swap(A[i], A[j])
        end if
    end for
    swap(A[i + 1], A[high])
    return i + 1
```

**Complexity:** Best and Average $\Theta(n \log n)$, Worst $\Theta(n^2)$, Space $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.

---

**(b)** Sort the array $[38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$ using Quick Sort. Show all partitioning steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Using **Lomuto partition** with last element as pivot.

**Pass 1:** `QuickSort(0, 6)`, $pivot = A[6] = 10$

- Scan and find elements $\le 10$: $A[3] = 3$ is the only one.
- After partition: $[3, 10, 43, 38, 9, 82, 27]$, $p = 1$

**Pass 2 (left):** `QuickSort(0, 0)` — base case.

**Pass 3 (right):** `QuickSort(2, 6)`, $pivot = A[6] = 27$, on sub-array $[43, 38, 9, 82, 27]$

- Elements $\le 27$: $A[4] = 9$. Swap: $[9, 38, 43, 82, 27]$
- Final swap of pivot: $[9, 27, 43, 82, 38]$, $p = 2$

**Pass 4 (right of Pass 3):** `QuickSort(3, 6)`, $pivot = A[6] = 38$, on sub-array $[43, 82, 38]$

- No element $\le 38$. Final swap: $[38, 82, 43]$, $p = 3$

**Pass 5:** `QuickSort(4, 6)`, $pivot = A[6] = 43$, on sub-array $[82, 43]$

- No element $\le 43$. Final swap: $[43, 82]$, $p = 4$

**Final Sorted Array:** $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$ ✓

> **[Marks Distribution (a):** Explanation 3M, Algorithm 3M, Complexity 1M**]**
> **[Marks Distribution (b):** Each pass 1M, Final answer 2M**]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Re-appearing Module Test Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain **Hoare's partition scheme**. Compare it with the **Lomuto partition** scheme. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hoare's Partition Algorithm:**

```
Algorithm HoarePartition(A, low, high):
    pivot ← A[low]
    i ← low - 1
    j ← high + 1
    loop
        repeat i ← i + 1 until A[i] ≥ pivot
        repeat j ← j - 1 until A[j] ≤ pivot
        if i ≥ j then
            return j
        end if
        swap(A[i], A[j])
    end loop
```

**How it works:** Two pointers start at the extremes and move **towards each other**. The left pointer $i$ moves right past elements $< pivot$; the right pointer $j$ moves left past elements $> pivot$. When both stop, the elements are swapped. When the pointers cross, partition is complete.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Lomuto | Hoare |
|---|---|---|
| **Pivot Position** | Last element | First element |
| **Pointers Used** | One ($j$), with $i$ as boundary | Two ($i$ and $j$) |
| **Direction** | Forward only | Bidirectional (converging) |
| **Pivot's Final Position** | Guaranteed | **NOT** guaranteed |
| **Return Value** | Index of pivot | Index of split point |
| **Number of Swaps** | More | Fewer |
| **Efficiency** | Less efficient | More efficient |
| **Simplicity** | Easier to implement & trace | Harder to implement |

---

**(b)** Derive the **best-case and worst-case time complexity** of Quick Sort using recurrence relations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Best Case:**
When the pivot is always the **median**, the array splits into two equal halves:

$$
T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c\,n
$$

Using the Master Theorem with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = c\,n$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = n
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n)$, we apply **Case 2** of the Master Theorem:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Worst Case:**
When the pivot is always the **smallest or largest** element, the split is $(0 : n-1)$:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + T(0) + c\,n = T(n-1) + c\,n
$$

Expanding the recurrence:

$$
T(n) = T(n-1) + c\,n = T(n-2) + c(n-1) + c\,n = \cdots
$$

Summing the arithmetic series:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + c\,(2 + 3 + 4 + \cdots + n) \\
     &= T(1) + c\!\left(\frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1\right) \\
     &= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **[Marks Distribution (a):** Hoare explanation 3M, Comparison table 3M, Conclusion 1M**]**
> **[Marks Distribution (b):** Best case 3M, Worst case 4M**]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Quick Sort questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** `if (low < high)` — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Lomuto and Hoare's return values** — Lomuto returns the pivot's final index; Hoare returns the **split point** (NOT the pivot's index). This is a classic 2-mark trap.
> 3. **Incorrect swap logic in Lomuto** — forgetting the final swap `swap(A[i+1], A[high])` that places the pivot.
> 4. **Tracing the wrong pivot** — students often confuse which element was chosen as pivot when tracing manually. Always mark the pivot clearly with $\mathbf{bold}$ or underline in the answer sheet.
> 5. **Mixing up recurrence relations** — best/average is $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$, but worst is $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$. Do NOT write the worst case as $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$.
> 6. **Omitting the comparison with Merge Sort / Heap Sort** — for 14-mark questions, the examiner expects a comparative discussion; just defining Quick Sort in isolation loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Quick Sort is in-place** ($\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ stack space) and **not stable**.
- **Average and best case** complexity is $\Theta(n \log n)$; **worst case** is $\Theta(n^2)$.
- **Worst case** occurs when pivot selection is poor (e.g., first/last element on already-sorted input).
- **Randomized pivot** or **median-of-three** mitigates worst-case behaviour probabilistically.
- **Lomuto partition** is simpler; **Hoare's partition** is faster (fewer swaps).
- The pivot ends up in its **final sorted position** after partitioning (Lomuto) — this is the key invariant.
- Quick Sort has a **small constant factor** in practice, making it **faster than Merge Sort** in most real-world scenarios despite the same asymptotic complexity.
- **Master Theorem** solves the best case: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$.
- **Worst case derivation** uses unrolling: $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn \Rightarrow \Theta(n^2)$.
- Quick Sort is the **default sort in C's `qsort`**, **Java's `Arrays.sort`** (for primitives — Dual-Pivot Quick Sort), and **many database engines**.
- **Stability** is sacrificed for speed — if you need a stable sort, prefer Merge Sort.
- **In the KTU exam**, always show the **complete trace** of the partition steps; partial traces lose marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Merge Sort and Heap Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Merge Sort and Heap Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Merge Sort — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merge Sort** is a comparison-based, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that recursively divides an unsorted array of size **n** into two equal halves, sorts them independently, and then **merges** the two sorted sub-arrays to produce a single sorted output. It guarantees a worst-case time complexity of **O(n log n)** and is a **stable** sort.

- **Paradigm**: Divide and Conquer (non-in-place, recursive).
- **Stability**: **Stable** — equal keys retain their original relative order.
- **In-place?** No — requires auxiliary memory of size **O(n)**.
- **Best / Average / Worst Case**: All three are **Θ(n log n)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Card Trick"

Imagine you are sorting a deck of 52 cards. You cannot hold the whole messy pile in your hand at once. So, you:
1. **Split** the deck into two halves (left pile, right pile).
2. Recursively repeat this until every pile has just one card (a single card is trivially sorted).
3. **Merge**: Pick up two single-card piles, compare their top cards, and place the smaller one onto a new output pile. Continue until both are exhausted.

That last step is the heart of merge sort — the **merge routine**. Splitting is cheap; merging is the actual work.

---

## 1.2 Heap Sort — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heap Sort** is a comparison-based, in-place sorting algorithm that uses a **Binary Heap** data structure (specifically a **Max-Heap**) to sort elements. The largest element is repeatedly extracted from the root, swapped with the last unsorted element, and the heap is **re-heapified** via a *sift-down* operation. It runs in **O(n log n)** time for all cases and uses **O(1)** extra memory.

- **Data Structure Used**: **Complete Binary Tree** implemented as an array (implicit heap).
- **Heap Property** (Max-Heap): Parent ≥ Children, i.e., $A[i] \ge A[2i+1]$ and $A[i] \ge A[2i+2]$.
- **Stability**: **Unstable** — equal keys may be reordered.
- **In-place?** **Yes** — only O(1) auxiliary memory.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tournament Bracket"

Picture a knockout tennis tournament. The best player keeps rising to the top of the bracket after every match. The **champion** (the maximum) sits at the very top. You then:
1. **Crown** the champion (it's the maximum — send it to the end of the array).
2. Promote the next-best player via play-offs (sift-down).
3. Repeat until the entire array is sorted from the back.

The tournament bracket is the heap; the "play-off matches" are comparisons during sift-down.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Together? (KTU Examiner's View)

| Property | Merge Sort | Heap Sort |
|---|---|---|
| Recurrence | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + O(\log n)$ |
| Extra Space | **O(n)** | **O(1)** |
| Stability | **Stable** | **Unstable** |
| Cache Performance | **Excellent** (sequential access) | **Poor** (jumps in array) |
| Use Case | Linked lists, external sorting | Memory-constrained environments |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently pairs these two in **Part B 14-mark questions** because they allow comparison of *space-time trade-offs* and *divide-and-conquer vs. iterative heap-based* strategies.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Merge Sort — Operational Mechanics

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Base Case**: If the sub-array has size $\le 1$, it is already sorted. **Return immediately.**
2. **Divide Step**: Find the midpoint $m = \lfloor (l + r) / 2 \rfloor$. Split the array into two halves:
   - Left sub-array: indices $[l, m]$
   - Right sub-array: indices $[m+1, r]$
3. **Conquer Step**: Recursively apply merge sort on both halves.
4. **Combine Step (Merge)**: Use a two-pointer technique to merge the two sorted halves into a single sorted array, copying back to the original.

### Recurrence Derivation

Let $T(n)$ be the time to sort $n$ elements.

- Divide: $O(1)$ (compute midpoint).
- Conquer: $2 \cdot T(n/2)$ (sort two halves of size $n/2$).
- Combine (Merge): $O(n)$ (each element is visited a constant number of times).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + O(n) \\
T(1) &= O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **Master Theorem (Case 2)**: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^{1} = n$, and $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$. Therefore $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

### The Merge Routine — Detailed Pseudocode Logic

Given two sorted sub-arrays $L[0 \ldots n_1-1]$ and $R[0 \ldots n_2-1]$:

1. Maintain three pointers: $i = 0$, $j = 0$, $k = l$ (start index in original array).
2. While $i < n_1$ AND $j < n_2$: compare $L[i]$ and $R[j]$, copy the smaller to $A[k]$, advance the corresponding pointer.
3. Copy any remaining elements of $L$ and $R$ into $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Note**: The merge step uses $\le$ (not $<$) when copying from the *right* sub-array. This is critical for stability. KTU examiners explicitly test this.

---

## 2.2 Heap Sort — Operational Mechanics

### Implicit Array Representation of a Heap

For a node at index $i$ (0-indexed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Parent}(i) &= \lfloor (i - 1) / 2 \rfloor \\
\text{LeftChild}(i) &= 2i + 1 \\
\text{RightChild}(i) &= 2i + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 1 — Build Max-Heap (Heapify)

**Approach 1 (Naive)**: Insert elements one by one using sift-up → $O(n \log n)$.

**Approach 2 (Optimal — used in KTU syllabus)**: Starting from the **last non-leaf node** $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor - 1$ down to index $0$, perform **sift-down** (also called *heapify* or *max-heapify*) on each node. This runs in **O(n)** time, despite each sift-down costing $O(\log n)$.

### Phase 2 — Sorting

1. **Swap** $A[0]$ (maximum) with $A[n-1]$.
2. **Reduce** heap size by 1.
3. Call **sift-down** on root $A[0]$ to restore the max-heap property.
4. Repeat until heap size = 1.

### Recurrence for Heap Sort

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n - 1) + O(\log n) \\
T(1) &= O(1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Unrolling this gives a telescoping sum of $\log n + \log(n-1) + \ldots + \log 1 = O(n \log n)$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Merge Sort | Heap Sort |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Complexity (Best)** | $\Omega(n \log n)$ | $\Omega(n \log n)$ |
| **Time Complexity (Average)** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Time Complexity (Worst)** | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ auxiliary | $O(1)$ in-place |
| **Recurrence Relation** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c\log n$ |
| **Master Theorem Case** | **Case 2** | Not directly applicable |
| **Stable?** | **Yes** | **No** |
| **In-place?** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Number of Comparisons (worst)** | $n \log n - n + 1$ | $\approx 2n \log n$ |
| **Recursion Stack Depth** | $\log_2 n$ | $O(1)$ (iterative) |
| **Adaptive?** | No (mostly) | No |
| **Parallelizable?** | Highly (divide phase) | No |
| **Best Used For** | Linked lists, external sort, stable sort needs | Memory-constrained in-place sorting |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Merge Sort** powers database engines (e.g., PostgreSQL's external sort runs), `Timsort` (Python's built-in `sorted()`) is a hybrid of merge sort and insertion sort, and external sorting of multi-GB files on disk.
- **Heap Sort** is the foundation of **priority queues**, **Dijkstra's algorithm**, and **schedulers** in operating systems. It is the in-place $O(n \log n)$ guarantee, making it ideal for embedded systems and memory-constrained devices.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Merge Sort Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10]$

### Phase 1 — Recursive Splitting (Divide)

We split until sub-arrays of size 1 are reached:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] \\
\text{Split at mid} &\rightarrow [38, 27, 43, 3] \text{ and } [9, 82, 10] \\
[38, 27, 43, 3] &\rightarrow [38, 27] \text{ and } [43, 3] \\
[9, 82, 10] &\rightarrow [9] \text{ and } [82, 10] \\
[38, 27] &\rightarrow [38] \text{ and } [27] \\
[43, 3] &\rightarrow [43] \text{ and } [3] \\
[82, 10] &\rightarrow [82] \text{ and } [10]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Phase 2 — Merging (Combine)

Now we merge bottom-up, with three-pointer comparison:

**Merge 1**: $[38]$ and $[27]$ → compare $38 > 27$ → output $[27, 38]$

**Merge 2**: $[43]$ and $[3]$ → compare $43 > 3$ → output $[3, 43]$

**Merge 3**: $[82]$ and $[10]$ → compare $82 > 10$ → output $[10, 82]$

**Merge 4**: $[27, 38]$ and $[3, 43]$

| Step | $i$ | $j$ | $L[i]$ | $R[j]$ | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 27 | 3 | [3] |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 27 | 43 | [3, 27] |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 38 | 43 | [3, 27, 38] |
| 4 | 2 | 1 | – | 43 | [3, 27, 38, 43] |

→ Output: $[3, 27, 38, 43]$

**Merge 5**: $[9]$ and $[10, 82]$

| Step | $i$ | $j$ | $L[i]$ | $R[j]$ | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 9 | 10 | [9] |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | – | 10 | [9, 10] |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | – | 82 | [9, 10, 82] |

→ Output: $[9, 10, 82]$

**Final Merge**: $[3, 27, 38, 43]$ and $[9, 10, 82]$

| Step | $i$ | $j$ | $L[i]$ | $R[j]$ | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 3 | 9 | [3] |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 27 | 9 | [3, 9] |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 27 | 10 | [3, 9, 10] |
| 4 | 1 | 2 | 27 | 82 | [3, 9, 10, 27] |
| 5 | 2 | 2 | 38 | 82 | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38] |
| 6 | 3 | 2 | 43 | 82 | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43] |
| 7 | 4 | 2 | – | 82 | [3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82] |

**Final Sorted Array:** $[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]$ ✓

### Recurrence Verification

Total comparisons: $\approx n \log_2 n = 7 \times 2.8 \approx 19.6$. Actual $= 7 + 7 + 5 + 3 + 2 = 24$ (close to upper bound, well within $O(n \log n)$).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Heap Sort Trace

**Input Array:** $A = [4, 10, 3, 5, 1, 8, 7, 6]$ (size $n = 8$)

### Step 1 — Build Max-Heap (Bottom-Up Heapify)

Last non-leaf node index: $\lfloor 8/2 \rfloor - 1 = 3$. We heapify from index 3 down to 0.

**Heapify at $i = 3$ (value 5)**: children at 7 (value 6). Largest child = 6. $5 < 6$, swap. Array becomes: $[4, 10, 3, 6, 1, 8, 7, 5]$.

**Heapify at $i = 2$ (value 3)**: children at 5 (value 8) and 6 (value 7). Largest child = 8. $3 < 8$, swap. Array becomes: $[4, 10, 8, 6, 1, 3, 7, 5]$. Check subtree at index 5 (value 3) — no children violating property. Stop.

**Heapify at $i = 1$ (value 10)**: children at 3 (value 6) and 4 (value 1). Largest = 10 (parent). No swap.

**Heapify at $i = 0$ (value 4)**: children at 1 (value 10) and 2 (value 8). Largest = 10. $4 < 10$, swap. Array becomes: $[10, 4, 8, 6, 1, 3, 7, 5]$. Recursively heapify at index 1 (value 4): children at 3 (value 6) and 4 (value 1). Largest = 6. $4 < 6$, swap. Array becomes: $[10, 6, 8, 4, 1, 3, 7, 5]$. Check subtree at index 3 (value 4): child at 7 (value 5). $4 < 5$, swap. Array becomes: $[10, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 4]$.

**Max-Heap Built:** $A = [10, 6, 8, 5, 1, 3, 7, 4]$

### Step 2 — Sorting Phase

| Pass | Swap $A[0] \leftrightarrow A[n-1]$ | Array After Swap | Sift-Down Actions | Array After Sift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
