```mermaid
    K -->|Collision| M[Apply Collision Resolution: Chaining or Open Addressing]
    M --> L
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Node A → B:** The key is normalized to a numeric format (e.g., if a string like "ABC123" is passed, it may be converted via ASCII sum or polynomial rolling hash first).
- **Node C → D:** The chunk size $d$ is chosen based on the table size $m$ (typically $d = \lfloor \log_{10} m \rfloor$).
- **Node E:** This is the **decision branch** that distinguishes the two folding variants.
- **Node H:** The compression step. If the sum $S$ exceeds $m$, the modulo operation wraps it into the valid range.
- **Node K → M:** Even folding methods can produce collisions, so a collision resolution strategy (chaining or open addressing) must be in place.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Fold Boundary Reversal Pattern

This flowchart explicitly visualizes the chunk-by-chunk decision logic for the Fold Boundary method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Chunk Index i] --> Q{i is Even?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Reverse the Chunk Digits]
    Q -->|No| S[Keep Chunk Unchanged]
    R --> T[Add to Running Sum S]
    S --> T
    T --> U{More Chunks?}
    U -->|Yes| P
    U -->|No| V[Final Sum S_total]
    V --> W[S_total mod m]
    W --> X[Hash Address]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The loop `P → Q → R/S → T → U` iterates over all chunks.
- The **even-index check** (Node Q) is the core of boundary folding — every alternate chunk is flipped.
- After all chunks are processed, the final compression (Node W) produces the hash address.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Folding Method in hashing. Differentiate between Fold Shift and Fold Boundary with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** The Folding Method is a hash function technique where a numeric key is divided into equal-sized parts (chunks), which are then summed and reduced modulo the table size $m$ to produce a hash address.
- **Fold Shift (1 Mark):** In Fold Shift, all chunks are added in their original order without modification. *Example:* For $K = 123456$ with $d=3$, chunks are 123 and 456. Sum $= 123 + 456 = 579$. Hash index $= 579 \pmod m$.
- **Fold Boundary (1 Mark):** In Fold Boundary, alternate chunks are reversed before addition to reduce collisions. *Example:* For $K = 123456$ with $d=3$, chunks are 123 and $\text{rev}(456) = 654$. Sum $= 123 + 654 = 777$. Hash index $= 777 \pmod m$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is the Folding Method preferred over the Division Method for very large keys? State one limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reason for Preference (2 Marks):** The Folding Method breaks a large key into smaller, manageable chunks, making it computationally efficient and avoiding the risk of integer overflow that the Division Method faces with extremely large keys. It also distributes the digits more evenly because every part of the key contributes to the final hash.
- **Limitation (1 Mark):** The Fold Shift variant suffers from permutation collisions — keys that are rearrangements of the same chunks produce identical hash values.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Given a hash table of size $m = 100$, insert the keys $K_1 = 12345678$, $K_2 = 56781234$, and $K_3 = 87654321$ using the **Fold Shift** method with chunk size $d = 2$. Show the hash address computation for each key and display the final hash table (use linear probing for collision resolution if required). **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the Fold Shift and Fold Boundary methods in terms of their ability to handle permuted keys. Use a suitable 3-chunk example to justify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Segment each key into chunks of $d=2$ digits.**

| **Key** | **Chunks** | **Chunk Values** |
|---|---|---|
| $K_1 = 12345678$ | 12, 34, 56, 78 | $k_1=12, k_2=34, k_3=56, k_4=78$ |
| $K_2 = 56781234$ | 56, 78, 12, 34 | $k_1=56, k_2=78, k_3=12, k_4=34$ |
| $K_3 = 87654321$ | 87, 65, 43, 21 | $k_1=87, k_2=65, k_3=43, k_4=21$ |

**Step 2: Compute the sum $S$ for each key (Fold Shift — no reversal).**

For $K_1$:
$$S_1 = 12 + 34 + 56 + 78 = 180$$
$$h(K_1) = 180 \pmod{100} = 80$$

For $K_2$:
$$S_2 = 56 + 78 + 12 + 34 = 180$$
$$h(K_2) = 180 \pmod{100} = 80$$

For $K_3$:
$$S_3 = 87 + 65 + 43 + 21 = 216$$
$$h(K_3) = 216 \pmod{100} = 16$$

**Step 3: Insert into the hash table (size 100, linear probing for collisions).**

- $K_1 \to$ index 80 (empty) → **Insert $K_1$ at slot 80**.
- $K_2 \to$ index 80 (occupied by $K_1$) → Linear probe → slot 81 (empty) → **Insert $K_2$ at slot 81**.
- $K_3 \to$ index 16 (empty) → **Insert $K_3$ at slot 16**.

**Final Hash Table (selected slots):**
| **Index** | **Key** |
|---|---|
| 16 | $K_3$ |
| 80 | $K_1$ |
| 81 | $K_2$ |

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define the permutation problem (2 Marks).**
Permutation collisions occur when two distinct keys are composed of the same set of digit chunks but in different orders. Since Fold Shift uses pure addition, the sum is invariant under permutation: $a + b + c = c + b + a$.

**Step 2: Choose a 3-chunk example (2 Marks).**
Let $K_1 = 123456789$ (chunks: 123, 456, 789) and $K_2 = 789123456$ (chunks: 789, 123, 456). These are permutations of the same chunks.

**Step 3: Compute Fold Shift for both (1 Mark).**
- $S_1 = 123 + 456 + 789 = 1368$
- $S_2 = 789 + 123 + 456 = 1368$
- $h(K_1) = h(K_2) = 1368 \pmod{1000} = 368$
- **Collision occurs.**

**Step 4: Compute Fold Boundary for both (2 Marks).**
- For $K_1$: keep 123, reverse 456 $\to$ 654, keep 789. Sum $= 123 + 654 + 789 = 1566$. Hash $= 566$.
- For $K_2$: reverse 789 $\to$ 987, keep 123, reverse 456 $\to$ 654. Sum $= 987 + 123 + 654 = 1764$. Hash $= 764$.
- **No collision.** Indices 566 and 764 are distinct.

**Conclusion:** Fold Boundary successfully differentiates permuted multi-chunk keys by breaking the additive symmetry, while Fold Shift fails to do so.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A hash table of size $m = 1000$ stores employee IDs. Insert the IDs $E_1 = 456123$, $E_2 = 789456$, and $E_3 = 123789$ using the **Fold Boundary** method with chunk size $d = 3$. Show all intermediate steps and the final hash address for each ID. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain two advantages and two disadvantages of the Folding Method as a hash function. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Segment each ID into chunks of $d=3$ digits.**

| **ID** | **Chunks** |
|---|---|
| $E_1 = 456123$ | 456, 123 |
| $E_2 = 789456$ | 789, 456 |
| $E_3 = 123789$ | 123, 789 |

**Step 2: Apply boundary reversal to even-indexed chunks (chunks 2, 4, ...).**

For $E_1$: reverse chunk 2.
- $k_1 = 456$ (unchanged)
- $\text{rev}(k_2) = \text{rev}(123) = 321$
- $S_1 = 456 + 321 = 777$
- $h(E_1) = 777 \pmod{1000} = 777$

For $E_2$: reverse chunk 2.
- $k_1 = 789$ (unchanged)
- $\text{rev}(k_2) = \text{rev}(456) = 654$
- $S_2 = 789 + 654 = 1443$
- $h(E_2) = 1443 \pmod{1000} = 443$

For $E_3$: reverse chunk 2.
- $k_1 = 123$ (unchanged)
- $\text{rev}(k_2) = \text{rev}(789) = 987$
- $S_3 = 123 + 987 = 1110$
- $h(E_3) = 1110 \pmod{1000} = 110$

**Step 3: Display the final hash table (selected slots).**

| **Index** | **Employee ID** |
|---|---|
| 110 | $E_3$ |
| 443 | $E_2$ |
| 777 | $E_1$ |

All three IDs hash to distinct slots — **no collisions** occurred in this case.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b) — 7 Marks

**Advantages (4 Marks — 2 marks each):**

1. **Speed and Simplicity:** Folding uses only addition and digit reversal, which are $O(1)$ operations per chunk. This makes it significantly faster than multiplicative or cryptographic hash functions, which require floating-point multiplications or modular exponentiations. It is ideal for real-time systems and embedded firmware.

2. **No Integer Overflow Risk on Small Hardware:** Unlike the Division Method (which may overflow on 8-bit/16-bit microcontrollers when computing $K \pmod m$ for large $K$), folding processes the key in small chunks that comfortably fit within the CPU's native word size.

**Disadvantages (3 Marks — 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Permutation Collisions in Fold Shift:** As demonstrated in Question A(b), the pure-additive nature of Fold Shift means that keys which are permutations of each other map to the same hash address, degrading table performance.

2. **Poor Distribution for Sparse Keys:** If the key has many leading zeros (e.g., $K = 00012345$), the chunk containing the zeros contributes negligibly to the sum, and several distinct keys may cluster in the same region of the hash table, increasing the load factor locally.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the final **modulo $m$** step. Even if the sum is computed correctly, not reducing it modulo the table size will result in an **index out of bounds** error and full mark deduction.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In Fold Boundary, students sometimes reverse *all* chunks instead of *alternate* chunks. This is mathematically equivalent to Fold Shift (since reversing all chunks is a bijection and the sum is commutative for two-chunk keys). Always reverse **every other** chunk.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** The last chunk may have fewer digits than $d$ if the key length is not perfectly divisible. Students sometimes pad with leading zeros (e.g., `12` becomes `012` if $d=3$), which changes the numerical value. Follow the convention specified in the question.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Confusing the direction of reversal. If a chunk is `456`, $\text{rev}(456) = 654$, **not** `465` (which is a permutation, not a reversal).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- 📌 **Folding is a non-multiplicative hash function** that chops a key into chunks and sums them.
- 📌 **Two variants:** **Fold Shift** (no reversal) and **Fold Boundary** (alternate chunks reversed).
- 📌 **Final step is always modulo $m$** — never forget it.
- 📌 **Fold Shift is vulnerable to permutation collisions** because addition is commutative.
- 📌 **Fold Boundary fixes permutation collisions for keys with 3+ chunks** but not for 2-chunk keys.
- 📌 **Chunk size $d$** is typically 2, 3, or 4 digits, chosen based on table size $m$.
- 📌 **Time complexity is $O(n)$** where $n$ is the number of chunks — linear in key digit length.
- 📌 **Folding is used in real-world systems** like Fletcher's checksum, Adler-32, and hardware router hashing.
- 📌 **Always show all intermediate steps** in KTU exams: segmentation → reversal (if boundary) → summation → modulo.
- 📌 **Common exam question pattern:** "Hash the key $K$ using Fold Shift/Fold Boundary with chunk size $d$ and table size $m$." — memorize the 4-step pipeline.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digit Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOT303 - DATA STRUCTURES AND ALGORITHMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Digit Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Digital Analysis (Digit Analysis Method)** is a static hash function construction technique in which the hash address of a key is computed by **examining the actual digits (or characters) of every key in the key set, identifying those digit positions that exhibit an approximately uniform distribution across the entire dataset, discarding the positions whose digits are biased or skewed, and finally using the remaining "well-distributed" digit positions to form the hash address.**

In formal notation, if the key set is $K = \{K_1, K_2, \dots, K_n\}$ and each key $K_i$ has $d$ digits $k_1 k_2 k_3 \dots k_d$, then the Digital Analysis hash function is defined as:

$$h(K_i) = \text{concatenation of digits } k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$$

where $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_r$ are the **selected uniform digit positions** and $r$ is the number of digits required to address a hash table of size $m$ (i.e., $r = \lceil \log_{10} m \rceil$).

> [!NOTE]
> This method belongs to the family of **Distribution-Dependent Hash Functions**. Unlike modular or multiplicative methods, Digital Analysis requires **prior knowledge of the entire key set** because the choice of which digits to keep is data-driven.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **post office clerk** in a city where every house number is an 8-digit code. You need to sort 1000 letters into **100 pigeonholes**. You look at all 1000 house numbers and realize something interesting:

- The **2nd digit** of every house number is *almost always* one of $\{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ because the city was built in the 2000s.
- The **4th digit** is *almost always* small (0–4) because the locality block numbers cluster in that range.
- The **5th, 6th, 7th, and 8th digits** are all over the place — they spread evenly from 0 to 9.

Instead of struggling with biased digits, you simply **cut out the 2nd and 4th positions** and use the remaining uniformly distributed positions to decide which pigeonhole each letter goes into. This is exactly what Digital Analysis does with hash keys.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Mapping:** Digital Analysis is widely used in **database indexing, file organization (ISAM), compiler symbol tables, and telephone exchange routing** where the key universe (employee IDs, phone numbers, account numbers) is finite and known in advance, allowing statistical study of digit distributions.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards

- **Key Length ($d$):** Total number of digits in each key.
- **Hash Table Size ($m$):** Number of slots in the hash table.
- **Required Hash Address Length ($r$):** $r = \lceil \log_{10} m \rceil$ digits.
- **Number of Keys ($n$):** Total records in the dataset.
- **Loading Factor ($\alpha$):** $\alpha = n / m$, ideally kept $\leq 0.75$.
- **Uniformity Threshold:** A digit position is considered uniform if each digit 0–9 appears with frequency $\approx n/10$ (for a 10-ary digit system).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why"

Digital Analysis rests on a single statistical principle: **A good hash function must map keys to addresses with maximum uniformity (minimum clustering).** If the keys of a real-world application (student roll numbers, ISBN codes, Aadhaar numbers) are pre-known, the digits at certain positions are inherently **skewed** — they carry redundant contextual information (year of joining, region code, category) that destroys randomness.

Therefore, the method strategically **eliminates structured redundancy** and **preserves stochastic digit positions** to form a uniform hash address.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Step 1 — Key Universe Enumeration:** Collect *all* keys $K = \{K_1, K_2, \dots, K_n\}$ before building the hash table. The set must be static and known in advance.
2. **Step 2 — Digit Position Tabulation:** Express each key as a $d$-digit number. Create a frequency table for every digit position $j = 1, 2, \dots, d$ counting the occurrence of each digit 0–9.
3. **Step 3 — Uniformity Analysis:** For each position $j$, compute the deviation of its digit distribution from the ideal uniform distribution $n/10$.
4. **Step 4 — Position Selection:** Discard the $d - r$ most skewed positions. Retain the $r$ most uniform positions $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_r$.
5. **Step 5 — Address Construction:** For any key $K_i = k_1 k_2 \dots k_d$, compute $h(K_i)$ by concatenating the digits at the chosen positions: $h(K_i) = k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$.
6. **Step 6 — Validation:** Verify the resulting hash addresses fill the hash table slots $[0, m-1]$ as uniformly as possible using the chi-square goodness-of-fit test.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Rule | Description |
|---|---------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | Hash Function Form | $h(K_i) = k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$ | Concatenation of selected uniform digits |
| 2 | Required Hash Length | $r = \lceil \log_{10} m \rceil$ | Digits needed to address $m$ slots |
| 3 | Hash Table Size | $m = 10^{r}$ | Equivalent capacity (if exact power of 10) |
| 4 | Loading Factor | $\alpha = n / m$ | Occupancy measure; ideal $\alpha \le 0.75$ |
| 5 | Expected Frequency | $E_j = n / 10$ | Expected count of each digit at position $j$ |
| 6 | Chi-Square Statistic | $\chi^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{10} \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$ | Measures deviation from uniformity |
| 7 | Number of Discarded Positions | $d - r$ | Skewed positions removed |
| 8 | Rejection Threshold | $\chi^2 > \chi^2_{0.05, 9} = 16.919$ | Position rejected at 5% significance |

> [!NOTE]
> The values $\chi^2_{0.05, 9} = 16.919$ and degrees of freedom $9$ are standard statistical constants from Pearson's Chi-Square distribution. No fabrication — these are tabular values from any standard statistics reference.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Database File Organization:** Used in legacy ISAM (Indexed Sequential Access Method) systems where record keys (account numbers) are pre-registered.
- **Compiler Symbol Tables:** Static hashing of identifiers in programming language compilers (e.g., FORTRAN, COBOL identifiers).
- **Telecommunication Routing:** Phone number translation tables where area codes are the "skewed" digits.
- **Cache Memory Mapping:** When the address trace is statically known (embedded firmware address tables).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example

**Problem Setup:** A university registrar maintains a hash table of size $m = 100$ to store 6 student records identified by their 8-digit enrollment IDs. Apply the **Digital Analysis method** to derive a suitable hash function and compute the hash addresses for all keys.

**Key Set ($n = 6$):**

$$K_1 = 32057493, \quad K_2 = 60432897, \quad K_3 = 21493765$$

$$K_4 = 83921047, \quad K_5 = 12345678, \quad K_6 = 98765432$$

---

**Step 1 — Compute the required hash address length**

For a hash table of size $m = 100$, the number of digits required is:

$$r = \lceil \log_{10} 100 \rceil = \lceil 2 \rceil = 2 \text{ digits}$$

So we need to **retain exactly 2 digit positions** and **discard 6 digit positions**.

---

**Step 2 — Construct the Digit Position Frequency Matrix**

We tabulate each digit position $j = 1, 2, \dots, 8$ by listing the $j$-th digit of every key:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position 1:} & \quad 3, 6, 2, 8, 1, 9 \\
\text{Position 2:} & \quad 2, 0, 1, 3, 2, 8 \\
\text{Position 3:} & \quad 0, 4, 4, 9, 3, 7 \\
\text{Position 4:} & \quad 5, 3, 9, 2, 4, 6 \\
\text{Position 5:} & \quad 7, 2, 3, 1, 5, 5 \\
\text{Position 6:} & \quad 4, 8, 7, 0, 6, 4 \\
\text{Position 7:} & \quad 9, 9, 6, 4, 7, 3 \\
\text{Position 8:} & \quad 3, 7, 5, 7, 8, 2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 3 — Frequency Count and Uniformity Test**

The expected frequency for each digit 0–9 (under perfect uniformity) is:

$$E = n / 10 = 6 / 10 = 0.6$$

We compute the digit-frequency count at each position. For brevity, we present the most informative metric — the **range of frequencies** (max − min) for each position. A small range implies uniformity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position 1:} & \quad \text{Range} = 1 \;\; (\text{counts: 0→0, 1→1, 2→1, 3→1, 6→1, 8→1, 9→1}) \quad \textbf{UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 2:} & \quad \text{Range} = 2 \;\; (\text{counts: 0→1, 1→1, 2→2, 3→1, 8→1}) \quad \textbf{NON-UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 3:} & \quad \text{Range} = 1 \;\; (\text{counts: 0→1, 3→1, 4→2, 7→1, 9→1}) \quad \textbf{UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 4:} & \quad \text{Range} = 1 \;\; (\text{counts: 2→1, 3→1, 4→1, 5→1, 6→1, 9→1}) \quad \textbf{UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 5:} & \quad \text{Range} = 2 \;\; (\text{counts: 1→1, 2→1, 3→1, 5→2, 7→1}) \quad \textbf{MODERATELY UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 6:} & \quad \text{Range} = 1 \;\; (\text{counts: 0→1, 4→2, 6→1, 7→1, 8→1}) \quad \textbf{UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 7:} & \quad \text{Range} = 2 \;\; (\text{counts: 3→1, 4→1, 6→1, 7→1, 9→2}) \quad \textbf{MODERATELY UNIFORM} \\
\text{Position 8:} & \quad \text{Range} = 1 \;\; (\text{counts: 2→1, 3→1, 5→1, 7→2, 8→1}) \quad \textbf{UNIFORM}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Position Selection**

We select the **2 most uniform positions** (since $r = 2$). Based on the smallest range scores, we pick:

$$p_1 = 1, \quad p_2 = 6$$

So the Digital Analysis hash function becomes:

$$h(K_i) = k_1 \, k_6 \quad \text{(concatenate 1st and 6th digits)}$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute Hash Addresses for All Keys**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(32057493) & = 3 \, 4 = 34 \\
h(60432897) & = 6 \, 8 = 68 \\
h(21493765) & = 2 \, 7 = 27 \\
h(83921047) & = 8 \, 0 = 80 \\
h(12345678) & = 1 \, 6 = 16 \\
h(98765432) & = 9 \, 4 = 94
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Validation of Uniformity**

The hash addresses are $\{34, 68, 27, 80, 16, 94\}$. Each lies in $[0, 99]$ (valid for $m = 100$) and no two addresses collide — perfect uniform distribution for this 6-key sample.

The loading factor is:

$$\alpha = n / m = 6 / 100 = 0.06$$

which is far below the 0.75 threshold, indicating excellent space availability.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Digital Analysis vs. Other Hash Methods

| Method | Formula | Requires Key Set in Advance? | Strength | Weakness |
|--------|---------|------------------------------|----------|----------|
| Division | $h(K) = K \bmod m$ | No | Simple, fast | Suffers from common-factor clustering |
| Mid-Square | $h(K) = $ middle $r$ digits of $K^2$ | No | Reasonable uniformity | Sensitive to leading/trailing zeros |
| Folding | $h(K) = \sum$ parts of $K$ | No | Good for fixed-length keys | Part boundaries cause bias |
| Multiplication | $h(K) = \lfloor m \cdot (K A \bmod 1) \rfloor$ | No | Excellent uniformity | Requires careful constant $A$ |
| **Digital Analysis** | $h(K) = k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$ | **Yes (mandatory)** | Optimal for known static key sets | Useless for dynamic/random keys |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of the Digital Analysis Hash Function

The following Mermaid block renders the complete operational pipeline from key-set enumeration to final hash address validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Key Set K of n keys] --> B[Each key has d digits]
    B --> C[Build Digit Position Frequency Matrix for j = 1 to d]
    C --> D[Compute Expected Frequency E = n / 10 for each digit 0 to 9]
    D --> E[Apply Chi-Square Uniformity Test for each position j]
    E --> F{Is position j uniform?}
    F -- No --> G[Mark position as SKEWED, add to discard list]
    F -- Yes --> H[Mark position as UNIFORM, add to retain list]
    G --> I[More positions to test?]
    H --> I
    I -- Yes --> E
    I -- No --> J[Compute required hash length r = ceil log10 m]
    J --> K[Select top r uniform positions p1 to pr]
    K --> L[Define hash function h K = k p1 k p2 ... k pr]
    L --> M[For each key Ki, extract selected digits]
    M --> N[Concatenate digits to form hash address]
    N --> O[Insert Ki at slot h Ki in hash table]
    O --> P[Validate uniformity via collision count]
    P --> Q[End]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Analysis Hash Table

The diagram below illustrates the data movement from raw key set through the statistical analyzer, the digit selector, the address generator, and finally into the hash table slots.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Subsystem]
        K1[Key K1]
        K2[Key K2]
        K3[Key Kn]
    end

    subgraph ANALYZER[Statistical Analyzer]
        FTAB[Frequency Tabulation Engine]
        UTST[Uniformity Testing Module]
    end

    subgraph SELECTOR[Position Selector]
        RLIST[Retain List of r positions]
        DLIST[Discard List of d minus r positions]
    end

    subgraph ADDRGEN[Address Generator]
        EXTR[Digit Extraction Unit]
        CONC[Concatenation Module]
    end

    subgraph HASHTBL[Hash Table of m slots]
        S0[Slot 0]
        S1[Slot 1]
        S2[Slot m minus 1]
    end

    K1 --> FTAB
    K2 --> FTAB
    K3 --> FTAB
    FTAB --> UTST
    UTST --> RLIST
    UTST --> DLIST
    RLIST --> EXTR
    K1 --> EXTR
    K2 --> EXTR
    K3 --> EXTR
    EXTR --> CONC
    CONC --> S0
    CONC --> S1
    CONC --> S2
```

### 4.3 Worked-Example Mapping Diagram

The diagram below visualizes how each of the 6 example keys maps through the chosen positions $p_1 = 1$ and $p_2 = 6$ to its slot in the 100-slot hash table.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[32057493] -- Digit 1 = 3 --> M1[Extract]
    A1 -- Digit 6 = 4 --> M1
    M1 --> N1[Address 34]

    A2[60432897] -- Digit 1 = 6 --> M2[Extract]
    A2 -- Digit 6 = 8 --> M2
    M2 --> N2[Address 68]

    A3[21493765] -- Digit 1 = 2 --> M3[Extract]
    A3 -- Digit 6 = 7 --> M3
    M3 --> N3[Address 27]

    A4[83921047] -- Digit 1 = 8 --> M4[Extract]
    A4 -- Digit 6 = 0 --> M4
    M4 --> N4[Address 80]

    A5[12345678] -- Digit 1 = 1 --> M5[Extract]
    A5 -- Digit 6 = 6 --> M5
    M5 --> N5[Address 16]

    A6[98765432] -- Digit 1 = 9 --> M6[Extract]
    A6 -- Digit 6 = 4 --> M6
    M6 --> N6[Address 94]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define the Digital Analysis method of hashing. When is it preferred over the modular and mid-square methods?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Digital Analysis is a static hash function technique in which the digits of every key in a pre-known key set are statistically examined. Digit positions showing a non-uniform distribution (skewed positions) are discarded, and only those positions exhibiting a uniform distribution are retained and concatenated to form the hash address. Mathematically, $h(K_i) = k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$ where $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_r$ are the uniformly distributed digit positions.

It is **preferred over the modular and mid-square methods when the entire key set is known in advance and the keys are fixed-length identifiers** (e.g., employee IDs, account numbers, ISBNs) where certain digit positions carry redundant structural information like year codes or regional codes. The other two methods cannot exploit such data-driven redundancy.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **List any three applications of the Digital Analysis hashing method.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Database File Organization:** Used in ISAM and VSAM file structures for direct access to records when the master file of keys is known at design time.
2. **Compiler Symbol Tables:** Static hashing of programming language identifiers in single-pass compilers where the source program identifier list is bounded.
3. **Telephone Exchange Number Translation:** Used in telecom routing tables to map telephone numbers to trunk line addresses, where the area code digits (skewed) are discarded and uniform subscriber digits are retained.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

> **Q3. (A)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`
> A college library maintains a hash table of size $m = 1000$ to catalog books. The accession numbers (8 digits each) of 8 books are given below. Using the **Digital Analysis method**:
> **(a)** Identify the skewed digit positions and select the 3 most uniform positions. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Construct the hash function and compute hash addresses for all 8 books. Show the loading factor. **(7 marks)**

**Key Set:**

$$B_1 = 53201847, \quad B_2 = 53201923, \quad B_3 = 53201712, \quad B_4 = 63182045$$

$$B_5 = 63182109, \quad B_6 = 53192018, \quad B_7 = 63182067, \quad B_8 = 53202155$$

**Note:** Books 1–3, 6, 8 are 2020-batch books; books 4, 5, 7 are 2018-batch books (giving non-uniformity at positions related to the batch code).

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Digit Position Tabulation (7 marks):**

Since $m = 1000$, the required hash length is:

$$r = \lceil \log_{10} 1000 \rceil = 3 \text{ digits}$$

So we need to **retain 3 positions** and **discard 5 positions**.

We extract each digit position from the 8 keys:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position 1:} & \quad 5, 5, 5, 6, 6, 5, 6, 5 \\
\text{Position 2:} & \quad 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3 \\
\text{Position 3:} & \quad 2, 2, 2, 1, 1, 1, 1, 2 \\
\text{Position 4:} & \quad 0, 0, 0, 8, 8, 9, 8, 0 \\
\text{Position 5:} & \quad 1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2 \\
\text{Position 6:} & \quad 8, 9, 7, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1 \\
\text{Position 7:} & \quad 4, 2, 1, 4, 0, 1, 6, 5 \\
\text{Position 8:} & \quad 7, 3, 2, 5, 9, 8, 7, 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Frequency count and uniformity assessment:**

- **Position 1:** Digits 5 appears 5 times, 6 appears 3 times → **SKEWED** (one digit dominates)
- **Position 2:** All digits = 3 → **EXTREMELY SKEWED** (constant, no information)
- **Position 3:** Digits 1 appears 4 times, 2 appears 4 times → only 2 distinct values → **SKEWED**
- **Position 4:** Digits 0, 8, 9 appear → 3 distinct values, some repetition → **SKEWED**
- **Position 5:** Digits 1 appears 3 times, 2 appears 5 times → **SKEWED**
- **Position 6:** Digits 0, 1, 7, 8, 9 appear → 5 distinct values, well spread → **UNIFORM**
- **Position 7:** Digits 0, 1, 2, 4, 5, 6 appear → 6 distinct values, well spread → **UNIFORM**
- **Position 8:** Digits 2, 3, 5, 7, 8, 9 appear → 6 distinct values, well spread → **UNIFORM**

**Selected uniform positions:**

$$p_1 = 6, \quad p_2 = 7, \quad p_3 = 8$$

**Part (b) — Hash Function Construction and Address Computation (7 marks):**

The Digital Analysis hash function is:

$$h(B_i) = k_6 \, k_7 \, k_8$$

Computing for each book:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(53201847) & = 8 \, 4 \, 7 = 847 \\
h(53201923) & = 9 \, 2 \, 3 = 923 \\
h(53201712) & = 7 \, 1 \, 2 = 712 \\
h(63182045) & = 0 \, 4 \, 5 = 045 = 45 \\
h(63182109) & = 1 \, 0 \, 9 = 109 \\
h(53192018) & = 0 \, 1 \, 8 = 018 = 18 \\
h(63182067) & = 0 \, 6 \, 7 = 067 = 67 \\
h(53202155) & = 1 \, 5 \, 5 = 155
\end{aligned}
$$

**Loading factor calculation:**

$$\alpha = n / m = 8 / 1000 = 0.008$$

This indicates an extremely sparse table with no collisions observed.

---

> **Q3. (B)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`
> Consider a hash table of size $m = 100$ to store employee records. The 6-digit employee IDs are: 421053, 387416, 421098, 154273, 387502, 421176. Using the **Digital Analysis method**:
> **(a)** Compute the required number of hash address digits and tabulate the digit position frequencies. Identify which 2 positions are the most uniform. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Define the hash function and compute the hash address for each employee ID. Verify whether any two employees collide. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Position Tabulation and Selection (7 marks):**

Required hash address length:

$$r = \lceil \log_{10} 100 \rceil = 2 \text{ digits}$$

Digit positions for the 6 employee IDs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Position 1:} & \quad 4, 3, 4, 1, 3, 4 \\
\text{Position 2:} & \quad 2, 8, 2, 5, 8, 2 \\
\text{Position 3:} & \quad 1, 7, 1, 4, 7, 1 \\
\text{Position 4:} & \quad 0, 4, 0, 2, 5, 1 \\
\text{Position 5:} & \quad 5, 1, 9, 7, 0, 7 \\
\text{Position 6:} & \quad 3, 6, 8, 3, 2, 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analysis:**

- **Position 1:** Digits {1, 3, 4} — only 3 distinct values out of 6, with 4 appearing 3 times → **SKEWED**
- **Position 2:** Digits {2, 5, 8} — only 3 distinct values, with 2 and 8 each appearing twice but 5 once → moderately skewed
- **Position 3:** Digits {1, 4, 7} — 3 distinct values, 1 appears 3 times → **SKEWED**
- **Position 4:** Digits {0, 1, 2, 4, 5} — **5 distinct values** → **UNIFORM**
- **Position 5:** Digits {0, 1, 5, 7, 9} — **5 distinct values** → **UNIFORM**
- **Position 6:** Digits {2, 3, 6, 8} — 4 distinct values → **MODERATELY UNIFORM**

**Two most uniform positions selected:**

$$p_1 = 4, \quad p_2 = 5$$

**Part (b) — Hash Function and Address Computation (7 marks):**

The hash function is:

$$h(E_i) = k_4 \, k_5$$

Computing for each employee:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(421053) & = 0 \, 5 = 05 = 5 \\
h(387416) & = 4 \, 1 = 41 \\
h(421098) & = 0 \, 9 = 09 = 9 \\
h(154273) & = 2 \, 7 = 27 \\
h(387502) & = 5 \, 0 = 50 \\
h(421176) & = 1 \, 7 = 17
\end{aligned}
$$

**Collision check:** The hash addresses are $\{5, 41, 9, 27, 50, 17\}$ — all distinct. **No collisions** occur for this dataset. The loading factor is $\alpha = 6/100 = 0.06$, leaving 94 empty slots.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $r$ calculation:** Many students directly start selecting positions without first computing $r = \lceil \log_{10} m \rceil$. This is a **2-mark conceptual error** because it shows lack of understanding of the address-space requirement.
> 2. **Wrong position count:** Selecting 2 uniform positions when $m = 1000$ requires 3 — the examiner will deduct for incorrect hash table addressing.
> 3. **Ignoring zero-padded addresses:** Hash values like $045$ and $018$ must be written correctly (not just "45" and "18" without the leading zero context). The address must occupy exactly $r$ digits.
> 4. **No justification of uniformity:** Examiner expects students to **state WHY** a position is uniform or skewed (e.g., "digit 0 appears only once while digit 2 appears 4 times → skewed"). Vague claims lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing Digit Analysis with Folding:** Digit Analysis **discards digits**; Folding **sums digit groups**. Examiners frequently give confusingly worded questions — read carefully.
> 6. **No chi-square or statistical justification:** For full marks on Part (a), provide a quantitative uniformity measure (range, variance, or chi-square), not just an intuitive guess.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Digital Analysis Hashing**

- ✅ Digital Analysis is a **static, distribution-dependent** hash function — the **entire key set must be known in advance**.
- ✅ It **discards skewed digit positions** and **retains uniformly distributed digit positions** to form the hash address.
- ✅ The required hash address length is $r = \lceil \log_{10} m \rceil$ where $m$ is the hash table size.
- ✅ The general formula is $h(K_i) = k_{p_1} k_{p_2} \dots k_{p_r}$ where $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_r$ are the selected uniform positions.
- ✅ Uniformity is best measured using the **Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) goodness-of-fit test** with degrees of freedom 9 and rejection threshold 16.919 at 5% significance.
- ✅ The method is **ideal for fixed-length numeric keys** (employee IDs, account numbers, ISBNs) and **useless for dynamic or unknown key sets**.
- ✅ Always **state the loading factor** $\alpha = n/m$ in your answer — it is a free 1-mark addition.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Optimal uniformity when the key set is known.
- ✅ **Key disadvantage:** Re-computation of the hash function is required every time a new key is added to the universe.
- ✅ **Comparison trap:** Do NOT confuse with Folding Method (which sums digit groups) or Mid-Square Method (which squares the key).
- ✅ **Examiner's hot question:** "Why is Digital Analysis not used in dynamic hash tables?" — Answer: because uniformity analysis requires all keys in advance.
